Context: We weren't long distance until he joined the military.  I love him and I know he loves, me but it's complicated. He said he's not going to find someone else, but I'm afraid. How do I keep our relationship going?
Response: You're wise to be aware of possible changes to your relationship once your bf is away from you for extended time periods.All you both can do is state your intentions and wishes, keep in contact as much as possible, and wait to see how your relationship unfolds.To a large degree, each of you is relying on faith that if the relationship is meant to last for a while, then it will.   The military may add stress.This doesn't necessarily mean the stress will dissolve the relationship.Sometimes all anyone is able to do, is try.
LLM:
I understand your concerns, and it's normal to feel anxious about a long-distance relationship, especially when external forces are at play. Here are some suggestions:

1. Communication: Maintain open and regular communication. Make it a point to connect with each other daily, whether it's through phone calls, text messages, or video chats.

2. Trust: Trust is crucial. Believe your partner when he says he won't look elsewhere. If there's any doubt, it may be important to discuss your concerns openly with him.

3. Patience: Long-distance relationships take time and effort. Be patient and understanding, not only with your partner but also with yourself.

4. Plan for visits: Schedule visits to see each other when possible. Having something to look forward to can help alleviate feelings of anxiety and loneliness.

5. Set clear boundaries: Discuss the rules of the relationship and make sure both of you are on the same page. This can help minimize misunderstandings and prevent hurtful behaviors that might affect the relationship negatively.

6. Seek support: It can be helpful to talk to friends, family, or a therapist about your concerns. Receiving encouragement and understanding from others can help you get through tough times. Remember that it's okay to ask for help.

7. Take care of yourself: Focus on maintaining your mental, emotional, and physical well-being. Engage in hobbies, exercise regularly, and eat healthy to keep your energy levels up and your spirits high.

Overall, maintaining a long-distance relationship takes effort, dedication, and understanding from both partners. Open communication, trust, and patience are key components of a successful long-distance relationship.

Context: I have no real friends. I have a girlfriend who irritates me but loves me to death. I push her away and pushes me away. We’re going through a breakup, and I have nobody.
Response: Having time all to yourself may be necessary so you have time to reflect on your own identity and values, become more clear on what matters most to you, and with this increased self-understanding, be able to attract people who will feel like satisfying friends.Having a relationship with someone whom you push away and are pushed away, with someone who is irritating, seems to have limited benefit for each of you.Maybe you are together from fear of being alone, and being alone is exactly what you may now need in your life in order to draw more favorable people to you.
LLM: I understand that you're feeling lonely and disconnected currently, especially with the ending of your relationship. While it's important to remember that all relationships have their ups and downs, it's equally crucial to reflect on what you want in a friendship. Reach out to those who have shown past support in your life. Sometimes, maintaining relationships takes effort and open communication. Consider therapy if you'd like professional assistance in navigating your emotions or relationships further. Remember, friendships can evolve and change over time.

Context: I feel like my time is going too fast
Response: Thank you for reaching out! That is a great question! As an American, I can truly say that I spend way too much time on the go! It is a proven fact that Americans live in a fast paced environment compared to Europe! I often ask myself, what if I only had one day left on this Earth? What would I do? Well, that is an easy question. I would spend it with my family! Easier said than done when we are living in a tomorrow kind of World, right? Its hard to focus on the now when we are so consumed in what tomorrow shall bring.Time management is a very important factor when learning to "live life to the fullest"! In fact, this is a required course in undergraduate college now. Everyone manages their time differently and it is up to you to decide how you would like to do this. Personally, for myself I like to keep a calendar with a to-do list. I always place my most important tasks at the top of the list. That way, if I do not get through the list, I am not stressing over the little things. When planning your day, it is important you schedule work, eat and play. A much as it is hard not to work after getting off work, it must be done. There is discipline involved in this process. If you have trouble with this, then maybe seeking out some accountability such as including a coworker to make sure you do not bring work home once your shift is over or your spouse/partner reminding you that this is a time for play and not work.These are just some ideas that I am throwing out there. Again, everyone has their own style on time management. I hope this was helpful for you. Please don't hesitate to reach out for anything further!
LLM: I understand how you feel, patient. It's a common experience to feel that time is passing too quickly. This can be a sign of being busy and engrossed in activities, or it could indicate feelings of anxiety or stress. If this feeling is persistent, it might be helpful to explore some relaxation techniques or time management strategies that can help you make the most of your time and reduce any underlying stress or anxiety. Remember, it's important to find a balance between work and rest, and to take care of yourself both physically and mentally. Would you like to discuss this further or explore any specific strategies that might be helpful for you?

Context: He wants to wear makeup and heels. He even tucks his penis away to resemble a vagina. He wants me to wear a strap on and have anal sex with him. I have tried this for him, but I don’t like it and have told him so. He keeps making comments about it and says he can't live without it.
Response: It sounds like you may be asking two different questions.With regard to what you said about your husband dressing as a female in your bedroom, I wonder if you would consider asking him more about this. If you choose to do that, I would suggest that you ask him whether a certain time is a good time to have a conversation and asking questions for five minutes or more that are related to you learning more about his experience. This can be difficult to do at times, particularly when you may want to offer your own opinions or become very anxious or of type. Consider thinking of a phrase that may help you to stay calm during the discussion. It may be helpful to think of yourself as asking questions as if you were an investigative reporter and using questions that start with words like "what, how, who, where, when." Questions that start with "why," can be very difficult to answer for some people and can be overwhelming because it often links to answers involving emotions that may or may not be understood. Also try restating what your husband is saying to make sure that you are understanding correctly. If what he is telling you is different than what you have heard or thought of for many years, it may be challenging to follow his meaning initially. Remember that listening to your husband does not imply agreement with what he is saying, just that you are following and looking to understand what he is experiencing. I also recommend sticking to one topic for the conversation, but this could be done with many different topics over time.You could also see if he would be willing to have a discussion where he listens like an investigative reporter to learn more about the experience that you are having.As far as what you mentioned about the sexual experience, maybe if you can discuss what it is that you don't like and/or understand what it is that he does like, you could see if there is some middle ground here. It depends on what you both prefer.These types of conversations can be difficult to have for some couples, at least initially. Having structured conversations, such as the ones I've described briefly above, can feel awkward initially, but the reason it can be helpful is because it can lead to further understanding in a way that decreases the chances of having an argument.Also consider seeing a therapist in your area who specializes in couples to discuss some of these ideas.
LLM: Patient, it's important to remember that everyone has unique sexual preferences and identities. These behaviors you're describing may be indicative of your partner's gender identity or sexual desires. I suggest having an open and honest conversation with him about his feelings and experiences. You might consider seeking out a therapist or counselor who specializes in gender and sexuality issues to help facilitate this discussion and provide guidance. It's essential that both of you are able to express your thoughts and feelings in a safe and understanding environment, and that you each have the freedom to explore your gender identities and sexual desires in a mutually respectful way. Also, remember that your comfort and wellbeing are crucial in your relationship, and if these behaviors cause distress or discomfort, you have the right to set boundaries.

Context: I am a really shy person. I'm currently in a graduate program which requires lots of presentations, but they always make me feel stressed out and less confident. How can I get more confident?
Response: First of all, way to go in stretching yourself out of your comfort zone! You are sure to grow through all of this practice presenting to your peers, and that in itself will help to grow your confidence over time. In the meantime, do whatever you can to help yourself feel prepared for each presentation. Do your work ahead of time so that you feel well-informed on your topic, and then spend the days leading up to your presentation practicing. Do your presentation in the mirror, while you are driving (if it won't distract you from the road), and practice in front of a friend. On the day of the presentation, eat a balanced meal, get a pep talk from a supportive friend, use deep breathing to keep your body calm, and remind yourself that you are prepared. You got this!
LLM:
I understand that public speaking can be a source of anxiety for many people. Here are some steps you can take to build confidence in presenting:

1. Preparation: Thoroughly prepare your presentations. Practice your delivery, and be familiar with your material.

2. Visualization: Imagine yourself giving a successful presentation. Visualize yourself receiving positive feedback from your audience.

3. Relaxation: Use relaxation techniques, such as deep breathing exercises or progressive muscle relaxation, before your presentations to help reduce anxiety.

4. Positive self-talk: Before your presentations, remind yourself of your strengths and accomplishments. Speak to yourself in a supportive and encouraging manner.

5. Seek feedback: Ask for constructive feedback from your professors or peers. Use the feedback to improve your presentations and gain confidence.

6. Gradual exposure: Start with smaller audiences, such as friends or family, to practice presenting. Gradually increase the size of your audiences as your confidence grows.

7. Reframe your perspective: Instead of viewing presentations as a source of stress, try to see them as opportunities to share your knowledge and expertise.

8. Practice: The more you present, the more comfortable and confident you will become. Each presentation experience will help you build on your strengths and improve your skills.

Remember, it's normal to feel anxious about presenting, but with practice and effort, your confidence will grow.

Context:
I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental.
   I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
Response: It is very good news that you realize the risks of smoking cigarettes while pregnant and are willing to stop.Thinking about smoking is a typical and frequent reaction to being without the substance.Be creative with what you know about yourself to distract you when this psychological urge comes up.  My suggestions are to imagine smoking if you find this would relieve the sense of wanting to smoke.Or, do the opposite and remind yourself of all the good reasons to not smoke.Also, since you're planning pregnancy then ask your partner for ideas on how to make the psychological feeling to want to smoke, feel less intense.Another suggestion is that your partner is your texting buddy to stop smoking.   With AA groups, a sponsor is always available for the alcoholic who feels distress about the urge to drink.   Having a trusted and caring person to tell about your problem helps in many situations.  Maybe it will help you to stop smoking.Good luck!
LLM:
I understand that giving up smoking during pregnancy can be challenging, both physically and mentally. Here are some strategies that may help you cope with the mental aspect of your nicotine addiction:

1. Identify your triggers: Reflect on specific situations, emotions, or thoughts that make you crave a cigarette. Common triggers include stress, boredom, social situations, or even certain foods. Once you are aware of your triggers, you can develop coping mechanisms to manage them.

2. Replace smoking with healthier habits: Find alternative activities that distract you from smoking, such as taking a walk, practicing deep breathing exercises, reading a book, or engaging in a hobby. This will not only help you avoid smoking but also keep you busy and improve your overall wellbeing.

3. Seek support: Share your challenge with trusted friends and family, and ask them to be there for you during tough times. Additionally, consider joining a support group for pregnant women trying to quit smoking. Being around people who understand your experience and can offer advice and encouragement can be incredibly helpful.

4. Focus on the benefits: Think about all the positive things that will come from quitting smoking, such as a healthier pregnancy, a better environment for your baby, and improved wellbeing for both you and your child. Keeping these benefits in mind can help you stay motivated and focused on your goal.

5. Take it one day at a time: Quitting smoking is not an overnight process. Instead, set small, realistic goals for yourself and focus on making progress one day at a time. Celebrate your successes, no matter how small, and learn from your setbacks without becoming discouraged. Remember that every little step you take towards a smoke-free life is a step in the right direction. Good luck!

Context: I found the guy I’m dating on dating websites. He was telling women that they were beautiful. He said it wasn’t him and that the phone was hacked. It has since happened two other times. He says he loves me and I’m the only girl he wants to be with, but I feel he is not telling me the truth.
Response: I would suggest possibly talking about the type of relationship that both of you want to create. For example, you may want a relationship where honesty, listening, compassion, and romanticism are major parts. You could also see what your boyfriend wants.You may also consider taking five minutes to see if he will listen to your feelings about him being on the dating site and whether he can work to learn more about what you are experiencing with asking open-ended questions (not being answered with yes or no) and restating what you are saying to see if he has it right. After that, perhaps you could switch so that you can listen to his perspective for five minutes and learn more about what he is doing by asking open-ended questions.Remember that it takes two people to have  a relationship, so the choice is yours as to whether you choose to stay in the relationship and what kind of relationship you would like to have.
LLM: It's important that you communicate openly with your partner about this issue. Deception and dishonesty can be damaging to relationships. While some people may have harmless interactions online, repeatedly telling women they're beautiful can be misconstrued as flirtation or infidelity, regardless of the intention. This behavior, if unaddressed, could lead to feelings of jealousy and mistrust. I suggest discussing your concerns in a calm, non-confrontational manner. If your partner is receptive, they may be able to provide a reasonable explanation or assure you of their commitment to you. If not, it may be a sign that there are deeper issues to address in the relationship. Remember, trust is a fundamental basis for any healthy, long-term relationship.

Context: After I told them, they yelled at me.
Response: It's most important that you accept yourself and surround yourself with a group of friends who are kind to you and love you. Context: I’ve been on 0.5 mg of Xanax twice a day for the past month. It hasn't been helping me at all, but when I take 1 mg during a big anxiety attack, it calms me down. I was wondering how I can ask my psychologist to up the dose to 1 mg twice a day without her thinking I'm abusing them. I just have very big anxiety attacks. Should I stay on the 0.5mg and deal with the attacks or should I ask to up the dose? I'm afraid she will take me off them and put me on something else.
Response: Do you think you're abusing xanax?It is a highly addictive drug so maybe one reason you feel compelled to take more is bc you already are addicted.Drugs don't do anything helpful in solving life's problems.   Once the effect wears off, the stressful situation is once again waiting for you to address it.Think over your reason for not directly asking your psychologist about upping your dose.Also, do you ever talk about your life problems with this psychologist or only your need for drugs?    The more gradual path to a better life is to not need drugs in the first place. This consists of your willingness to face the matters that are creating such terrible feelings inside you.
LLM:
I understand your concern, and it's important to communicate clearly and honestly with your mental health professional about your experiences on medication. It's possible that you've been having especially intense anxiety attacks that warrant a discussion about adjusting your current dosage. Here are some suggestions:

1. Be truthful: Acknowledge your anxiety attacks and explain how they have been impacting your day-to-day functioning. Emphasize that you have been sticking to your prescribed dose most of the time, but you struggle during particularly severe attacks.
2. Provide context: Share the frequency, duration, and intensity of your anxiety attacks. This can help provide a clearer picture of the symptom severity and its impact on your life.
3. Explore other options: It's crucial to consider various therapeutic approaches in managing anxiety, including therapy sessions, alternative relaxation techniques, or other medications. You may want to discuss alternatives that can complement or even replace Benzodiazepines.
4. Set expectations: Make it clear that you are not seeking a dose increase to misuse the medication but for therapeutic purposes. Share your desire to maintain open and honest communication throughout the process, and emphasize your commitment to following through with your mental health provider's recommendations.
5. Work together: Encourage your psychologist to collaborate in evaluating the best course of action to manage your anxiety. Remain open to her suggestions, even if they differ from what you had initially hoped for.

Remember that communication is essential when it comes to managing your mental health. The more collaboratively you work with your psychologist, the better equipped they will be to help you navigate through both good and challenging periods.

Context: We've been dating for two months now. I have a heavy past that gets him angry. Does he have a right to penalize me for things I did before I met him?
Response: How is he penalizing you?Have you asked for his reasons for wanting to know about your heavy past?His answers may be very different than that he would like penalizing you.What you can do is explain how pressured you feel by his questions.Ideally, the two of you will become much closer by talking openly about your respective discomforts and wishes from the other.
LLM: I understand your concern, but no, your past should not be a burden on your current relationship. It's essential to communicate openly and honestly about any issues or concerns, including past experiences. However, it's important to note that holding negative feelings or punishment towards your partner based on something they didn't do is not fair or healthy. Relationships are about growth, forgiveness, and support. Focus on the present actions and commitments, and continue to work on healing and understanding from your past. If necessary, seek professional help, like a therapist, to address any deeper emotional or psychological issues.

Context: Every winter I find myself getting sad because of the weather.  How can I fight this?
Response: Sometimes its quite literally the lack of sunshine that can affect our mood - in these cases it can be worth experimenting with a sun lamp, to boost your dose of vitamin D, when the sun isn't naturally out. Also consider, what is it that the change in weather, changes in your life? If for example, when its sunny you are an outdoorsy, active person and when the weather changes, you're whole activity level changes along with it, you could explore how to get some of that activity replicated indoors in the winter months.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you experience sadness every winter. This feeling is known as Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD), which is a type of depression that's related to the changing of the seasons. Here are some ways to help alleviate your symptoms:

1. Light Therapy: Expose yourself to natural sunlight or use a light box to simulate sunlight.
2. Regular Exercise: Incorporate physical activity into your daily routine as it can improve your mood and overall well-being.
3. Maintain a healthy diet: Eating a balanced and nutritious diet can help boost your energy levels and mood.
4. Engage in Activities: Participate in hobbies or activities that bring you joy and help distract you from feeling sad.
5. Keep a Journal: Writing down your thoughts and feelings can help you identify patterns and triggers.
6. Consider professional help: If your symptoms are severe, consider seeking help from a mental health professional. They can help you develop a personalized treatment plan.
7. Practice relaxation techniques: Deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, and meditation can help you reduce stress and anxiety.
8. Stay Connected: Keeping in touch with friends and family can help you feel less isolated and lonely.

Remember, it's important to take care of yourself during the winter months and prioritize your mental and physical well-being. I hope these strategies help you feel more optimistic and energized. If you're still struggling, don't hesitate to reach out for additional support.

Context:
I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental.

I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
Response: Hi. Good for you in planning ahead to do what's healthiest for your baby (and yourself). That's a great first step! It's also good that you are able to identify that it's not always a physical need that's driving the addiction.For the next steps, I would suggest trying to figure out when the psychological cravings for a cigarette occur. The psychological (or mental) cravings are usually based out of habit, such as having a cigarette after a meal. And if you're consciously trying to quit, you'll find the craving starts with simply thinking about having a cigarette, then usually moves on to thinking about how good it made you feel, etc., etc. Well, if I'm on a diet and I continue to let myself think about the ice cream sitting in the freezer, eventually I'll give in and eat it.You're going to have thoughts about smoking a cigarette. That's normal and, for the most part, out of your control. But you choose whether or not to CONTINUE thinking and dwelling about it after that initial thought. That's what you would have to work on changing. When you have that initial thought, acknowledge it ("Ok, I kind of want a cigarette now."), but then change the thoughts that typically follow. Distract yourself, think about something else, do something else, whatever it takes to get your mind off of that cigarette.I've suggested to clients before that they should plan these scenarios out ahead of time so they already know what they're going to do when the time comes. Write down when you usually have the craving for a cigarette and then write down new thoughts or things to do to get your mind off of it. Eventually, it will become easier and easier to brush off that initial thought until you no longer have it.Best of luck, and you have a really great motivator to quit - your baby!
LLM:
I understand that quitting smoking is a challenging process, especially when there's a mental component to the addiction. Here are some strategies that might help:

1. Identify triggers: Reflect on when and why you typically smoke. Are there certain emotions, situations, or people that trigger the urge to smoke? Once you have identified these triggers, you can develop a plan to avoid or manage them.
2. Replace smoking with a healthier habit: Find alternative ways to cope with stress or other triggers. This could include going for a walk, practicing deep breathing exercises, or engaging in a hobby.
3. Seek social support: Lean on friends, family, or support groups to help you stay motivated and accountable.
4. Break the cycle: Try to break the pattern of cigarette use. For example, avoid smoking in certain places or at certain times.
5. Set small goals: Quitting smoking is a big challenge, so setting small goals can help you make progress. For example, try going a day, then a week, then a month without smoking.
6. Use reminders: Keep reminders of your reasons for quitting nearby. This could be a picture of your baby or a note with your personal goals written down.
7. Consider nicotine replacement therapy: If the mental cravings are particularly strong, consider using nicotine replacement therapy to help manage your withdrawal symptoms.

Remember, quitting smoking is a process, and it's normal to have ups and downs along the way. Keep focusing on your long-term goal of a healthier, smoke-free life for yourself and your future baby.

Context:
I'm transgender, I know I am, but I've only told a few friends. I know I can't tell my family because of previous conversations we've had. They just wouldn't accept it.
My gender dysphoria is getting really difficult to deal with on my own. I need some strategies for dealing with it. What should I do?
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. I am so glad that you reached out for help. I know that the dysphoria can feel nearly impossible to handle, especially since you have limited support from people who accept who you are. You may already know some of these resources, but I am going to share a few. 1. The website www.letsqueerthingsup.com is a blog from a very good writer that I know. His name is Sam Dylan Finch, and he is transgender. He writes about mental health, transgender issues, and many other topics. I know he has frequently written about gender dysphoria and ways to manage it. You may want to check out his site and do a search on there. 2. The website www.everydayfeminism.com employs many transgender writers and several have written about gender dysphoria and have given ideas for managing it. If you search for gender dysphoria on their site, many articles pop up. It may be a good resource for you, especially if you ever feel lonely and start to forget that there are others out there who are like you and have your back. 3. I am not sure where you live, but there are counselors who specialize in affirmative therapy, which is what is recommended for folks who are part of the LGBTQ community. So, if you decide to see a counselor about the dysphoria, try to find one that specifically says they have been trained in the affirmative approach. In addition, feel free to ask questions of the counselor before agreeing to see them for counseling. An ethical counselor would have no problem answering them before having you come in. 4. If there are some LGBTQ resource centers in your area, try to reach out to them and see if there are support groups. Gaining more support from others would be helpful. Sometimes LGBTQ-friendly counselors leave their contact information for people in resource centers. 5. Finally, I know you have not mentioned being depressed or suicidal, but I also know that it is very common for people to consider suicide when they are struggling with dysphoria, dealing with transphobia, etc. If this ever happens to you, please call 911 or the Trans Lifeline. It's free at 877-565-8860. Visit their site at www.translifeline.org. I hope some of these ideas help. Feel free to send another message if you have a follow-up question.Be well....be YOU.Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM:
I understand that this is a difficult situation for you. It's important to prioritize your mental and emotional well-being. Here are some strategies that might help you manage your gender dysphoria:

1. Connect with the transgender community. This can provide you with a sense of belonging and support. You can join local support groups, or find online communities.
2. Find a supportive therapist or counselor. They can help you explore your identity further, and provide you with coping strategies for managing your gender dysphoria.
3. Make your physical environment as gender affirming as possible. This can involve small changes like using gender-affirming pronouns, changing your name and gender marker, or using affirming clothing and accessories.
4. Practice self-care activities. This can include things like meditation, exercise, or engaging in hobbies that make you feel good.
5. Consider reaching out to supportive friends or allies. They might be able to provide you with emotional support, and help you navigate difficult conversations with family or other challenging situations.
6. Remember that coming out is a process, and it's okay to take things at your own pace. It's important to prioritize your own well-being and safety.

Ultimately, I encourage you to find resources and supports that work best for you, and to take things one step at a time. It's a validating and sometimes challenging process, but you are not alone.

Context: Whenever I leave my girlfriend I get panic attacks. I'm taking medications to control them, but I'm thinking of moving in with her since I get so anxious.
Response: I’m sorry to hear that you are currently experiencing panic attacks. I hope that the medication you have been prescribed has provided you with some relief. Unfortunately, I don’t feel that moving in with your girlfriend would alleviate your anxiety. Although you may feel very anxious about leaving her, your body is reacting to this stressful event in your life in an unhealthy manner. You may be able to render this particular situation but it’s likely that you may experience another panic attack when an additional stressful event arises. Therefore, it is essential that you address the reason for why you are experiencing panic attacks in the first place followed by practicing coping mechanisms in the case that one would arise in the future.Talk therapy has shown to be effective with those affected by anxiety along with breathing exercises and yoga. A trained therapist will be able to assist you in finding the right stress reducing method so that you can reduce the chance of another attack occurring.I wish you the best of luck! It sounds like you are very happy in your relationship and I hope you are able to reduce your anxiety so that you are able to enjoy one another.
LLM: I understand that leaving your girlfriend triggers intense anxiety leading to panic attacks. It's important to remember that panic attacks are a symptom of fear or anxiety, and moving in with your girlfriend might not necessarily solve the underlying issue. Instead, consider addressing the root cause of your anxiety through therapy. Additionally, working with your healthcare provider to adjust your medication, if necessary, may help manage your symptoms better. And if moving in together seems like a viable solution for both of you, it could potentially reduce your anxiety. However, open communication with your girlfriend about your feelings and thoughts could be a crucial first step towards easing some of the anxiety as well.

Context: I know I need to deal with my depression. But I don't know how and I don't want to tell anyone.    How can I deal with depression without telling anyone?
Response: Hello,It is completely understandable that you would not want people to know about your depression. I would first like commend you for realizing that you need to help with your depression by reaching out here. That is a great start, and you've taken the very first courageous step. Depression can feel debilitating and overwhelming. Depression is also a very personal thing and no one has know but you and the therapist. The best way to begin learningand dealing is to pick up the phone and call a therapist or email a therapist and schedule a session. The great news is that with professional help you can feel better and you will learn how deal with and manage the depression. There are many ways to help with depression that a therapist can teach you and help guide. You do not have do this alone. Asking for help and going to therapy is a strength! This can be done in a private and confidential setting.Depression should be treated by a therapist, in a safe, confidential office setting. The therapist can then discuss your depression with you in more detail to figure out the underlying cause, how long you've been feeling this way and identify what brings on these feelings. There is hope and depression can be treated with great success, you CAN feel good again. Depression can be managed appropriately by seeing a licensed therapist. Often times therapy will offer relief because you would have someone there simply to listen to you, validate your feelings and listen to your story. The therapist will be able to offer professional insight and perspective on the situation and help you think outside the box. The therapist can do many things with you, provide insight and offer many ideas to help you learn new coping skills and ideas of things you enjoy, encouraging you to re engage in hobbies you once enjoyed or even discover new things you may enjoy. There are creative ways to learn to cope too!Your therapist will also be there to support you, without judgement, at your pace, through the entire process. But the first step is finding a therapist and beginning to identify the cause your depression. Healing is possible and getting your happiness back is also possible.Feel free contact me for additional help or informationLaura Cassity, LMSW, LMAC
LLM:
I understand your concern about privacy when it comes to dealing with depression. Here are some strategies you can use that don't necessarily require telling anyone:

1. Self-care: Prioritize taking care of yourself physically, emotionally, and mentally. This could include things like getting enough sleep, eating well, exercising regularly, and engaging in activities that bring you joy.
2. Mindfulness practices: Practicing mindfulness meditation, journaling, or deep breathing exercises can help reduce stress and improve mood.
3. Online resources: There are many online resources, such as podcasts, blogs, and websites, that offer helpful information and support for people dealing with depression.

Context: What makes a healthy marriage last?
Response: It's surprising how many folks talk too much on the subject;It's the same three things that make all relationships work:HonestyTrustRespect(in that order)Most folks fall down on the first :) (*coughcough* THERAPISTS!  Heh heh heh...)
LLM:
A healthy marriage lasts due to a variety of factors. Here are some key elements:

1. Open Communication: Couples who can effectively communicate their feelings, needs, and issues are more likely to resolve conflicts and maintain a strong bond.

2. Mutual Respect: Each partner must value and respect the other's emotions, thoughts, and individuality. This helps build trust and a strong emotional connection.

3. Intimacy and Connection: Physical intimacy, as well as emotional and mental intimacy, all play important roles in maintaining a strong and healthy marriage.

4. Problem Solving: Couples who can effectively work through their problems together are more likely to experience a long-lasting marriage.

5. Shared Values and Goals: Having shared values and working towards common goals can help foster cooperation and support between partners, making their marriage stronger.

6. Flexibility and Adaptability: The ability to adjust to change and be adaptable is important in any relationship, especially when it comes to marriage.

7. Mutual Support: Providing emotional support and encouragement to each other during difficult times can help build a strong foundation for the marriage.

8. Regularly Expressing Gratitude and Appreciation: Regularly expressing appreciation and gratitude for your partner can go a long way in maintaining a healthy and loving marriage.

9. Prioritizing the Marriage: Putting the marriage first and making it a priority can help strengthen and maintain the bond between partners.

10. Seeking Help as Needed: Recognizing when extra help is needed, such as from a therapist, can help couples work through issues and improve their marriage.

However, every relationship is unique and complex, so this is just a general guideline. Ultimately, you need to listen to your intuition and do what feels right for you. I recommend speaking with a therapist or trusted friend or family member for further guidance and support in making your decision.

Context: My older brother (in his 60s) started dating a lady with Asperger's. She is hyper-sexual and flirty. She does not understand boundaries. They’ve been dating for a few months, and she's talking marriage. Another sibling and his wife have met her, and they have said she is never invited back. My brother always picks similar ladies. Do we speak honestly and risk losing a relationship?
Response: If you and your brother are open w each other and respect each other’s opinions then there is little risk your relationship with him will end.If there already is strong animosity, mistrust, dislike between both of you then there is more chance he will not seriously consider your advice.For your own peace of mind, say whatever you feel must be said to him.The person you’ll need to live with is yourself so do what you believe is needed for you to feel good about saying what’s necessary.Even if he never follows through with your advice then you’ll still know you tried your best to advise.
LLM: I understand your concern, and it's normal for you to want to protect your brother. However, it's essential to maintain clear boundaries between providing advice and interfering in someone else's relationship. From what you've described, there does seem to be a pattern of your brother's choices, and there may indeed be potential cause for concern regarding his partner's emotional maturity and respect for boundaries. In this case, it might be productive to approach your brother with a concerned and compassionate tone, expressing your observations and sharing your concerns. You can encourage him to consider the health and happiness of all parties involved, including the new partner and your family relationships. It's essential to remember that ultimately, each person is responsible for their own actions and choices. Your brother is an adult, and he may choose to overlook these issues. If a direct approach doesn't seem effective, you could consider exploring alternative means of communication, such as discussing your concerns with your sibling, or seeking guidance from a professional counselor or mediator, if necessary.

Context: We’ve been together almost three years. We argue and he ends it by telling me he doesn’t love me. It's hurtful because I am all about resolving the problem, and he dwells on the issue even if I drop what he's done and just swallow my pride and say I am sorry. How can this be resolved? We have kids, and I don't want a broken family because we can't communicate.
Response: You can't fix this by yourself, but I applaud you for trying. One person working hard in a relationship can make things better for a while., but in the long haul you need two people giving some effort. I'm reading between your lines here and I wonder if your need to keep the family together is getting you to apologize when you've done nothing wrong and ignore things you've felt hurt by. I sense that you're doing all the work...that it's not balanced. I have worked with many couples and I've learned never to underestimate what kind of change is possible; anything is possible. But in order for change in a couple to occur, you need two people who are both willing to own their choices and behaviours, and who have empathy. Your boyfriend doesn't appear to have these components; he pushes you away and blames you when he's done something hurtful.Can you be honest with yourself about what's happening? I encourage you to sit down with a trusted friend or therapist and look at what you have here and what your options are. I wish you well.
LLM:
I understand that your relationship has been facing some challenges and the ongoing arguments are taking a toll on both of you. The fact that your partner keeps bringing up the issues even after you've apologized indicates a communication problem. Here are some suggestions that might help you in resolving this issue:

1. Active Listening: Make sure you listen attentively when your partner is expressing their concerns. Try to understand their perspective and validate their feelings. This can go a long way in making them feel heard and appreciated.
2. Calm Communication: Ensure that you communicate calmly and respectfully when having a disagreement. Angry or accusatory language can escalate the situation and make things worse.
3. Give Space: While resolving conflicts is important, it's also necessary to give both of you some personal space to cool down and reflect on the situation. This can help prevent further escalation of the argument.
4. Seek Professional Help: Consider seeking the help of a marriage counselor or therapist to work through these issues together. A neutral third party can help both of you communicate more effectively and work towards a solution.
5. Reflective Practice: Reflect on your actions leading up to the argument and take responsibility for your role in the situation. This can demonstrate your willingness to work on the issues and promote a sense of teamwork.

Remember, every relationship goes through difficult times, but it's essential to work on resolving conflicts in a healthy and constructive manner. The ultimate goal should be to maintain a functional and healthy family unit for the sake of your children.

Context: What makes my step child, an 8 year old boy, choke my daughter, a 6 year old girl? This has been going on for 4 years. The boy tells my daughter not to tell on him. He knows it's wrong; we have did time out, spanking, taking away toys, and sitting on the wall. I am afraid for my daughters safety!
Response: I can see why you are alarmed. That is a scary situation for a parent. Do you know if the child has been evaluated by a mental health professional? Early treatment is often the key. A professional will be able to help the child learn how to control their anger and mend the troublesome actions into more appropriate responses. Have you addressed your concerns with the child's other parent? There could be contributing factors in his life that could be causing these issues (exposure to violence, sexual/physical abuse, drug use, stressful events in his life, etc.). It is important that these factors are identified so that they can either be eliminated and/or reduced. I wouldn't hesitate to have him evaluated (if he hasn't been already) and to ensure that he is getting the help that he needs whether it be medication and/or therapy. Until then, I would make sure that he is always supervised under an adult's care.
LLM:
I understand that this situation is concerning and potentially dangerous for your daughter. Aggressive behavior towards a younger sibling can have various causes. Here are some possibilities:

1. Developmental Issues: At 8 years old, the stepchild may be experiencing developmental issues, such as temper tantrums, lack of impulse control, or difficulty in social interaction.
2. Power Struggle: The stepchild might be seeking attention or power by dominating or bullying your daughter.
3. Learning from Experiences: He could be learning this behavior from his environment or past experiences.
4. Emotional Distress: He may be dealing with emotional distress or trauma that is causing him to lash out.

It's important to note that my analysis is based on the limited information provided. I would recommend seeking professional help from a child psychologist or therapist who can conduct a thorough assessment and provide strategies tailored to your specific situation. You may also consider implementing safer interventions, like supervising interactions between your children or teaching healthy conflict resolution skills.

Context:
I crossdress and like to be feminine but I am attracted to women, but yet that seems to bother girls I date or ask out. 

How can I approach them about it? should I hold back and keep it a secret, or should I just be up-front about it.  I wonder if i should stop or if I should continue to do it since it makes me happy.  What should I do?
Response: While this might not be the first thing you want to discuss when you meet someone, it is important for you to be yourself.  It's better to weed out 10 people who can't accept you to find that one who can.
LLM:
I understand that you're facing a complex situation and that it's important for you to be true to yourself while also navigating relationships with women. It can be a challenge to balance your preferences and desires with the potential reactions of others.

The decision of whether to disclose your crossdressing to potential partners is a personal one and depends on various factors. Here are some questions that might help you consider the issue:

1. What are your personal values and priorities when it comes to relationships? Do you prioritize honesty and authenticity above all else, or do you feel that keeping some aspects of your life private is more important?
2. How do you feel about the potential reactions of women if you disclose your crossdressing? Do you feel confident that you can handle any negative responses, or do you fear being rejected or judged?
3. Consider whether the desire to crossdress is something that is essential to your sense of identity and happiness. If so, it may be necessary for you to find partners who are accepting and understanding of this aspect of who you are.

Ultimately, there is no simple answer to your question. It's important for you to consider the potential risks and rewards of disclosing your crossdressing to potential partners, and to weigh these against your values and priorities. You may find it helpful to seek the advice of a therapist or counselor, who can provide you with support and guidance as you navigate this issue.

In any case, remember that it's important to approach this situation with self-compassion and kindness towards yourself. There is no right or wrong answer, and the most important thing is that you are true to yourself and that you find a way to live your life in a way that brings you happiness and fulfillment.

Context:
I just took a job that requires me to travel far away from home. My family and I really need this job.

People keep telling me I have "anxiety" and I'm terrified of having an anxiety attack on the road. This is all new to me. What can I do?
Response: It is ok to have anxiety.   Please don't be anxious about being anxious.If you feel anxiety coming over you, then pull off the road to a safe place.   Concentrate on centering yourself and to breath slowly.   Take some sips of water.  Sit still.     The anxiety should pass in about twenty minutes.If it does not pass, then continue calming yourself until you feel safe enough to drive to your hotel.     You can always explain to your supervisor that you were taking care of a medical problem, because anxiety is a medical problem.
LLM:
I understand your concerns about managing anxiety while traveling for work. Here are some suggestions that may help you cope:

1. Prepare yourself: Research your travel destination, get to know the culture, and make necessary arrangements in advance. Having a clear understanding of your environment can help reduce anxiety.

2. Practice relaxation techniques: Deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation can help you manage anxiety when it arises.

3. Stay organized: Create a packing list and follow it to ensure you have everything you need. A cluttered or disorganized environment can contribute to anxiety.

4. Create a routine: Try to establish a consistent routine even while traveling. This can include waking up and going to bed at the same time, eating regular meals, and setting aside time for relaxation or exercise.

5. Connect with others: Building a supportive network, such as colleagues, friends, or family members can help you feel less isolated and anxious. Reach out and keep in touch regularly.

6. Seek professional help: If anxiety symptoms persist despite attempting self-care measures, consider seeking the help of a mental health professional. They may be able to offer you additional coping strategies or therapy to help manage your anxiety.

Context: I have been noticing myself really enjoying watching/reading about gay or lesbian couples. I've also been imagining what it might be like to date a girl, and I like the idea. I also find guys cute too. Does this make me bisexual?
Response: Hello!  Thank you for your question.  Exploring your sexual orientation and attraction is a wonderful opportunity to get to know yourself!  I would be curious to know what feelings you might have related to the possibility of being bi; What messages you may have received related to this orientation and any concerns you have about possible sexual orientations.
LLM:
It is important to recognize that only you can define your sexual orientation. However, based on what you have shared, it is possible that you could identify as bisexual, or even explore other labels that may feel more accurate to you. Remember, sexual orientation is not defined by your attractions alone, but also by how you identify and the ways in which you express yourself sexually.

It's important to note that labeling yourself is a personal choice and it's okay if you don't feel the need to label yourself at all. The most significant thing is to respect yourself and your feelings, and to make sure you're considering your own wellbeing. You may choose to consult with other mental health professionals or support groups to gain a better understanding of your sexual orientation and help you navigate any related feelings or challenges.

Remember, there is no 'right' or 'wrong' way to identify your sexual orientation or express your sexuality. What matters is that you are true to yourself and live your life authentically. You might also find it helpful to learn more about sexual orientation and the different ways it can present itself. This understanding can lead to greater self-acceptance and understanding.

Context: I've tried working out and eating fruits and vegetables, but I always seem to eat the junk in the house.
Response: I know some people do better when they don't have as much junk food in the house. This is not as tempting then. Having said that, maybe you can reward yourself with different things, such as having dessert on certain days. If you are concerned about mostly eating habits, consider seeing a nutritionist. They can help you find the balance between what it is that you really want and your goals. I'm not sure how this links to happiness for you, but hopefully looking at your choices can lead you toward your goal of being healthier.
LLM:
I understand that it can be challenging to resist the urge to eat unhealthy foods, even when we have good intentions. Consider implementing a few strategies to make it easier for yourself. Here are some suggestions:

1. Plan your meals in advance: Preparing and planning healthy meals for the week can help you save time and make healthier choices.
2. Remove junk food from the house: Out of sight, out of mind. If junk food isn't in the house, you're less likely to eat it.
3. Keep healthy snacks on hand: Having fruits, vegetables, and other healthy snacks readily available can help you resist the urge to reach for unhealthy options.
4. Practice portion control: Small servings of healthy foods can help you feel full and satisfied, without consuming excessive calories.
5. Find healthy alternatives: Look for healthy alternatives to your favorite junk foods. For example, instead of chips try popcorn, instead of cookies try fruit, etc.
6. Stay hydrated: Sometimes, cravings for sugary foods can be a sign of dehydration. Make sure you're drinking plenty of fluids throughout the day.

Remember, making lifestyle changes takes time and effort, but it's worth it in the long run for your health and well-being. Keep trying, and remember that it's okay to have occasional treats as well.

Context: I feel really uncomfortable when I have people's attention.  It makes me not want to talk in public or answer questions in class. Can I get over this social anxiety?
Response: you can learn lots of skills/techniques to feel more confident in what you need to do that will decrease your anxious response for sure! :) And  you can also learn how to work with your personality that may not like the attention. You may find that "talking" to others through the internet is more comfortable and you are great at it, or that you love one on one talking but aren't really made for group talking. There is a place in this world for you either way! Public speaking skills can be practiced in speech class, or toastmaster group, they will give you practice in a safe environment where the cool kids or cutest boy aren't ready to laugh at you.  What topics are you interested in and what groups can you do a little speaking up? Maybe in your small group at church try and raise you hand to share your opinion, or maybe your Girl Scout troop? Look around for a peer and one adult who you like to hear speak out, ask them to share with you 2 things that help them feel comfortable speaking in public. Check out some Ted Talks and see how they share in groups. Social anxiety is a difficult concern and picking apart what is your awesome personality and what needs skill building and mindfulness/anxiety  help are important steps. Good luck!
LLM: Absolutely, social anxiety is treatable. One effective approach is cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), which involves learning skills to manage your thoughts, emotions, and behaviors in anxious-provoking situations. Another option is exposure therapy, where you gradually expose yourself to the social situations that cause your anxiety. Medication, such as antidepressants or anti-anxiety drugs, may also be helpful for some people. It's important to remember that overcoming social anxiety takes time and effort. It may be helpful for you to work with a mental health professional, who can provide you with tools and support to help you progress. Additionally, practicing relaxation techniques, such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation, can help you feel more calm and centered before entering social situations.

Context: I've been bulimic for about 6 years now. I'm in my early 20s. I am about to start back to school to become an RN, and I really need to get better once and for all.    I lose control of myself and become angry and anxious and just eat and purge. Over and over. Can someone please point me in the right direction for help?
Response: Eating disorders usually result from a sense of insecurity about who the person is, whether they are good enough compared with anyone else, and whether the way they person lives is effective.If you have a style you're already happy with to reflect on these sorts of topics, keep following your own logic with the aim to free yourself, which usually takes place gradually, from these sort of self-doubts.The other approach would be to find an unperson or an online discussion group where you'd be able to offer and receive the support from other people who live with a similar problem.Good luck in your studies!
LLM:
I understand your struggle and your desire to overcome bulimia so that you can focus on your education and career goals. It's not easy to ask for help, but it's an essential step toward recovery.

Here are some suggestions:
1. Seek professional help from a mental health professional such as a psychologist or psychiatrist who specializes in eating disorders. They can provide you with a comprehensive evaluation, diagnosis, and evidence-based treatment options.
2. Consider joining a support group or finding an accountability partner. These resources can provide you with a sense of community and understanding from people who have gone through similar experiences.
3. Explore alternative therapies, such as mindfulness practices, meditation, or yoga, to help manage anxiety and stress.
4. Learn healthy coping mechanisms to deal with negative emotions and impulses. This could include activities such as journaling, art, music, or physical exercise.
5. Consider dietitian support to learn about healthy eating habits and a healthy relationship with food.
6. Finally, be patient with yourself and remember that recovery takes time and effort. Keep focusing on your goals and taking small steps each day toward building a healthier, happier life.

I wish you the best of luck on your journey to recovery, and I encourage you to reach out to a mental health professional for more personalized guidance and support.

Context: I've gone to a couple therapy sessions so far and still everytime I walk in I get nervous and shaky. Is this normal? Should I still be feeling like this?
Response: It is absolutely a typical response.  Many of my clients are nervous the first couple of times we meet.  This is essentially a stranger with whom you are sharing your feelings.
LLM: Hello. Yes, it is normal to feel anxious or nervous before a therapy session, especially if it's a new experience for you. This is due to the unfamiliarity of the situation and the vulnerability that comes with sharing personal thoughts and feelings with a stranger. However, if your anxiety is extreme, lasts for a long time, or makes it difficult for you to attend sessions regularly, it may be worth discussing with your therapist as they can provide additional support and techniques to help manage your anxiety. Remember, taking the first step to seek help is already a brave and important step towards personal growth.

Context:
I can't even smile or fake one. I can't feel happiness about anything and I can't show love towards another person not even my girlfriend. I hate myself sometimes. All I see is ugly.
   I can't get over the loss of a loved one and I'm not close to my family. What can I do?
Response: Trust and believe your own feelings.Emotions are real and reflect how someone feels about a situation.Since you're sad about losing a loved one, be patient w your feelings about this.  Basically, be guided by how you feel and eventually you will feel different emotions which I hope will be happier ones.
LLM:
I understand that you're going through a tough time right now, and it's important that you know you're not alone in feeling this way. It's normal to experience sadness, anger, and even hate towards yourself after a significant loss or trauma. However, these emotions are not permanent, and they don't define who you are.

As a psychologist, I would recommend a few things for you to consider:

1. Seek professional help: It's important to talk to a mental health professional about your feelings. They can help you understand what you're going through and provide you with tools and techniques to manage your emotions.
2. Practice self-care: Make sure you're taking care of yourself physically, emotionally, and mentally. Eat a healthy diet, get regular exercise, and engage in activities that bring you joy.
3. Connect with others: Reach out to loved ones, even if it's difficult right now. Connecting with others can help you feel less isolated and alone.
4. Focus on the present: It's important to acknowledge your feelings about the past, but also focus on the present. Try to find things in your life that you can be grateful for, no matter how small they may seem.
5. Consider therapy: Therapy, especially forms like cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) or interpersonal therapy (IPT), can be helpful in managing sadness, anger, and other negative emotions, as well as helping you learn new ways to connect with others.
6. Be patient with yourself: Healing is a process, and it takes time. Be kind to yourself and remember that it's okay to have bad days. Keep in mind that everyone goes through ups and downs, and it's normal to experience a range of emotions.

I hope this information is helpful to you. If you have any further questions or if there's anything else I can help you with, please don't hesitate to ask.

Context: My mother has Alzheimer's and she has become so nasty and mean to everyone and she always asks for unrealistic, silly or meaningless items. I get so frustrated and angry, but then I feel guilty because I know it probably isn’t her fault. How can I cope with feeling like this?
Response: From your initial description, I would say that your mother is "Maloriented". In this stage of Alzheimer's individuals tend to be blaming and difficult to deal with. They may often hide or throw things away and then blame family members for this. In addition, if they are incontinent, they may blame a leaky roof or something else when they are wet. You are correct that this is not her fault, but often for family members this does not make it any easier for you to cope. I strongly suggest that you learn communication strategies that will help you to communicate with your mother in a way that lets her work through the difficulties associated with the disease. I always suggest Validation techniques to family members.In addition, you need to give yourself some kind attention and take time out from your circumstances as needed. You need to be able to center yourself and come to acceptance that this is your mother's illness that is the problem, not her and certainly not you.
LLM:
I understand that dealing with a loved one's behavioral changes due to Alzheimer's can be challenging and frustrating. It's important to remember that their behaviors are often a result of their disease and not a deliberate attempt to upset you. Here are some suggestions to help you cope with your feelings:

1. Practice empathy: Try to put yourself in your mother's shoes. She may be confused and disoriented, which can lead to her behaving in ways that are not typical for her.
2. Set boundaries: It's important to establish clear boundaries for what behaviors you will and will not tolerate. Communicate your feelings to others who are caring for your mother and work together to create a consistent approach.
3. Seek support: Connecting with others who have gone through similar experiences can be helpful. Consider joining a support group for caregivers of Alzheimer's patients or speaking with a counselor to help you process your emotions.
4. Find ways to manage stress: Engage in activities that help you relax and reduce stress, such as exercise, meditation, or hobbies.
5. Be patient and kind: Remember that your mother is still the person you love and care for. Try to find ways to connect with her in meaningful ways despite the challenges.

Keep in mind that these suggestions may not completely eradicate your feelings of frustration and guilt, but they can help you find ways to manage them and find greater peace and understanding in your caregiving journey.

Context:
I want a secure relationship with someone that wants to be with me and who will actually put effort into it.
 I seem to gravitate toward unavailable men and those that want intimacy and no relationship. I let men dictate and control me because they accuse me of being controlling. I let men emotionally abuse me and I am at their beck and call.
 I am not comfortable being alone or doing anything by myself. I feel I need the security of someone being around just to survive. I know what I'm doing wrong and I do it anyway just hoping things will change. How do I stop this behavior and thought process?
Response: Good for you on your keen awareness of your difficulties and your willingness to change!The type of change you'd like to achieve is possible, and since a change in self-confidence and self-love is deep rooted, this comes about gradually and slowly.Keep understanding yourself during your moments of doubt and anxiety.  Its fine to feel terrible and certainly better to know you feel insecure than to act upon the insecurity by being with men who take advantage of you.Everyone needs to feel loved and recognized.Until you attract a partner who will love and appreciate you, develop relationships with co-workers and classmates, neighbors, so that you have some quality of giving and being given by someone.It is a way to nurture yourself and another person until your strength and self-belief grow bigger.
LLM:
I understand that you're feeling distressed about your pattern of attracting unavailable men and enabling toxic relationships. It's important to acknowledge the pain and discomfort that comes with this behavior.

Firstly, I'd suggest practicing self-compassion and self-care. You deserve to be in a healthy, secure relationship where you feel valued, respected, and loved. Reach out to supportive friends and family or consider joining a support group.

Secondly, examine why you might be drawn to unavailable men. Are there underlying emotional needs or fears that might be contributing to this pattern? A therapist can help you explore potential underlying causes and provide you with coping strategies.

Thirdly, learn to set healthy boundaries and prioritize your own needs. This might mean learning assertiveness skills or practicing mindfulness meditation to help manage anxiety around being alone.

Lastly, be patient with yourself and acknowledge that change takes time. Allow yourself to make mistakes and learn from them. Remember that everyone deserves a healthy, secure relationship and that it's possible to make healthy choices.

Context:
I was with my friends fishing by a pond. I found a frog and stabbed it with my friend's knife. I'm not sure why I did it. My friends saw me do it it and were shocked and got really mad at me.

Now if someone brings it up I get really upset and panicked. I don't know why I feel like this. Does this mean there is something wrong with me?
Response: The good news is your awareness that being violent to animals feels out of character for you.   I'm glad you have awareness and I'm glad you are not at ease with killing frogs.Fairly likely that you were acting out aggressively toward the frog, feelings of being on the receiving side of similar type of violence.Basically, you did unto the frog what someone has or to you, feels like, has been done to you.Use your surprising finding of how strong your anger and most likely inward pain.   Reflect on your closest relationships and how these people treat you.   It is possible you feel more hurt by someone who is close to you than you ever realized.Stabbing innocent frogs is wrong.  It is understandable as a way to show you how much inner pain you probably have.It is also wrong that someone close to you hurt you.The direction to go is to recognize your pain and then decide how to go about changing what is possible on your side of that or those relationship(s).
LLM:
It's normal to feel confused and upset when we do something unexpected that leads to negative consequences, especially when it involves harming a living being and causing others to react strongly. It's important to understand that everyone has impulses and makes mistakes from time to time, often in moments of stress, excitement or peer pressure. The fact that you feel bad about what happened and are having a hard time dealing with others' reactions is a sign that you have a conscience and that you care about how your actions affect others.

That being said, it's also important to reflect on what may have contributed to your impulsive behavior. Was there something happening in your life that was particularly stressful or chaotic at the time? Were you feeling angry, frustrated, or anxious? Did you feel a sense of power or control in the moment? These are all valid questions to ask yourself.

It may also be helpful to recognize that your reaction now, feeling upset and panicked, is a normal response. You've done something that goes against your values and principles, and it's natural to feel guilty and ashamed. However, it's important to remember that everyone makes mistakes, and it's how we respond to them that matters. Instead of trying to avoid the situation, it might be more productive to talk openly and honestly about what happened with your friends and seek their forgiveness, if appropriate. And if you find that you're having a hard time managing your emotions or dealing with the guilt, it may be helpful to seek the support of a mental health professional. They can help you process your feelings and develop strategies for managing your impulses and dealing with difficult emotions in healthier ways.

Context: I’m a man, and I’m soon to be married. I have been messing around with other men on the side. It started as a once in a while thing, but it’s been happening a lot. I don't know what to do.
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. When faced with these kinds of difficult challenges, I usually encourage people to look to their values for help in determining what their behavior should be. Indeed, it is usually when our behaviors conflict with our values that we don't feel very content with our lives. Perhaps you can take some time to think through a couple of these questions: What kind of person do I want to be deep in my heart? What kind of character do I want to have when in public and in my private world when nobody is watching? How would I want others to describe me? Let's say that you want others to see you as being authentic, and YOU also value authenticity as something you want to live by. You may feel like this value is currently at odds with the behaviors you are engaging in right now. That you are not being the person that you really are deep in your heart. This is not an unusual struggle when it comes to sexual behavior and sexual orientation. So please, try not to feel alone. We all know quite well how unacceptable it may be to be seen as gay, lesbian, bisexual, etc. And I am not just talking about being unaccepted by others, but a person may have a VERY hard time accepting themselves. Thinking about your values and living a life based on them can bring a lot of freedom, but I won't lie, it can also bring a lot of pain at the same time. For example, someone with the value of living authentically may decide they have to tell others who they are and what they stand for, even if that means people may disapprove. That may cause a lot of pain, but it may also come with a sense of freedom to live a life based on honesty and authenticity. It may be helpful to find a counselor who is trained in affirmative therapy, which is recommended for people who are navigating their sexual behaviors and figuring out what is right for them. I would also sit down and think through some of the questions above and consider what your values are... both as an individual and as someone's fiancé.Above all, I wish you well in this journey. Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM:
I understand that you're facing a difficult situation, and it's important to address your feelings and behaviors before making any decisions that could potentially impact not only yourself but also your partner and future marriage.

First and foremost, it's crucial for you to consider why you're engaging in these activities. Is it due to feelings of dissatisfaction with your partner, unmet emotional needs, or perhaps a deeper exploration of your sexuality? Gaining a better understanding of your motivations will be an essential step in finding a resolution.

Another important aspect is communication with your partner. Honesty and transparency are crucial in any relationship. It's recommended to approach the conversation with sensitivity, empathy, and a clear intention to work towards a solution, rather than placing blame or using accusatory language.

Lastly, seeking professional help can be very beneficial. A therapist or counselor can help guide you in exploring the underlying emotions and provide coping strategies to deal with feelings of shame, guilt, or confusion. It's essential to remember that working through these issues takes time, patience, and a genuine commitment to personal growth and healing.

Context:
I have so many issues to address. I have a history of sexual abuse, I’m a breast cancer survivor and I am a lifetime insomniac.    I have a long history of depression and I’m beginning to have anxiety. I have low self esteem but I’ve been happily married for almost 35 years.
   I’ve never had counseling about any of this. Do I have too many issues to address in counseling?
Response: Absolutely not.  I strongly recommending working on one issue/need at a time.  In therapy you will set smart goals and objectives that will help you reach your goals.  I see you as a survivor and not a victim.  Best wishes to you.
LLM:
It's understandable for you to feel overwhelmed with the number of issues you currently face, given your history of trauma, cancer survival, insomnia, depression, and newfound anxiety. It's important to note that there is no such thing as "too many issues" to address in counseling. In fact, I commend you for recognizing the importance of taking care of your mental health.

Counseling can provide a safe and supportive space where you can explore these issues at your own pace, and work towards developing the skills and resilience needed to manage them effectively. While it's true that dealing with multiple issues may take some time, the benefits of therapy can be significant and far-reaching.

Context:

Response: That is a really tough situation that a lot of young people are experiencing right now. The first thing to under is that you can’t change who your parents are and cannot change their behavior. The thing to keep in mind is that you can control your responses and actions.  You may need to keep living at home with them for the next few months or years and the best way to do this is to have a plan. The second thing is to keep in mind that their controlling behavior is most likely not about based your behavior. Controlling parents are often driven by their concept of what will keep their children safe and happy. Unfortunately this is not always accurate but keep in mind it is not a personal attack.  There are ways you can deal with controlling parents – and most of them require creating a plan of action.Examples of action plans:Decide in advance how you’ll calmly and rationally respond to your parents when you feel they’re trying to control youArrange to phone a friend or trusted adult when you feel like you’re losing controlTalk in person to an adult you trust. LLM:

1. Communicate clearly: HAVE AN HONEST AND RESPECTFUL CONVERSATION with your mother about your feelings and your need for more independence.

To learn how to avoid getting into relationships with men in bad situations, consider implementing the following strategies:

Remember, you deserve to be in a loving, mutually beneficial relationship. It may take time and practice, but with intentional effort and support, you can make a change for the better.
