Context: I am becoming a Water Safety Instructor but I didn't have enough for a proper swimsuit. I was told by a boy in class that my top was displaying everything. I was very embarrassed.
Response: Sometimes we make ourselves feel worse with our own thoughts about things we can't change. We know that it is possible to overcome embarrassment and self-blame, but why does it seem so difficult to overcome it sometimes? If you tune into your own thoughts, or your "internal dialogue", you'll notice that your thoughts are connected to your feelings. So people who are holding onto embarrassment and self-blame often have thoughts like, "How could I do that?", "I looked so stupid!" "Now people saw things I did not want them to see!" If we tune in more, we might notice we are thinking that this is terrible, horrible, and that we cannot deal with it. These are self-sabotaging thoughts that lead to shame, self-blame and embarrassment. If you look at the evidence, you can find more rational thoughts such as, " We all make mistakes", "People can look all different ways at different time - this incident does not define me!" and "If someone has a bad image or thought about me, it is really OK! People have all kinds of thoughts about other people, and it does not have to be a major problem." When you practice your rational thoughts often and replace the negative ones, you will start to feel better, more confident and your embarrassment will decrease quickly.
LLM: I'm sorry to hear that you've experienced this situation, which can be both embarrassing and distressing for you. I can understand why you may be feeling self-conscious and embarrassed about the incident. It's important to remember that bodies come in all shapes and sizes, and everyone deserves to feel comfortable and confident in their own skin. It might be helpful to invest in a proper swimsuit that makes you feel good about yourself and allows you to focus on the important work you'll be doing as a Water Safety Instructor. Additionally, you may want to consider having a conversation with the boy who made the comment to clarify how his words made you feel and establish some boundaries for future interactions. Remember that your self-worth is not determined by other people's opinions and that you are capable of doing an excellent job as a Water Safety Instructor regardless of what you wear. If you find that the distress from this incident continues to impact you, it may be helpful to consider speaking with a mental health professional to explore coping strategies and build more self-confidence.

Context: I would like to be able to have more positive relationships in the present.
Response: Remember, the past no longer exists and the future is just an illusion as we don't know what will happen tomorrow, so take the present as your guide. That is all we can do, so keep saying this to yourself and try to help someone each day.  The happiness is helping others to feel a bit better.
LLM:
I understand. Building positive relationships takes effort, but it's definitely worth it. Here are some steps you can take:

1. Identify what you value in a relationship: Be clear about what kind of relationships you want to build and what you value in them (e.g., trust, respect, open communication, etc.).
2. Practice active listening: Give your full attention to the person speaking and make an effort to understand their perspective. This shows respect and helps build trust.
3. Show empathy: Put yourself in the other person's shoes and try to understand their feelings. This can help deepen your connection.
4. Practice effective communication: Be clear, concise and respectful when expressing your thoughts and feelings. This can help prevent misunderstandings and conflicts.
5. Be patient: Building positive relationships takes time. Don't expect things to happen overnight, and be willing to put in the effort to maintain the relationship over time.
6. Focus on the present: Instead of dwelling on past disappointments, focus on the present moment and what you can do to make the relationship stronger.
7. Show appreciation: Express your gratitude and appreciation for the other person. This can help build positive emotions and strengthen the bond.

Remember, everyone is unique and these steps may not work for everyone. It's important to be open to different approaches and find what works best for you. I hope this helps, and please let me know if you have any other questions.

Context: The eight months I've been dating my boyfriend, I have lied about a lot of things. I slept with a couple guys to get back at my boyfriend for breaking up with me. Now anything I say to him he won't believe, even if I'm being honest. I don't know what else to do at this point. He constantly criticizes me.
Response: Start a conversation with him about your sincere interest to earn back his trust.Ask him what he expects from you so that he will trust you again.If you are consciously willing and do try to earn back his trust, and have periodic conversations with him on whether he feels more at ease with trusting you, then after a few months of this, both of you should feel more secure in the relationship.
LLM: I understand that you're in a difficult situation right now. Lying in a relationship can create a lot of mistrust and negatively impact communication. It's essential to acknowledge your actions and their consequences. I would suggest being open and honest with your boyfriend about what you've done and why, even though it may be painful. If he rejects your confession, it might be best to reassess whether this is a healthy relationship. Continuously being criticized in a relationship can be damaging, and it's important to be treated with respect and understanding. If you decide to stay in the relationship, make sure to work on rebuilding the trust and focusing on honest and open communication. If you need further assistance, consider seeking the help of a therapist to navigate this situation.

Context: My fiancé doesn't think I trust him because I was cheated on before. However, he hides his phone and has been texting his ex-girlfriend, who he has two kids with. What should I do?
Response: Oh Chino, I only have half the picture here so I will be cautious. There's a chicken and an egg, and I don't know which came first. One thing I see clearly is that your fiance will always have to have communications with his ex, because they have children. Can you accept that? When suspicion and insecurity come along, your job is to look at the big picture and the real picture. Is there any real evidence that he is cheating? Remember that texting his ex is not evidence alone if the communications are about the kids. Has he earned your trust? If so, then it's not fair to punish him for the betrayals of others. He's not your ex. Reassure your heart that you are his priority if the larger evidence supports that. At the same time, you can let him know what your triggers are. For example, hiding his phone doesn't send you a good message, right? See, this is the chicken and the egg...is he hiding the phone because you've been overreacting when he gets a text?  So let him know you won't blow up about him getting a text if he doesn't conceal or password his phone. Transparency helps build trust. You have sensitivities, and it helps you a lot if he's aware of them and willing to try not to do things that trigger fear. Let him know when you get scared, and hopefully he'll give you the openness and security you need in order to trust him and act as if you trust him.  :)
LLM: I understand that this situation may be causing you distress and confusion. The behavior of your fiancé, including hiding his phone and continuing communication with an ex-girlfriend, may be causing you to feel a lack of trust due to past experiences of being cheated on. This is a valid concern and warrants open communication. If you feel comfortable, express your concerns in a calm and non-confrontational manner. Ask your fiancé for transparency regarding the nature and reason for his texting, and express your need for trust and assurance in the relationship. Additionally, it may be helpful to establish boundaries that foster trust and healthy communication between you and your partner. This may include setting clear rules around texting and communication with ex-partners, as well as maintaining open lines of communication and emotional availability within your current relationship.

Context: I just wanted to get to know one so I can hear about their college experience and the courses they took. I also wanted to know if they enjoy their job and how long they were in school.
Response: It's a great idea for you to reach out to find a psychologist to talk to if this is a field you might be interested in pursuing. Regarding the length of schooling, it generally takes 4 years of college and an additional 4 to 7 years of graduate school to earn a doctorate degree to become a psychologist. There are also other similar professions, including counseling that don't require a doctorate degree. Licensed counselors generally completed 4 years of college and an additional 2 years of graduate school. If you are thinking about a career in psychology, the local APA chapter would be a good place to start to find a local psychologist to talk to. Here's a link to the NJ APA chapter:https://www.psychologynj.org/
LLM: I understand that you're curious about someone else's college experience and career path. However, it's important to remember that everyone's journey is unique. Context: We've been dating for two months now. I have a heavy past that gets him angry. Does he have a right to penalize me for things I did before I met him?
Response: Not in my book. You're only two months in? At this early stage, this is a bit of a warning sign. If he can't accept or feel comfortable with who you are and decisions you've made, there's already a level of discomfort here that shouldn't be ignored. No, he doesn't have the right to penalize you or punish you ...not just for past things...not for anything. What he does get to do it tell you how it affects him. Sure...if he feels uncomfortable or needs reassurance...listen to those things. But If he doesn't dig who you are, maybe he can walk the other way.
LLM: It's natural for past issues to create discomfort or concern in a new relationship. However, as a psychologist, I would remind you that each relationship is unique and distinct from those that came before it. If your partner's reactions are consistently harmful or abusive, you may want to reconsider the health of the relationship. If he expresses frustration out of concern or care, it's crucial to have an open and empathetic conversation to build mutual understanding and resolution.

Context: My roommate has had a drinking problem for a while. She goes out, gets trashed, and then expects everyone to take care of her. You hate me. Tell me you love me," and I feel like I have to pity her because she has also been suicidal before.
Response: It does sound like your roommate may have a drinking problem, and the short answer is that you can't do anything about it, only she can. We can't change what other people say, think, or do. You can reassure her that you like her (if that's true), but you don't have to pity her.
LLM:
I understand that your roommate's drinking behavior and emotional manipulation have been causing you distress. It's important to recognize that you cannot control another person's drinking or their emotional responses. This is not your responsibility. You are not required to make her feel validated, or to accept abusive or manipulative behavior.

It's crucial that you set healthy boundaries and prioritize your own emotional well-being. You can express your feelings to her, but make it clear that her actions have consequences and that you cannot continue to enable her behavior.

You've mentioned her suicidal thoughts, and it's important to take these seriously. If you feel she is a danger to herself, please encourage her to seek professional help immediately, such as a doctor or a mental health professional.

I recommend that you consider your own safety and well-being in this situation, as well. If her behavior is too much for you to bear, or if she is becoming physically or emotionally abusive, you may consider finding a new living situation.

In addition to setting boundaries with your roommate, consider talking with a therapist about your own emotional response to her behavior and how to cope with the stress it causes you.

It's not easy to be in a situation where someone you care about is struggling, but remembering that you can't fix things for her and focusing on your own emotional health and well-being can be helpful.

Context: After first meeting the client, what is the process that a counselor facilitates?
Response: Hi.  This is an excellent question !   I think that the answer probably varies depending upon the particular therapist .  In my work with people , once we have met and I have gotten an idea of the person'a concerns and the issues they want help with , I spend the next few sessions gathering as much history and as much information about the client's current concerns in order to formulate some ideas about what may be causing distress .  I would then share my thoughts with the client to see if they feel I am understanding them and on the right track.  We would then discuss the best plan to address the client's concerns .  Usually I will suggest strategies that I think may be helpful and ask the client for feedback about whether or not they think my suggestions feel helpful .   I always encourage clients to be really honest with me about this.  I tell them that I would hate for them to agree to try things that they know they won't try just to avoid "hurting my feelings" or "offending me."  I want to be helpful and while I have the expertise as far as typically helpful  strategies, I really like to work collaboratively and have clients tell me what they do and don't like / agree with or not agree with when I share my thoughts about a treatment plan .  We the   work together to come up with a plan that will be helpful , but also realistic and then revise it and try new things if necessary as we go along.   If things aren't improving , I am very happy and willing to try something new ! I hope this is helpful for you !
LLM: After the initial meeting or intake session, a counselor will begin the therapeutic process by establishing a therapeutic relationship with the client. This involves creating a safe and trusting environment where the client feels comfortable sharing their thoughts, feelings, and experiences. The counselor will also assess the client's needs and concerns, and use various therapeutic techniques and modalities to help the client achieve their goals. This may involve setting up ongoing sessions, creating a treatment plan, or making referrals to other professionals as needed. The exact process may vary depending on the specific needs and unique circumstances of the client.

Context: I feel really uncomfortable when I have people's attention.  It makes me not want to talk in public or answer questions in class. Can I get over this social anxiety?
Response: Since anxiety usually about anticipating a bad result, or fear of being judged negatively, as though having failed some sort of test, consider what would give you the feeling of anticipating a good result from talking to an audience?Maybe, for example, if you prepared and rehearsed your talk more than what you typically would, you'd feel more secure in speaking  in front of an audience.Once you sort through your particular reason as to the source of your discomfort, it may feel more manageable.Yes, social anxiety can change.  You may naturally prefer smaller groups than an audience.And, it is possible to decrease anxiety in what may always be somewhat uncomfortable for you.
LLM: Absolutely, social anxiety is a common experience and can be managed with various techniques. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is an effective approach. In CBT, we identify and challenge negative thoughts and behaviors that contribute to social anxiety. Exposure therapy, a part of CBT, can help you gradually face feared situations, allowing you to build confidence and coping skills. Additionally, practicing relaxation techniques and engaging in activities that bring you joy can help reduce anxiety overall. Remember, I'm here to support you throughout this journey, so don't hesitate to ask if you have any questions or concerns.

Context: I've been with him for a couple months. We will talk everyday and he will get mad over something I will say and not talk to me. We have our great moments, but I just need to focus on my personal situations, and I feel he is slowing me down with that. I still wanna be with him, but not now.

Context: When I get talked to by my dad or stepmom, or if I'm in trouble, I will begin to cry and not say what's wrong with me. When my dad or stepmom ask what is wrong, I never know the answers or the words to put together what is actually wrong with me.

Context:
I've been bullied for years and the teachers have done nothing about it. I haven't been diagnosed with depression, but i have been extremely sad for years.

How can I deal with being bullied at school when the teachers won't help?
Response: To be bullied is very painful.I'm glad you know the bullies are doing the wrong thing and whatever they are telling you is pure meanness.Depending on how independent you feel, why not tell either your classroom teacher or schedule an appointment with the vice principal?Bullying other students is taken very seriously in some states and schools are expected to address the bullying until the victimized student feels safe.If you hesitate to do this on your own, then would you ask either of your parents or even a friend of yours who is also in the school, to report the problem?In case you are afraid the bullies will retaliate for reporting them, then tell this to whatever school authority you decide to ask for help.You're entitled to be protected by your school.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you've been experiencing bullying for an extended period of time and that you have not received adequate support from your teachers. I can understand how this can be a very distressing situation and can contribute to feelings of sadness.

Firstly, it's important to focus on what you can do to help yourself in this situation. Here are some steps you can take:

1. Try to build a support network outside of school. This could be family, friends, or mentors. Having positive relationships with people who care for you can help you feel better about yourself and give you the strength to cope with the bullying.
2. Practice good self-care. This can include getting enough sleep, eating well, and engaging in activities that bring you joy. Taking care of yourself can help improve your mood and give you the energy to deal with challenging situations.
3. Consider speaking with a trusted adult outside of school, such as a guidance counselor, school psychologist, or a trusted family member. They may be able to provide you with additional support and advocate on your behalf.
4. Consider seeking professional help, such as seeing a therapist or counselor. They can provide you with tools and strategies to help you cope with bullying and improve your overall mental health and wellbeing.
5. Look into the school's bullying policy and procedures. Are there resources or support available to you there? Have other students reported bullying in the past and what actions have been taken? Make sure you are familiar with these policies and know how to access them if necessary.
6. Consider joining extracurricular activities or clubs where you can meet like-minded individuals and build strong relationships. This can help you feel more connected and less isolated.

It's important to remember that you are not alone in this situation, and that seeking help and support is a courageous step towards improving your overall wellbeing. I wish you all the best in navigating this challenging time. Please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns.

Context: Sometimes, when I look at my pet cat, I think about how innocent he is and how somebody could hurt or kill him. Context: When I see something I don’t like, I go off like a ticking time bomb. I go from “0 to 100” really quickly.
Response: Sometimes we react to situations immediately, without thinking of the consequences of our actions. Typically by not reacting right away, we are better able to gather our thoughts, see the situation more clearly and from other perspectives, and respond more calmly. One of the strategies that almost always helps is deep breathing. When faced with a stressful situation, you can give yourself a time out by removing yourself from the stress and spend 5 minutes taking deep breaths (breathe in slowly counting to 4 and breathe out even slower counting to 6), while focusing and thinking only about your breathing. After doing so, think of the various ways you can respond to the situation and choose the one most appropriate. Good luck!
LLM: That's a common experience for many people, particularly those with high emotion sensitivity or a strong reaction to stressors. This reaction is known as an emotional outburst, and it can be challenging to manage. It's essential to identify the triggers that cause these intense emotional responses, as well as effective coping mechanisms to prevent or manage outbursts. Practicing relaxation techniques, such as deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation, can help reduce the intensity of emotional responses. Additionally, cognitive restructuring and problem-solving skills can help put things into perspective, allowing you to respond to situations in healthier ways instead of reaching a "100" emotion level. Remember, it's okay to have emotions, but it's also essential to learn how to manage them effectively.

Context: He's been losing feelings and he doesn't know why. I love him very very much. He sometimes thinks I'm obsessed when I'm not at all. Response: I'm willing to bet that this isn't what you are hoping to hear, but I'd suggest giving him space. Ooph, that's a tough one, right?! I know. LLM:
I understand your concern, and it's natural to feel hurt and worried when your partner seems to be losing feelings towards you. However, it's important to remember that relationships go through ups and downs, and this could be a phase that requires extra effort and patience.

Based on what you've shared, it does seem that both of you could benefit from more communication. You may want to start by having a calm and non-confrontational conversation with your partner about your concerns and how you both can work together to strengthen your relationship. It's also important that you listen actively and respond in a considerate way to what your partner is saying.

If your partner is not willing to talk about it, you may need to consider other ways to express your feelings and concerns. You can write letters, send texts, or even consider couples therapy. It's important to remember that change takes time, and it may take some effort and persistence on your part to see improvements.

At the same time, it's important to take care of yourself and not get too fixated on the relationship. Try to engage in hobbies, spend time with friends, and prioritize self-care. Keep in mind that a healthy relationship involves two partners who are both willing and able to invest time, energy, and effort into the relationship.

Context: I believe my partner has a masturbation and porn problem. He masturbates daily, even when I am lying in bed sleeping beside him. We have sex once a week. He is rough and worries about his needs. He never touches me, and treats me like a porn star, wanting to finish on my face or chest.

LLM:
I understand your concerns. It sounds like there may be some significant mismatches in your sexual relationship that are causing distress. The frequency and intensity of his solo activities, combined with what appears to be a disconnect between your sexual preferences, might be indicative of an underlying issue.

It is important to note that everyone has different sexual needs and desires. However, when these differences significantly impact one partner and contribute to feelings of inadequacy or disconnection, it can be detrimental to the relationship dynamic.

Several factors could be contributing to his behavior. Anxiety, stress, or underlying mental health concerns might be driving his need for more frequent sexual release. Additionally, the influence of societal norms and societal pressure to maintain a "sexually active" image can be powerful.

I would suggest considering the following options:
1. Open communication: Discuss your feelings and concerns with your partner. Be open and honest about what's bothering you, without judging or blaming him.
2. Professional help: Consider seeking guidance from a sex therapist or relationship counselor. They can help facilitate a conversation and provide strategies for improving your relationship.
3. Education and self-reflection: Both partners can benefit from learning more about each other's sexual preferences and desires. This can be done through reading books, attending workshops, or seeking out professional resources.
4. Self-care: Ensure both of you are taking care of yourselves physically, emotionally, and mentally. This can help reduce feelings of stress and anxiety that might be contributing to your partner's behavior.

Remember, it may take time to see improvement. Patience and a willingness to work together are essential.

Context: I start counseling/therapy in a few days (I'm freaking out) but my main fear is that I'll cry and embarrass myself, is it something to worry about?
Response: Please feel free to cry during therapy if you suddenly feel painful emotions arising when you describe some of your unsatisfying and unfulfilling relationships and situations.Crying when realizing the emotional hurt and unfairness between you and someone, clears you for new ways of handling interactions.As a therapist, I always have a box of tissues on the table next to the couch where people who utilize my therapy, sit.I hope you will soon discover that once your tears unlock your awareness of harmful relationships in your life, you will quickly see the freedom to establish new and happier patterns of relating.Have a good therapy!

Context: How do I decide if I should end my relationship with my boyfriend?
Response: This is a tough decision to make for anyone that has ever been involved in a relationship.  My advice is for you to consider what is making you unhappy in this relationship.  Sometimes we think we are ready to move on but don't know the reason why and then we regret it.  The worst thing you want after the fact is to have regret.  Take time to do some soul searching and imagine your life without this person before you make any decisions.  This will also help you understand what it is you are looking for in a romantic relationship. Best of luck in your love life. Mirella~Image and Likeness Counseling
LLM:
I'm glad you're considering your emotional well-being in this decision-making process. When deciding to end a relationship, it's essential to evaluate if the relationship brings you more happiness, peace, and growth than it causes stress, unhappiness, or negativity.

Consider the following aspects:

1. Communication: Can you express your feelings and thoughts effectively with your partner? Do they listen and respond with care and understanding?

2. Respect: Is there mutual respect and trust in your relationship? Do you respect each other's boundaries and beliefs?

3. Compatibility: Do your values, interests, and goals align? Do you enjoy spending time together and feel a strong connection?

4. Emotional Support: Does your partner provide emotional support and encouragement in times of difficulty?

5. Future: Do you see a future with your partner, and does it align with your personal goals?

6. Safety: Are you emotionally and physically safe in the relationship?

7. Balance: Does the good outweigh the bad? Do the positive aspects of the relationship contribute more to your overall well-being than the negative?

Keep in mind that it's natural for relationships to have ups and downs, but it's essential to evaluate if this relationship is overall beneficial to you. Remember, my role is to help you process your thoughts and feelings, but the final decision is yours alone.

Feel free to share more details and we can explore these aspects further. However, it may be beneficial to consult a mental health professional if you have trouble making this decision.

Context:

LLM: I understand that you feel overwhelmed with the number of issues you are dealing with. However, having multiple issues does not mean that they cannot be addressed in counseling. In fact, many people come to therapy with complex and multi-faceted concerns. I would recommend a comprehensive approach, where we can explore each issue in a thoughtful and supportive way. This may mean integrating different therapeutic approaches to address your history of sexual abuse, depression, anxiety, low self-esteem, chronic insomnia, and experiences as a breast cancer survivor. I want to assure you that with a personalized treatment plan and a collaborative therapeutic relationship, you can make meaningful progress towards improving your overall well-being. Please feel free to ask any questions or share any concerns you may have.

Context: I have PTSD. The side effects are really bad and have impacts on sex, anger and my relationships. I even lost my job month ago.    How can I get my life back?
Response: Slowly is the rate at which you'll get back your life.Being traumatized means not feeling safe in almost all areas of life.Be patient with yourself as you try to regain trust that people will not harm you and will be sources of satisfaction in your life.It is possible that the trauma in your life requires such great attention on your part to your own inner emotional safety that you are better off with a less intensive job than the one you recently lost.Try to prioritize restoring your emotional and psychological health.  With this as the top area of your attention then you may have an easier time to accept a lesser degree of involvement in your work and relationships.When you feel angry, try to examine if underlying the anger are feelings of stress, fear, insecurity regarding your position in relationship to the person toward whom you feel angry.   Anger is often the surface reaction to more destabilizing emotions like fear and insecurity.Gradually by nurturing and comforting yourself, living at a pace which is uniquely comfortable to what and how much you can handle, you'll regain your trust in both yourself and relating to others.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear about your PTSD and the challenges you've been facing. Getting your life back on track after such experiences can be difficult, but it's not impossible. Here are some steps that may help:

1. Seek professional help: Consider reaching out to a mental health professional who specializes in trauma and PTSD. They can help you develop coping skills, manage symptoms, and provide support through the healing process.
2. Create a self-care routine: Focus on yourself and your well-being. Prioritize getting enough sleep, eating healthy meals, and incorporating activities that bring joy and relaxation into your daily life.
3. Connect with others: Engage in activities that allow you to connect with others, such as joining a support group or hobby club. Building a strong social network can help reduce feelings of isolation and improve overall well-being.
4. Develop a plan for managing symptoms: Identify triggers and develop strategies to cope with them when they arise. This may include using relaxation techniques, distracting yourself, or seeking support from a trusted friend or mental health professional.
5. Seek employment resources: Look into job placement programs or career counseling services to help you find a job that fits your abilities and needs. It's important to remember that taking time to focus on your mental health is an essential step toward getting your life back on track. Don't be too hard on yourself, and take things one day at a time.

Context: I read that you should ignore them and they have to come to a conclusion that they were wrong on their own terms. Is that correct?
Response: It is not correct because someone who is narcissistic believes they are always right.If you ignore the person, then their thinking is that there is something wrong with you.Ignoring the person to the degree this is possible in the situation or relationship, will spare you to be misunderstood further.
LLM:
As a psychologist, I would say that ignoring someone who is upset or distressed can sometimes be a useful strategy, particularly if the interaction is escalating or becoming unproductive. However, it's important to remember that this approach doesn't guarantee that the other person will come to a realization on their own, and it may not be effective in all situations.

In the context of interpersonal conflicts or conflicts in relationships, a more effective approach may be to try and understand the other person's perspective and validate their feelings, while also expressing your own thoughts and feelings in a respectful and calm manner. This is often referred to as "active listening" or "effective communication" and can help foster a more productive and empathic interaction.

Ultimately, the best approach depends on the specific situation and the people involved. As a psychologist, I would be happy to help you explore different communication strategies and techniques that might be useful in your particular circumstances.

Context:

First and foremost, cut yourself some slack. No one is perfect. We fail more often than we succeed, but in the end we learn, and that learning helps us grow. Yes, you're right, the abusive aspects of your marriage are not good, and need to stop. You probably are doing more harm than good in that regard, and this will quickly destroy anything you have in your marriage that is even remotely in your favor. Seeing a therapist for yourself, and then seeing a therapist as a couple is a good approach. You have issues that you need to resolve, that, while they impact your marriage because they are part of the history of your life, they are separate from your marriage in many respects.Joining a marriage support group can also be helpful. You can find these through local counseling resources. Sometimes your local crisis numbers can provide services in your area that can be helpful. If you belong to a religious or spiritual community, then you might see if there is any type of counseling offered for free or at a reduced rate. Sometimes pastoral support is focused on the spiritual side of any issues addressed, so if you feel this is important it might be a viable option for you.Lastly, parents are imperfect. Yep...its true. They had no more of a guaranteed parenting success manual than you do at having chances for a perfect marriage. We limp along and do our best. Having said that, we can still take the best from our past and apply to our future, no matter how small or insignificant it might be for us. The bad stuff serves as lessons of what to avoid, and we are each responsible and accountable for our actions in this life. Both with ourselves, and toward other people. So this becomes a guiding principle in our actions. It may be that you discover that the marriage you are in is not a good fit.  I am sure that remains to be seen, but either way you both must be involved in saving it. If only one of you cares, then 100% of the energy to salvage the marriage rests on the shoulders of one person. That's not fair. So be sure you have a clear understanding from your wife about what she wants, and work to create an amicable solution. All of this said, seek out supportive friends, and actively work to do your part to make the changes you want to see in your life. One way or the other, you will benefit from it.Be well.
LLM:
I understand that you're experiencing a great deal of relationship difficulties with your wife, and you're feeling overwhelmed and frustrated. It sounds like you come from a complex family background, which has contributed to your current challenges in maintaining a healthy and fulfilling relationship.

To address your concerns, I would recommend the following steps:

1. Communication: Make an effort to clearly express your thoughts, feelings, and needs to your wife, encouraging her to do the same. Identify specific triggers for disagreements and work collaboratively to find solutions.

2. Empathy and Understanding: Try to understand your wife's perspective and needs. Validate her feelings and show her that you care about her emotions as well as your own.

3. Professional Help: Consider seeking guidance from a couples therapist. A professional can help facilitate constructive conversations and provide tools and techniques to improve communication and understanding between the two of you.

4. Personal Growth: Focus on self-improvement and personal growth. Work on resolving past issues and developing emotional intelligence, which will enable you to better handle disagreements and emotions in your relationship.

5. Compromise and Flexibility: Be willing to make compromises and be open to changes, especially as they relate to family and relationship expectations. Understand that everyone has unique backgrounds and perspectives, and that being flexible in your approach can strengthen your bond with your wife.

6. Create Space: Establish boundaries that prioritize each other's emotional well-being and personal needs. This can involve setting aside specific times for meaningful conversations or creating physical space when disagreements escalate.

7. Seek Closure: Addressing past issues, unresolved conflicts, and broken relationships can be important steps towards personal healing and overall relationship health.

I encourage you to continue this conversation with a professional counselor or therapist. They can offer more personalized and professional advice to help you make significant progress in your relationship.

Context: I'm obsessing about a terrible breakup. Everything is a constant reminder. How do I move on?
Response: The best way to move on is to give yourself sufficient time and space away from your ex so you can heal.  That means no checking out your ex on social media platforms and no contact.  In order to move on and diminish the obsessions, help yourself recognize and accept that the relationship is over, and make sure to get sufficient support in processing all your feelings.  Once that takes place, it can be useful to explore and process with a professional the meaning of the relationship and to understand your part in what transpired.Sometimes people obsess because they have difficulties accepting what's already taken place and want things to be different.  The key to moving forward is to be loving toward yourself, to give yourself permission to grieve the loss, and to start cultivating new and healthy habits/patterns.  Reengage in your present life, ask yourself what you want your life to look like, and start creating goals and taking small steps to create the amazing life you want for yourself.
LLM:
I understand that the breakup has been painful for you, and it's natural to feel overwhelmed by constant reminders. The process of moving on involves several steps. First, allow yourself to process your emotions, including sadness, anger, or frustration. Journaling, talking to trusted friends or family members, or even joining a support group can be helpful.

Next, engage in activities that bring you joy and help take your mind off the breakup. This can include anything from reading a book to practicing a new hobby. Being physically active, such as through exercise, has also been shown to help improve mood and overall well-being.

Another step is to focus on the present moment and setting achievable, short-term goals for yourself. This can help provide a sense of accomplishment and direction.

It's also important to practice self-care, which includes getting enough sleep, eating well, maintaining good hygiene, and taking time for relaxation and stress management techniques such as meditation or yoga.

Lastly, consider seeking professional help if the emotions and thoughts are overwhelming. Talking to a therapist, psychologist, or counselor can provide a safe and supportive space to work through the feelings and develop effective coping strategies.

It may take time, but keep in mind that healing is a process, and it's important to be patient and gentle with yourself throughout this journey.

Context: My girlfriend just quit drinking and she became really depressed. She told me that she wants to move. What can I do to help her? I want her to stay.
Response: You should start by speaking with her about what has her so down. Have their been some significant events in her life that have affected her? Have their been significant events in your relationship that can be affecting  you both? If she just stopped drinking, she may be struggling to finding an alternative and healthier behavior. This is something the two of you can explore together. Also, if need be perhaps she can speak to a therapist to discuss her feelings about quitting alcohol. It sounds like you really care and right now she could use all the support she can get despite her pushing you away.
LLM:
I understand that your girlfriend is going through a difficult time after quitting drinking. It's important to remember that quitting alcohol can lead to withdrawal symptoms, which can include depression and anxiety. Here are some suggestions on how you can help her through this period:

1. Be supportive: Let her know that you are there for her and that you care about her.
2. Listen actively: Encourage her to express her feelings and concerns.
3. Encourage self-care: Help her find healthy activities that can help her manage stress and improve her mood, such as exercise, meditation, reading, or taking a relaxing bath.
4. Encourage professional help: If her depression is severe, encourage her to seek professional help from a mental health practitioner or counselor.
5. Be patient: Quitting drinking is a major change, and it can take time for her to adjust.
6. Avoid enabling behavior: While you want to support her, be sure not to enable her to return to drinking or engage in other unhealthy behaviors.
7. Be flexible: Be open to changing your plans if she needs more time to rest or take care of herself.
8. Set healthy boundaries: Establish clear and respectful boundaries that promote a healthy and mutually supportive relationship.

Context: I'm feeling rejected and frustrated. This is not new. The first month we were together, sex was about every day. Then it slowed down to once a month right away. I love my husband, but I am struggling with this.

LLM: I understand that you're feeling frustrated and rejected in your relationship due to a decrease in sexual intimacy with your husband. Sexual desire and frequency can vary throughout a relationship, and it's common for fluctuations to occur. Engaging in activities that foster intimacy and connection outside of sexual intercourse may also be helpful. Context: My daughter didn't see her biological father for the last three years. She doesn’t want to see him because she remember really bad things from him such as domestic violence and child abuse. Response: Hi Dillon,I'm from Canada, so I don't know the laws in your state. Context: My parents seem okay with other sexualities, but normally they only talk about being gay. When they do talk about bisexuality, they say things like “they'll do anything” or things that make me very uncomfortable because I am bisexual. I don't know if I am ready to come out to them.
Response: Perhaps you are not ready to come out to your parents.  Often times our readiness is not dependent on what others' will say or how they react. But more so dependent on how emotionally prepared we are to deal with others' reactions.  It may be beneficial for you to play out each possible scenario and conclusion (eg. your parents being supportive vs. your parents being disappointed and so on). How are you prepared to deal with the outcome?  You may also seek help through counselors, support groups and/or individuals you know that have dealt with similar situations to help you prepare to discuss this with your parents.  All the best!
LLM: I understand your concern. It's not uncommon for individuals to have mixed feelings about coming out to their parents, especially when they have heard negative or uncomfortable remarks about their sexuality. It's important to recognize that people's attitudes and beliefs can evolve over time. Before making a decision about coming out to your parents, it might be helpful to have an honest and open conversation with them. Use this opportunity to share your feelings and concerns about their past remarks, and ask them about their current views on bisexuality and other non-heteronormative sexualities. This can give you valuable insight into their current level of acceptance and understanding, and help you make an informed decision about when and how to come out to them. Remember, coming out is a personal decision, and you should only do so when you feel safe and ready to do so. If needed, you might also consider seeking out additional support from friends, therapists, or organizations to help you navigate this process.

Context: I've been going through a rough time lately. I been into nothing but women. I’ve never thought about men until a week ago. I’m very upset and depressed about this. It's not normal to me. I looked at gay porn more than once to prove that I’m not gay. I get the same results each time, and I feel disgust. This is tough on me. I'm scared that I looked too many times. I keep thinking about it and shake all the time.
Response: Hello. Coming to terms with the idea that you might be of a sexuality other than the one in which you were socially raised to be, can be emotionally disturbing, and quite unsettling. It creates anxiety, maybe even panic, and leaves us feeling confused and uncertain about our own sense of identity. It becomes important to first ask yourself how you feel when you are thinking of being intimate with guys, or if you desire them to be intimate with you. It is important to know what impact this has on you, because it can determine your level of follow through and desire in pursuing sexual activity with someone of the same sex. I am not going to pass judgment on you either way - even for having the thoughts. I have worked with the lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer (LGBTQ) community in various ways over the years. Many I speak to, say they had to struggle to come to terms with this awareness about how they see themselves sexually. Maybe you are bisexual, and don't have an exclusive attraction to the same sex. You might consider doing some searches online for community support groups in your area, to explore the feelings and issues around this topic. Talking to someone who you trust can be helpful too. Running from your feelings is not a good idea, and others in a support group for sexuality issues, might be able to relate to you and what you're going through right now. This will offer you perspective, as you think on how this plays out in your life.Counselors are typically well trained to provide clinical services to clients who are experiencing feelings and thoughts much like the ones you are having now. I encourage you to consider reaching out for help. You are not crazy or insane for thinking of working with a counselor, nor of having feelings of same sex attraction. If you decide you are indeed gay, then know that you are not alone and never will be alone in that feeling. There is a huge community of support for you. Additionally, we all need help sometimes dealing with all sort of issues. A counselor can work more in depth with you to examine the motivations behind the feelings, and help you come to terms with them more directly. I hope that you come to a place where you feel less alone and more secure with yourself as you examine this area of your life. No matter what, do not fear being yourself. Again...you are not alone, I promise.Warm regards,Shawn Berthel, M.S., LMHC
LLM: I understand that you're feeling distressed about the recent emergence of same-sex attractions and that you're experiencing strong negative emotions and even fear. It's important to acknowledge that sexuality is not binary and that it can evolve and change throughout one's life. It's also crucial to recognize that experiencing same-sex attraction does not make you any less of a person or define your identity. The repeated watching of gay porn does not automatically mean you are gay. It's essential to engage in open, honest communication with yourself and, if necessary, your support network about your feelings and concerns. You might also consider seeking the help of a mental health professional, such as a therapist, who can provide you with guidance, support, and help you navigate through this complex emotional experience. Remember, there's no "right" or "wrong" way to feel about your sexuality, and your feelings are valid, regardless of societal or personal expectations.

Context:
I just took a job that requires me to travel far away from home. My family and I really need this job.
   People keep telling me I have "anxiety" and I'm terrified of having an anxiety attack on the road. This is all new to me. What can I do?
Response: Hi, It is really good that you are working so hard to take care of your family. Anxiety can be so challenging, especially when new challenges have come up. It would be good to work through some of these issues you are experiencing, and examine your self care and support processes.
LLM:
I understand your situation and concerns. Anxiety can be debilitating, and traveling can add additional stressors. Here are some suggestions:

1. Preparation: Make sure you're well-prepared for your trip. This can help alleviate some of the anxiety.
2. Relaxation Techniques: Learn some relaxation techniques like deep-breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation. Practice these techniques daily to help manage your anxiety.
3. Set Small Goals: Break the trip into smaller manageable segments. This can help prevent overwhelming feelings.
4. Healthy Lifestyle: Ensure you're getting adequate sleep, eating well, and staying hydrated. Regular exercise can help reduce anxiety and improve overall well-being.
5. Seek Support: Reach out to your family, friends, or colleagues. They can provide emotional support and help you manage any anxiety triggers.

It's also important to note that everyone experiences anxiety at times, and it is not uncommon to feel anxious when traveling or starting a new job. However, if your anxiety is significantly impacting your ability to function, it may be helpful to speak with a mental health professional who can help you develop a more comprehensive coping strategy.

Remember, taking care of your mental health is just as important as taking care of your physical health. I wish you all the best in your new journey. Let me know if you have any other questions.

Context:
I'm in my late teens and live with my dad.  The only time I go out is for my college classes. Sometimes when I see my friends I want to talk with them, but sometimes I won't want to talk to them for days or even weeks. 

Sometimes I feel i'm not worth knowing or i'm never going to do anything right.

Are they right, am I depressed?
Response: Hello. Being a teenager in our society today can cause depression quite easily. We look at the world in which we live, and the doubts we have about being able to make a contribution, or to have value and worth with the people around us, and we begin to internalize those messages of self-worth. If that alone were not enough, you are trying to figure out where you want to go in life, and who you are supposed to be in the world. Teens are faced with pressures of being judged by your peers for how you present yourself; following current fashions; possessing the trendy things; and hanging out with the "in crowd." Here's a "secret" that might help with most of those issues: Not much of those problems I've just described will really matter as they might seem to right now, in the real world. If you are feeling depression because you feel you lack some of those things that are seen as important by others (like your peers), I can assure you that being someone who is kind; compassionate; understanding of another person's suffering; and the effort you make to be the best person you can become in the world, will far outweigh anything you experienced in your teen years.When we are young, we judge everything based on what our peers have, what they say, the way they dress, who's popular, who's not popular, and for the most of it, all of that is external. So the question becomes, are you trying to fix the feelings on the inside (the internal stuff), with the trappings of all the stuff on the outside (the internal)? That is to say, do you feel that if you had what your friends have, you would be happy and less depressed? Depression is usually a symptom of something much bigger, it is not typically a problem by itself (just as a person who experiences a high temperature may likely have a cold). So fixing the symptom requires looking at the possible causes. In your post, you mentioned that you live with your dad. Are your parents divorced or separated, or has your mom passed away? Either of these circumstances could certainly cause depression. Finding out when you started feeling depressed, might be able to pinpoint the trigger that caused it, which requires looking at when you last felt really happy. It is also important to keep in mind, that as a teenager, your emotions are often being experienced very randomly, and sometimes without cause. It is part of your emotional development, but as you grow older can learn how to manage those emotions better and practice doing things that help you feel better (without causing harm to yourself).Have you tried talking to your dad about your feelings, or maybe another relative if you feel safer doing so? Do you have a friend who is your best friend...someone who is there for you no matter what? If not, there is a service you can use that is a national number for teens to talk with teen peer counselors. They are teens like yourself, but they have some insights that might be helpful. Its called Teen Line (https://teenlineonline.org/). It helped me when I was in my teens, and has helped lots of other teens as well. You might find a place to get some referrals to other local services in your area. If you want are in Washington state and would like to connect with me directly, you call link to me via my profile page. Or perhaps finding a counselor in your local area might be useful. Most towns and cities have some form of low cost counseling. You might also check with your school for assistance.Don't be afraid of not always knowing how you are supposed to feel. Adults don't always have a handle on it either. Being a teenager carries a lot stress, fear, and uncertainty. But you are not alone, and there is help if you reach out for it. I wish for you the very best, and bright future.
LLM:
Based on the information you've shared, it's possible that you may be experiencing some symptoms of depression. However, it's important to note that having some of these symptoms doesn't automatically mean that you're depressed. Depression is a complex mental health condition and various factors contribute to its diagnosis.

Your feelings of low self-worth, disinterest in socializing, and avoidance of seeing friends for extended periods of time are common symptoms of depression. However, other factors like your personal history, physical health, environment, and coping skills should also be considered when determining if you're depressed.

If you feel that your symptoms are significantly affecting your ability to function in daily life or you're considering self-harm or suicide, it's important to reach out to a mental health professional. They can provide you with a proper evaluation and the appropriate treatment options.

In the meantime, consider taking small steps to improve your mood and wellbeing. This can include activities like regular physical exercise, a healthy and balanced diet, adequate sleep, pursuing hobbies, and practicing stress management techniques.

Additionally, try talking to someone you trust, like a family member or friend, about how you're feeling. It can be helpful to speak openly about your thoughts and emotions with someone who cares about you and can offer support.

Remember, it's okay to ask for help and take care of yourself.

Context: I’m facing severe depression and anxiety and I just feel like I’m going through a lot. This really distracts me and I cant get my mind off the things that are bothering me.    How do I overcome this anxierty and depression?
Response: Anxiety and depression are often linked. I don't consider them as diseases. Depression is a symptom of deep hurts to the sense of self that leaves a person feeling shamed. Anxiety is a symptom of not being given the proper emotional resources to handle the problems of life. It is also the result of being taught that life is a dangerous place that can't be managed.
LLM:
I understand that you're going through a tough time right now. Anxiety and depression are common mental health concerns, and it's important to remember that you're not alone. Here are some steps that may help you feel better:

1. Reach out for support: Connecting with friends, family, or a mental health professional can provide reassurance and offer valuable insights.
2. Self-care: Engage in activities that you enjoy and bring you a sense of calm and relaxation. This could include practicing mindfulness meditation, reading, exercise, or taking a warm bath.
3. Adopt a healthy lifestyle: Ensure that you're getting enough quality sleep, eating nutritious meals, and engaging in regular physical activity. Avoid smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, and caffeine intake, as these substances can worsen anxiety and depression.
4. Practice relaxation techniques: Deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, visualization, and progressive relaxation can help reduce tension and promote feelings of calm.
5. Set small, achievable goals: Breaking large tasks into smaller, manageable goals can help build a sense of accomplishment and boost self-esteem.

Remember, recovery takes time and effort. Be patient with yourself and celebrate your progress, no matter how small. If your symptoms persist or worsen, I encourage you to reach out to a mental health professional for additional guidance and support.

Context: My husband and I have been married for seven years, and in that time, we have only had sex four or five times. Others have told me that most men would have left me by now. Honestly, I think I have a low sex drive or neither one of us actually knows what we are doing. I want to be better connected with my husband.
Response: It's encouraging that you say you want "to be better connected with [your] husband," and since he hasn't left you, he must care about you and the relationship more than he does about just having sex. You don't say whether he complains or not, but even if he doesn't it seems there's a sense of something important missing for both of you.Most likely to connect better with him physically you'll need to become better connected with yourself and your body. Men often feel fulfilled by sex simply because it happened - the woman they want received them, allowed them to make love with her. For women that can sometimes be more complicated. A woman who loves sex most likely also loves her body, knows what pleasures her, and feels confident asking for what she wants. Self-pleasuring can be a way for you to discover more about your sexuality, and a classic resource is Betty Dodson's book Sex for One: The Joy of Selfloving. A good counselor or sex therapist can be very helpful with your whole relationship as well as the sexual part of it, but not all counselors are comfortable working with sexual issues. LLM: I understand your concerns and it's important to acknowledge that sexual intimacy is an essential aspect of a healthy, long-term relationship. There could be several reasons for the infrequency of your sexual encounters. One possibility is that both you and your husband have low sex drives, which is common and nothing to be ashamed of. Another possibility is that there are underlying emotional or communication issues that need to be addressed. It might be helpful to discuss this openly and honestly with your husband. You could suggest couples therapy to help you both explore these issues and learn ways to improve your sexual connection. Context: I'm a teenager and I get these really intense mood swings.    My mood will be really high and I'll think of something that I want to do. When I start to make it happen I get irritated by other people if they intervene. Then if the thing I wanted to do doesn't work out, I have these tendencies to blame other people for it not working out.    Can you explain what's going on?
Response: Mood swings and getting frustrated when things don't work out is totally normal. Sometimes, we get tied to one thing we really want to do and it can feel pretty intense when that gets interrupted or doesn't work out. This said, you're coming to CounselChat and so I'm guessing this is something that is pretty intense for you and something that you are concerned about. If this is getting in the way of your life, it may be worthwhile to reach out to a therapist or doctor to get some help with this and see if there might be something more going on. While for most people frustration/mood swings is related to typical life and stress, if very intense it can be a sign of a number of challenges ranging from anxiety to a traumatic experience to something like Bipolar Disorder where a person's mood and energy might fly so high (mania) that one feels like they can do anything but often become frustrated when interrupted (note: there is a lot more to Bipolar Disorder than this and this is certainly not a diagnosis). Wish you well!
LLM: Absolutely, I'd be happy to help explain. Based on what you've shared, it sounds like you might be experiencing symptoms of Bipolar Disorder, specifically Bipolar II, which is marked by episodes of major depressive episodes and hypomanic episodes, not full manic episodes. Hypomanic episodes, like the one you described, can include elevated or irritable moods, increased goal-directed activity, and a decreased need for sleep. This can lead to irritation when others intervene and a tendency to blame them when things don't go as planned. However, it's important to remember that only a mental health professional can make a definitive diagnosis based on a thorough evaluation. I would recommend meeting with a mental health provider to discuss your symptoms and explore potential treatment options, such as psychotherapy and medication.

Context:

   I've never tried or contemplated suicide. I've always wanted to fix my issues, but I never get around to it.
   How can I change my feeling of being worthless to everyone?
Response: You can, for sure... and the first step is to believe you are worthy TO YOURSELF. Recognizing you are in a quest is already a big first step. Taking the time to start figuring out who you are is definitely the next... do so with SELF COMPASSION and kindness. Know this is a process and just look forward for what you might find.  I do recommend you find someone to talk to (there are many options out there that can fit the situation you are in), and I also recommend you start by giving yourself the importance to invest some time and effort in this quest. YOU DESERVE IT. There are some excellent TED Talks you can google, great books you can read, or even joining a yoga practice, team or some kind of hobby may help. Anything that shows yourself you are worthy of this effort. Have patience and start!!!! the answer is out there waiting for you. Asking these questions already set you on the right path.
LLM:
I understand that you're feeling overwhelmed and worthless, but please know that feelings are not the same as reality. It's important to remember that everyone has worth and value, regardless of how they feel. Here are some things that might help:

1. Identify your strengths: Make a list of your strengths, accomplishments, and positive characteristics. Focusing on the good things about yourself can help counter the negative thoughts.

2. Challenge negative thoughts: Try to identify and challenge your negative thoughts with evidence to the contrary. This is called cognitive restructuring.

3. Practice self-care: Engage in activities that make you feel good about yourself, such as exercise, hobbies, or reading. Eat a healthy diet, get enough sleep, and avoid drugs and alcohol.

4. Connect with others: Reach out to supportive friends and family members. Talk about your feelings and invite them to share their own.

5. Seek professional help: Consider seeing a mental health professional for counseling or therapy. They can provide guidance, support, and techniques to help you manage your thoughts and feelings more effectively.

Remember, it takes time and effort to change how you feel about yourself, but it's worth it. Make sure you're taking care of yourself and seeking the help you need.

LLM:

If her stress responses are interfering significantly with her ability to function day-to-day, or if they're accompanied by physical symptoms like headaches, stomachaches, or problems sleeping, then it may be a good idea to consult a healthcare professional or a mental health specialist, such as a psychologist or a therapist, for an evaluation.

Encourage her to engage in regular physical activity and maintain a balanced diet. You might also consider implementing a consistent study routine and setting aside dedicated time for activities she enjoys to help minimize stress and promote a sense of balance in her life.

However, I want to assure you that you are not alone in this, and there are effective treatments to help manage these symptoms.

I would recommend that you speak with a mental health professional, such as a therapist who specializes in trauma, to discuss your experiences and develop a personalized treatment plan. This could include various therapeutic approaches, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR), or other psychotherapies, to help you manage the emotions and memories associated with the past traumas.

It's also important to communicate openly with your boyfriend about what you are experiencing and how he can best support you during these moments. You can explain to him that these reactions are not about him, but rather about the past traumas, and that it's a part of your healing process.

Context: I've hit my head on walls and floors ever since I was young. Response: The best way to handle anxiety of this level is with a combination of appropriate medication given to you by a medical doctor, and therapy to help you understand the thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that are causing the anxiety. This is not something that anyone should just “white knuckle” and try to get through on their own with no help. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy is a technique that has been proven helpful for depression and anxiety. This takes a therapist trained in CBT. You will learn to recognize when and why you perform the behavior of hitting your head, help you deal with the underlying cause of this, and replace the behavior with a more positive behavior. LLM:

Context: My husband and I had our first threesome recently. Everyone was drinking and he was on her more then me.    He and I talked about it afterwards and it made me feel better, and now I'm craving more of it. But before it gets close to happening I get this empty feeling. Why am I feeling this way?
Response: You need to have an honest conversation with each other about WHY you both want a threesome. Evaluate the status of your relationship with each other. Are you having issues? Fighting? Not feeling satisfied with each other? How has your sex life been with each other?  If there are any problems, insecurities, issues, introducing something like this may only make your relationship worse. Open relationships and threesomes rarely work out well. Sure, there are some who successfully live this lifestyle, but it only works when both people are completely secure in their relationship with each other, harbor no jealousies or insecurities, and aren't looking to someone else to satisfy needs that aren't getting met by their spouse. Let me say that again: This is not likely to work out well for you if either of you are insecure, jealous, or looking to have needs met by this other person that you aren't getting from your spouse. That empty feeling you're having - listen to it. Dig deep to find out where it is coming from. What thoughts are going through your head when you feel that way? You'll get the answer.The other thing that helps make something like this work is for both of you to listen and respect each other. If you don't like it that he paid more attention to the other girl, he needs to know that, and he needs to respect that.  If you're going to do it again, he needs to know what you're comfortable with and what you want. Set the limits before you're in the moment, before it's too late to take an action back. Turn it around and ask him what he would be comfortable with if it were a man instead of a woman.It is perfectly natural to be curious and want to experiment. A lot of people get that out of the way before settling down in a monogamous relationship. People who marry young and/or inexperienced may still feel that curiosity about things like that and want to experience them, but don't want to hurt their spouse. The REASON you are both doing this matters a lot. If there are problems in your relationship, this is likely to only make things worse.
LLM:
It's important to remember that every individual's experiences and reactions to consensual sexual dynamics are unique. What you're feeling could be due to various factors, such as:

1. Envy: Recognizing that your husband may have spent more time with the other person during the threesome could be eliciting feelings of jealousy, leading to the empty feeling when anticipating future experiences.
2. Comparison: Comparing yourself to the other person in the threesome and feeling insufficient or inadequate in some way may contribute to the feelings of emptiness.
3. Infidelity: For some individuals, the fear of infidelity, even when consensual and open relationships are agreed upon, may result in unwanted feelings of sadness or guilt.
4. Intimacy: Your need and desire for emotional connection during the threesome may not have been fulfilled, causing you to feel empty or unsatisfied.

In any case, this is a complex emotion with many possible underlying causes. It may benefit you to have an open and honest conversation with your husband about your feelings, discuss boundaries and future experiences, and potentially consult a mental health professional for further guidance and assistance. Remember that every individual has unique emotional needs, and a therapist or counselor can help you navigate your situation and feel more confident in making informed decisions for your relationships.

Context: I am going through a very hard time and I'm so depressed. My parents are getting a divorce and a lot of bad things are happening. I want to lull myself.
Response: It sounds like a tough time and it's normal to feel down when your family is going through a divorce or other hardship. There's a difference between feelings like sadness, discouragement, grief, loneliness and depression, though. Sadness is sadness and sometimes we have to go through a grieving or other process, that is a normal reaction to events in our lives. Depression, on the other hand, is more about being stuck. Lulling yourself with self-care sounds like a healthy response and a way to give yourself support. So as to the uncomfortable but healthy part of your feelings, do the best you can to put names to it and share with a trusted other person. You will find comfort. The part of your situation that's depression -- feeling stuck, not able to get out of bad, concentrate, changes in sleeping and eating, loss of enjoyment in living, poor hygiene, feelings of hopelessness, that's different. First step, identify your own symptoms of depression. If you have any thoughts of hurting yourself, tell someone. If you're not sure, you can try 800-lifenet or other support line to get feedback. First aid for depression includes: daily care and feeding, good sleeping and eating, lots of exercise--even when you don't feel like it-- healthy socializing, and focusing on talking to yourself in a positive way. If you aren't able to move yourself out of your depression,  work with a professional to make short-term goals and get some support. You may also talk to your doctor or psychiatrist about trying an anti-depressant to give you a little lift while you go through a rough patch.Best wishes,Karen
LLM: I'm sorry to hear that you're going through a tough time. It's natural to feel sad and overwhelmed during a divorce, especially when there are additional stressors at play. However, it's important to remember that engaging in self-harm is not a healthy coping mechanism. Instead, consider healthier ways to manage your emotions, such as expressing yourself through writing, art, or music, practicing relaxation techniques like deep breathing or meditation, reaching out to a trusted friend or therapist, or engaging in regular physical activity. Remember, it's okay to feel sad and take time for self-care, but intentionally harming yourself won't help and can make things worse. I'm here if you need someone to talk to or if you want to explore other coping strategies.

If you're struggling to forgive your mom, it may be helpful to consider the following steps:

1. Acknowledge your emotions: It's natural to feel angry, hurt, and resentful. Validate these emotions by acknowledging how they make you feel.
2. Reflect on the situation: Try to understand the reasons behind your mom's mistakes. Were there any external factors that may have influenced her behavior?

Could it be that there is something they don't like about me? I don't understand why they won't answer my messages. How do I get people to respond to me?
Response: I understand the feeling of not being liked due to not having any responses on the sites you list.More than likely the non-response has less to do with liking you than w liking the way you write your messages.Have you tried asking a question in your posts?  This would be one way to invite people to answer your post.When you write,  imagine yourself as the reader of the post.If you think about the wording of a question which would motivate you to respond, then this formula will very likely be the same for many others who read your post.Good luck!
LLM:
It's natural for you to feel concerned when people don't respond to your messages. However, it's essential to understand that not receiving an immediate response doesn't necessarily mean that there's something wrong with you or that people don't like you. There could be several reasons why people don't answer your messages right away:

1. They might be busy: People lead complex lives, and their phones might not always be with them. Somebody could be at work, in class, or out running errands.

2. They might be preoccupied: People's mental and emotional states can vary, and they might be dealing with stress, anxiety, or other issues that make it difficult for them to respond to messages promptly.

3. They might be trying to avoid conflict: Sometimes, people don't know how to respond to messages that might be emotionally charged or that might provoke conflict. In such cases, they might choose to ignore the messages instead of addressing the issue.

4. They might have other priorities: People prioritize their time and energy differently, and some might prefer to spend their time on activities other than responding to messages.

Instead of assuming that people don't like you, try to communicate with them directly. If you feel comfortable, you could send them a text or call them to ask why they haven't responded to your messages. It might be an awkward conversation, but it could lead to a productive exchange. If you sense that the conversation might be difficult, you might want to consider seeking the help of a mental health professional, who can help you develop effective communication skills and provide guidance on how to deal with difficult social situations.

In summary, there are several reasons why people might not respond to your messages right away, and most of them have nothing to do with you or your worth as a person. Keep this in mind as you navigate social situations and communication with others.
