Context: My parents seem okay with other sexualities, but normally they only talk about being gay. When they do talk about bisexuality, they say things like “they'll do anything” or things that make me very uncomfortable because I am bisexual. I don't know if I am ready to come out to them.
Response:
Hello, and thank you for your question. Being bisexual can be particularly
difficult because it is often misunderstood, even by people who are “supposed”
to be part of your own community, such as lesbians and gays. It happens so
often that there is actually a term for it – 
bi-erasure. This is simply something that happens when people deny that
bisexuality is a real thing, or discriminate against bisexuals due to beliefs
like your parents seem to have. 
The unfortunate truth is that you can never
be sure of how you parents may take this kind of information. It sounds
promising that they are accepting of other sexual orientations, and it may
simply be that your parents lack education about bisexuality. With accurate
information, they may come around, but there is no way to be sure. 
Some of my colleagues have already given you good information. Here are some more things that you can consider
and questions you can ask yourself before making the decision to tell them:
 
1.      If they don’t respond the way you want them to, have you developed a support
system to support you through it? This is very important. The truth is that all
the friends in the world won’t prevent pain or sadness if your parents don’t
respond as you hope, but they will at least be there to remind you that you ARE
okay as you are. And that is critical for you to remember. 
 
2.      There is great information on the internet about bisexuality that you can
offer your parents if you think it would be helpful. It may be important for
them to know the kind of damage that is being done to bisexual adults and young
people because of the myths surrounding bisexuality. Here is an article from
GLAAD about bi-erasure: http://www.glaad.org/bisexual/bierasure
 
3.      Coming out to people is obviously deeply personal and should only take
place when you are ready. Sometimes people get pressured from friends, media,
and even people they are romantically involved with to come out before they are
ready. It really is up to you. If you are feeling pressure from others, don’t
feel bad about setting your boundaries and telling them that you will come out
on your own time. Intimate partners should respect you enough to understand
this. 
 
4.      Something else to think about is your own comfort in being bisexual. Just
because someone is LGBTQ doesn’t mean that they necessarily want to be. Many
people struggle with this. Having
your own doubts or insecurities may also play a part in how comfortable you are
in telling your parents. So, perhaps do some self-exploration about your
feelings toward yourself. 
 
Sometimes people take friends with them
when they decide to come out to their parents. This is something you may want
to consider. The important thing to remember is that you are okay. Really. If
your parents don’t respond well, that doesn’t mean that they never will. Some
start off upset, but then work their way through it. 
Best of luck to you. 
Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM:
I understand your concern, and it's important to remember that everyone's coming out process is unique. It's not uncommon for people to feel uncomfortable talking about their sexuality with unsupportive or less-than-accepting family members. However, your parents' acceptance of other sexualities but their discomfort with bisexuality could be due to a lack of understanding or education about bisexuality. It may be helpful for them to learn more about it.

If you do decide to come out to them, consider doing it when you feel ready and in a setting where you feel safe and supported. You might want to prepare them with some information about bisexuality and its uniqueness, as well as share your own experiences and feelings. It can also be helpful to have resources and support systems in place in case of a negative reaction.

Alternatively, you might choose to prioritize your mental and emotional wellbeing and decide to come out to other trusted friends or support systems first. It's important to remember that everyone's journey is personal and that it's okay to take things at your own pace. Ultimately, the decision is yours, and I encourage you to consider what feels right for you.

Context: I have been diagnosed with posttraumatic stress disorder due to my military experiences. Not a year ago, I had a car accident. Could this experience add more problems?
Response: Post Traumatic Stress Disorder can occur after any traumatic event where a real and present threat of harm or loss of life to yourself or others is present. Yes, a car accident could increase PTSD symptoms such as hyper-vigilance, anxiety, nightmares, re-playing the event, etc. Depending on the severity of the accident new triggers might exist. This is not to say that you cannot recover. Are you currently experiencing additional trauma symptoms?
LLM: Absolutely, a car accident can reactivate the symptoms of PTSD, especially if the experience was traumatic. The symptoms can include intrusive thoughts or re-experiencing the trauma through flashbacks, avoidance of reminders, negative thoughts and feelings, or changes in reactions and beliefs. I recommend seeking professional help, such as therapy, to learn coping strategies that can help you manage these symptoms. It's also important to prioritize self-care, such as regular exercise, healthy diet, and getting enough rest. Remember, it's okay to ask for help and take care of yourself.

Context: I'm currently struggling with diagnosed depression, anxiety, and Misophonia. Also, I am 99% sure I have Borderline Personality Disorder and Avoidant Personality Disorder. I read the DSM-IV, taken college psychology, etc. I know it in my heart I have them. Is self-diagnosing okay with the right research?
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. Self-diagnosing is something that we all do about one health issue or another. Taking a guess about what may or may not be happening to us is completely normal, however the actual clinical diagnoses of a mental health disorder requires a licensed mental health professional. Here are a few reasons for this: 1. Making a clinical diagnosis often requires other things besides matching up the symptoms of a disorder. To come to a diagnoses for any of the things you mentioned, a counselor would consult the DSM, but they may also use some type of screening tool, assessment or scale. Many disorders have similarities and it requires someone with training to tease out the differences in the symptoms. 2. Our judgement is sometimes off when it comes to assessing ourselves. We really can't be objective. Something as serious as determining a diagnosis really needs to be done by someone who has that objectivity. In addition, once you know your diagnosis, treating it usually requires help from a professional. 3. Sometimes the symptoms of a mental health disorder makes self-diagnoses impossible, in and of itself. For example, someone could be having hallucinations or delusions and not realize it. That would clearly make it impossible for them to diagnose themselves. There a many examples where something like this could happen with different mental health disorders. There are other reasons, but these are three important ones. Now, does this mean that you are wrong about your diagnosis? Maybe not. You may be correct. But, it is important to get this confirmed by a professional mental health clinician. If a disorder is then found, you can begin to talk about treatment. Hope this was helpful. Be well.Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPCC, NCC
LLM: While I understand that you have done extensive research and have a strong sense of your conditions, it's important to note that self-diagnosis should be approached with caution. Mental health disorders are complex and require a thorough assessment by a qualified mental health professional to ensure an accurate diagnosis. The DSM-IV, while a valuable resource, should not replace a proper evaluation. The misconceptions or missed aspects of self-diagnosis could be detrimental for your therapeutic progress. For the most accurate and effective treatment, it is crucial to work directly with a mental health professional.

Context:
I just took a job that requires me to travel far away from home. My family and I really need this job.
   People keep telling me I have "anxiety" and I'm terrified of having an anxiety attack on the road. This is all new to me. What can I do?
Response: Congratulations on your new job. Chances are, you accepted this opportunity knowing that sometimes, you feel heightened levels of anxiety while traveling (or at least, people are telling you this is true) Rather than working towards how to stop it from happening, it might make more sense to prepare for if it does: often, when we know how we might deal with a situation, the situation doesn't manifest. On a more broad scope, though, tools to minimize anxious feelings abound: there are apps (headspace or happify for example) therapy helps, and tools like yoga and meditation can help reduce anxious feelings too. Safe travels~
LLM:
I understand your concerns. Anxiety is a normal emotion, and it's common for people to experience it when facing new and challenging situations like traveling for work. Here are some suggestions that may help you manage your anxiety:

1. Prepare: Make a travel plan and pack everything you might need well in advance. This will help you feel more in control of the situation.
2. Relaxation techniques: Practicing relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or meditation can help you calm your mind and body.
3. Support system: Stay connected with your family and friends back home. Regular communication can help you feel less isolated and more supported.
4. Professional help: Consider seeking the help of a mental health professional who can work with you to develop coping strategies and manage your anxiety.
5. Physical Self-Care: Get enough rest, eat nutritious meals, and engage in regular exercise. These activities can help improve your mood and overall sense of well-being.

Remember, it's normal to feel anxiousabout new situations, but with the right tools and resources, you can manage your anxiety and focus on the job at hand.

Context: Both of my parents committed suicide together, and I was the one who found them. I suffer from overwhelming depression, which is having an extreme effect on me and my husband’s sex lives. He does not understand at all and is always making me feel so uncomfortable about sex.
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. First, I want to tell you how sorry I am for the experience you had with your parents. That is a grief and trauma that is certainly hard to imagine. Trauma and grief can affect us in many ways, and certainly deeply personal things like our sex lives. It may be difficult for others to make the connection, but it is there. So, for example, if stress and trauma make you feel like you are not interested in sex, a partner may take it as a personal rejection rather than response to stress, trauma or grief. If your husband will agree to it, couple's counseling may be your best bet. A counselor can help you with your communication and may be able to provide some education to your husband about trauma and how it can have an impact on intimacy. If he doesn't agree to go, it may be worth it to go on your own. You still have a lot you are dealing with yourself. And sometimes our personally therapy can influence others, so it may be a good idea. It is important to remember that even though you are married you have the right to make decisions about your body, and that includes when you want to have sex with it. I hope this is helpful, and some of my colleagues may have ideas, also. Be well.Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM: I'm so sorry for your loss and the difficult situation you're in right now. The trauma of discovering your parents' suicide and the grief that follows can take a toll on various aspects of your life, including your intimacy with your spouse. It's understandable that you're experiencing depression, which may impact your sex life. It's essential to remember that these feelings are not your fault, and it's crucial to have an open dialogue with your husband to help him understand your experience. Suggest that you both consider seeking professional help, such as couples therapy, to work through this challenge together. Additionally, consider focusing on self-care practices that help you cope with your feelings of sadness and trauma, like mindfulness exercises, talking with a support network, or practicing relaxation techniques.

Context: My mother takes care of niece whom my sister abandoned. She calls me every day complaining, but I don't want to hear it anymore.
Response: Both you and your mom are in a tough spot.  She has returned to parenting after she thought she had raised her kids, and while I am sure you'd like to be supportive, you recognize that listening to her complaints really does not help her and drains you.  In addition to that, it sounds like Mom's complaining has become "the" conversation she has with you, leaving other, more fulfilling conversations out of your relationship.Think about what you want instead with your mom.  More time with just her?  A chance to offer true help,not just listen to complaints? When you have some idea of what you want, tell her.  You can do this kindly and respectfully, with a concern for both her and your relationship with her.  It might go something like this..."Mom, you really have your hands full.  Sis dumped your grandbaby on you and I bet this isn't how you imagined grandparenting would look.  It's got to be hard and I am not sure how I can be helpful.  Beyond that, I really miss talking to you about other things.  So, Mom, is there something I can do that would be helpful?  If not, let's talk about something else".In addition to that, if your mom is open to other resources, help her find them - she needs some emotional support from a non-family member.Good luck to you both - and to your niece.  It is a hard situation for you all.
LLM:
I understand that your mother's complaints about taking care of your niece are causing you emotional distress. It's essential to remember that supporting family members can be challenging, but it's also an act of love and care. However, if these conversations are causing you significant stress, it's essential to set healthy boundaries.

You might want to try the following strategies:

1. Be empathetic but firm: Let your mother know how much you understand and appreciate her efforts, but at the same time, communicate that you can't offer further assistance or engage in lengthy conversations about the situation.
2. Offer alternative solutions: Suggest resources or professional help that may alleviate some of your mother's burdens.
3. Take care of yourself: Prioritize your own mental and emotional well-being by engaging in self-care activities, seeking support from friends, or talking with a therapist.
4. Set clear limits: Be explicit about the topics you're willing to discuss and those you'd rather avoid.
5. If necessary, disengage: Sometimes limiting contact with a situation or person can be the best course of action for your overall health and happiness.

It may take some time and practice to establish these new communication patterns, but over time, they will contribute to healthier relationships and greater emotional well-being for everyone involved.

Context:
I've been with my partner for 4 years. She's given me no reason not to trust her. But lately I've been overreacting a lot when she's just doing normal things. I think it's triggering my bad past relationships.

How can I get rid of these insecurities and be more trusting with her?
Response: What's most important is that you recognize that what you've been doing is off base - that what you've been feeling is "overreacting," that you have "insecurities," and you suspect your actions and reactions are connected to your own past and not to the current situation or to your partner. That's an excellent first step because without recognizing these things about yourself, it's unlikely that you would change.That said, you sound as if you are really ready to start some therapy to work on your own personal growth... not because there is something "wrong" with you, but because you're ready to learn more about yourself and make some creative changes in your behavior. Yes, there are lots of self-help books and courses, but we humans often make the most progress when we have support in  real-time from another human being who can sit with us, point out our blind spots, and teach us some skills that are hard to learn on our own.
LLM:
It's common for past experiences to affect our current relationships, and your reaction towards your partner's normal behavior may be a manifestation of those past insecurities. Here are some suggestions that may help you build trust and improve communication with your partner:

1. Self-reflection: It's essential to identify the root cause of your insecurities and understand how they are influencing your behavior. Try to reflect on your past relationships and the dynamics that have caused you to feel insecure.
2. Open communication: Share your concerns with your partner, explain how past experiences have influenced your behavior and how it makes you feel. Remember, honesty and transparency are essential to building a strong foundation in any relationship.
3. Practice forgiveness: Holding grudges from the past can make it challenging to trust in the present. Try to practice forgiveness by letting go of past hurts and focusing on the present moment.
4. Set realistic expectations: It's important to remember that no one is perfect, and we all make mistakes. Set realistic expectations for yourself and your partner and focus on the good things that each other brings into your life.
5. Practice relaxation techniques: When you notice yourself reacting negatively to your partner's normal behavior, try to practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation to help manage your emotions.
6. seek professional help: If your insecurities are significantly impacting your daily life and relationships, consider seeking professional help from a mental health professional who can provide you with coping strategies and help you work through your past experiences.

Context: My wife just last week said she wants a divorce and it's all sudden. I understand that marriages have their ups and downs but I don't understand why she can't tell me why she wants a divorce. Should I fight for my family (daughter and wife)?
Response: Chances are, if you really think about it, there were signs that something wasn’t quite right in your marriage. You may have passed these things off as being the normal ups and downs of a marriage, but whatever it was (or wasn't), it's obviously bothering your wife. I would say yes, keep trying to fight for your family, at least until you know what the problems are from your wife's perspective and if they're repairable. Ask your wife if she’s willing to try to work on the marriage and then see a marriage counselor. Just be sure you’re open to hearing what's said and try not to get defensive if she blames you for some things.Even if your wife is adamant about getting a divorce, that doesn't mean you’re divorcing your daughter and you should make sure she knows that. Continue to be a part of her life. And although it may be difficult, if a divorce does happen, try to be as amicable as possible with your wife so your daughter isn't further impacted by this new family dynamic.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear about the sudden turn of events in your marriage, but I can't provide you with definitive advice without knowing more about the specific circumstances of your situation. It's not uncommon for individuals going through a divorce to feel hurt, confused, and uncertain about their future.

Your wife's unwillingness to discuss her reasons for wanting a divorce may be a protective measure, a reflection of her emotional state, or a tactic to avoid an confrontation. Regardless of her reasons, it's essential that you focus on what you can control in the situation.

It's important to keep in mind that the outcome of a divorce is influenced by many factors beyond your control, such as your wife's emotions, the legal proceedings, and the dynamics of your relationship. Rather than trying to fight for your family, consider focusing on your own emotional wellbeing and taking care of yourself during this difficult time.

It's also important to remember that a divorce doesn't necessarily mean that you and your wife will be completely separated from one another. You can still maintain a relationship with your wife and your daughter, and continuing to communicate openly with your wife about your feelings and concerns can help you better understand her perspective and potentially even work towards finding a resolution or compromise.

If you're finding it difficult to cope with the emotions and challenges of the situation, you may find it helpful to speak with a therapist or counselor to gain additional support and insights as you navigate this process.

Context: This is preventing me from getting a job. I have bad relationship with my bipolar mother. I am living with her but it stresses me out so much to be around her that I have locked myself in my room. I have no friends or other family to help me. I have no money for therapy, but I think I need therapy before I can secure a job.
Response: Overwhelming fear, social anxiety and depression are especially painful because they make behaving constructively a challenge.  But there are also lots of examples of folks who have successfully worked their way through this.  So there is hope!   In addition to the suggestion to visit your doctor (who may prescribe a medication to help you relax), there are many social service agencies who provide free counseling or take medicaid.  A good CBT therapist will help you look at your thinking so that you may see how you are distorting your thoughts.  Behavior is also important.  Despite how you may feel: it is helpful to go out, to do things that relax you (maybe a yoga class or a meditation class?).  Exercise. Diet. Eat well. Think back on the things that you used to do that helped in the past, and force yourself to do these things again.  I know that this seems hard, but it beats the alternative, doesn't it?  Of course, I recommend you pick up a copy of my book, Living Yes, which will help you look at these and other ideas to help you out.  I hope you get unstuck soon!  ~Mark (www.MarkMorrisLCSW.com and www.LivingYes.org)
LLM:
I understand that the stress of living with your bipolar mother is significantly impacting your life, making it difficult for you to focus on securing a job. This is a common situation for those dealing with family dynamics and mental health challenges.

Firstly, I recommend reaching out to social service organizations or non-profits in your area to inquire about free or low-cost therapeutic services. You may also consider seeking support groups for adults with parents who have bipolar disorder. These resources can provide valuable emotional and practical assistance in managing your situation.

Regarding jobs, consider looking for opportunities that offer a flexible schedule and a supportive work environment. Some jobs, like telecommuting or freelancing, can provide more autonomy, allowing you to manage your work hours around your personal circumstances. Additionally, reaching out to Vocational Rehabilitation Services or Disability Services at your local employment office may be beneficial, as you might qualify for extra support due to your mother's condition affecting your mental health and ability to secure employment.

If getting therapy or finding a job seems overwhelming, I encourage breaking it down into smaller steps, tackling each challenge one at a time. Remember, making progress may not always be a straight line and that's okay. The journey to improving one's mental health and securing employment often requires patience, persistence, and support.

Lastly, remember to practice self-care, eat healthy, engage in physical activity, and make time for relaxation. These things can make a significant difference in how you cope with daily stressors and build resilience.

Context: She's my stepmom. I'm pretty sure she's illegally buying the drugs and hiding them from my dad.
Response: I would suggest having a conversation with your dad and then having a conversation as a family with your stepmom.  Usually there are other signs of use that can often be missed.  Be aware of behavior changes and mood swings.  Be supportive and ready to listen often times individuals suffering from addiction will deny use until they are ready for help.
LLM: I understand your concern, but as a psychologist, I can't diagnose or make judgments about people based on hearsay. However, if you believe there's a possibility of substance abuse in your household, it's important to prioritize your safety and wellbeing. You may consider talking to a trusted adult, such as a teacher, relative, or even a therapist. They can help you navigate the situation and provide you with resources if needed. Additionally, anonymous hotlines are available in many areas to report such concerns. Remember that it's crucial not to confront your stepmom yourself, as it could potentially put you in an unsafe situation.

Context:
I'm planning to have baby, so I have to quit smoking - but it's hard. Sometimes it's not a physical need, it's mental.
   I cannot help myself from thinking about smoking. What can I do to get rid of this addiction?
Response: Hi. Good for you in planning ahead to do what's healthiest for your baby (and yourself). That's a great first step! It's also good that you are able to identify that it's not always a physical need that's driving the addiction.For the next steps, I would suggest trying to figure out when the psychological cravings for a cigarette occur. The psychological (or mental) cravings are usually based out of habit, such as having a cigarette after a meal. And if you're consciously trying to quit, you'll find the craving starts with simply thinking about having a cigarette, then usually moves on to thinking about how good it made you feel, etc., etc. Well, if I'm on a diet and I continue to let myself think about the ice cream sitting in the freezer, eventually I'll give in and eat it.You're going to have thoughts about smoking a cigarette. That's normal and, for the most part, out of your control. But you choose whether or not to CONTINUE thinking and dwelling about it after that initial thought. That's what you would have to work on changing. When you have that initial thought, acknowledge it ("Ok, I kind of want a cigarette now."), but then change the thoughts that typically follow. Distract yourself, think about something else, do something else, whatever it takes to get your mind off of that cigarette.I've suggested to clients before that they should plan these scenarios out ahead of time so they already know what they're going to do when the time comes. Write down when you usually have the craving for a cigarette and then write down new thoughts or things to do to get your mind off of it. Eventually, it will become easier and easier to brush off that initial thought until you no longer have it.Best of luck, and you have a really great motivator to quit - your baby!
LLM:
I understand that quitting smoking, especially during pregnancy, is an important decision for your health and your baby's health. It's common to experience mental and emotional challenges when trying to quit, as nicotine is a highly addictive substance. Here are some strategies that might help:

1. Make a plan: Set a quit date, and get prepared. Identify triggers that make you crave cigarettes, and plan for ways to avoid or manage them.

2. Speak with your healthcare provider: They can offer guidance, resources, and support throughout your pregnancy and beyond. Consider enrolling in a smoking cessation program or seeking counseling to help manage the mental and emotional aspects of quitting.

3. Practice stress management techniques: Find healthy ways to cope with stress, such as deep breathing, meditation, or prenatal yoga.

4. Reward yourself: Set achievable goals, like going a day, a week, or a month without smoking, and reward yourself when you reach each milestone.

5. Lean on your support system: Share your struggles and accomplishments with friends and family, and seek their encouragement and understanding.

6. Avoid triggers: Stay away from places, people, or situations that make you crave cigarettes.

7. Use nicotine replacement therapy (NRT): Consult your healthcare provider about using NRT, such as patches, gum, or lozenges to help manage cravings.

8. Focus on your motivation: Think about the reasons why you want to quit. Keep this motivation at the forefront when you feel tempted to smoke.

9. Be patient with yourself: Quitting smoking is a challenging process, and it's important to remember that setbacks and slip-ups are common. Try not to get discouraged and instead pick yourself up and keep moving forward.

10. Stay positive and believe in yourself: It can be helpful to visualize yourself as a non-smoker, confident in your ability to overcome the addiction and build a healthier life for yourself and your baby.

Context: As a child, my parents injured my brother, so they went to prison. I was there when he had gotten hurt, but I can't seem to remember. I also can't remember being with them quite well. It's a blur.
Response: It's important to know that there are counselors who are trained in working with survivors of trauma and you can get help.  The "blur" in your memories is your brain's attempt to protect you from the experiences in your past.  To recover them can be a slow process done by a trained professional that you trust.  When you are ready and feel safe, those memories can be recovered and can be integrated with other memories.  This will help to lessen the potency of the memory, help to make sense of what has happened, and allow you to begin the healing process.  Check into therapist who are trained in EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing).
LLM: I understand that the memories of those events from your childhood are troubling for you. It's not uncommon for traumatic experiences to be repressed or for memories to be hazy, especially when one is young and still developing the ability to process such complex emotions and situations. This phenomenon is known as dissociation and is a common coping mechanism for dealing with trauma. You may find it helpful to explore your feelings about these memories with a therapist, who can offer you a safe and confidential space to process your emotions and gain a better understanding of how these experiences have affected you. Remember that it's important to be kind to yourself and acknowledge that you're doing the best you can with the resources you have.

Context:
I've been with my boyfriend over 3 years. He cheated on me and the woman is now pregnant. He is physically and mentally abusive towards me. I have attempted to talk with him in order to resolve our issues; however, he never wants to talk and often blames me for everything.
I’m always there when he needs my help. He tells me although I help him doesn’t mean that I am a good person. He always discredits me as a woman and it’s hurtful. I spend most day crying and feeling lonely. I am unhappy and unsure of what I need to do.
Response: That sounds like a very hurtful situation. Unfortunately, without a batterers intervention course or a desire to change, the abuse cycle is unlikely to stop. I do not recommend that you try to leave on your own due that being the most dangerous time when in an abusive relationship. As an abuser, his goal is to control you whether it's through mental or verbal means. I recommend that you contact a domestic violence shelter in your area. I have worked closely with Harbor House of Central Florida and know they provide housing, transportation, and any other needed resources to women leaving an abusive relationship. There is help out there and you are not alone. If you need any additional resources in your area, please don't hesitate to contact me.
LLM: I'm sorry to hear about the pain and suffering you have been experiencing in your relationship. It sounds like you're in an abusive and unhealthy situation. I encourage you to prioritize your own well-being and consider seeking professional help as you navigate through this difficult time. This may include speaking with a mental health professional, such as a therapist or counselor, who can provide you with support, guidance, and coping skills to help you manage the emotions you're feeling and work through the complicated situation you find yourself in. You may also want to consider reaching out to local support groups for people who have experienced or are currently in abusive relationships. Remember, you deserve to be treated with respect, kindness, and love. Don't allow yourself to continue to be in a situation where you are repeatedly being hurt and diminished. I'm here for you and want to help however I can. I strongly urge you to consider reaching out to local domestic violence helplines or hotlines for further guidance and support. Additionally, seeking resources from national organizations, such as the National Domestic Violence Hotline (1-800-799-7233) in the United States, can be incredibly helpful. I believe in your strength and ability to find a way forward, and I will continue to be here for you as a source of support and guidance.

Context: I am a peaceful person, but I often find myself having thoughts about killing people. Eventhough I like the thoughts at the time, they make me cry. Why do I feel like this?
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. Clearly this is something that has you very concerned. Sometimes thoughts are just thoughts, and they are pretty harmless. Many people have thoughts of killing someone, but they would never actually do it. They really don't even want to do it, they are just having a thought. Other times, thoughts are a sign of something more serious, and we should receive help when they start to worry us. I would recommend meeting with a counselor to discuss these thoughts. It is usually a safe environment where you can get advice with little judgement. Now, If you begin to start thinking about killing someone specific and making a plan to do it in reality- you should call for help immediately. By help, I mean 9-11, or a family member or friend. You would want to get help before doing something to harm yourself and others. Again, none of this is to judge, but rather to encourage you to get the help you're seeking. Either way, when we become concerned about some aspect of our behavior, seeking counseling is usually a good choice. Be well, Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPCC, NCC
LLM: I understand that this is a difficult topic to discuss, but it's important that we talk about it. These thoughts you're having are not an uncommon experience, and they don't necessarily mean that you're a harmful person. It's possible that these thoughts are a manifestation of deep-seated emotional pain or discomfort. They may be related to various underlying emotional issues, such as anger, frustration, or even a feeling of powerlessness. It's also important to note that these thoughts don't necessarily equate to action. In fact, most people who have such thoughts do not act on them. I would encourage you to speak with a mental health professional about this, who can help you explore the possible causes and provide you with appropriate resources and coping strategies. Remember that it's okay to ask for help and support when you need it.

Context: I am really worried about one of my friends because I think he has major depression. He disagrees with me on that. He is shut off when it comes to talking to people and telling them how he really feels. He told me he feels empty inside and the only emotions he feels are anger and sadness. I suggested to him to get help and talk to his mom about it but he refuses.
Response: First of all, I can tell that you really care about your friend and I think it's great that you are reaching out with your concern. It's hard to determine whether your friend would meet the criteria for an official diagnosis of depression without working with him, however, whether he does or not, therapy may be beneficial for him in working through these difficult feelings and relational challenges. Unfortunately, you can't make your friend get help. He will ultimately need to make that decision for himself, however, you can talk to him about your concerns and your hopes that he will reach out for help.
LLM:
I understand your concern for your friend's wellbeing. Major depression is a serious mental health condition, and it can be difficult for someone experiencing it to recognize and accept the diagnosis on their own. The symptoms you've described, such as feeling empty, avoiding social interactions, and only experiencing anger and sadness, are consistent with depression. However, it's important to remember that ultimately, your friend has to make the decision to seek help.

As a friend, you can continue to be supportive by encouraging him to take small steps, such as going for a walk together or engaging in an activity they enjoy. You can also offer to go with him to a doctor's appointment or help him research different treatment options. It may also be helpful to educate him about depression and the benefits of seeking help.

However, it's essential to respect his decision and not force the issue if he's not ready. Mental health treatment is a highly personal and sometimes complex process, and people often need to come to the decision to seek help in their own time and in their own way.

If you're concerned about your friend's safety, you should consider reaching out to a mental health professional or crisis hotline for guidance. They can provide you with resources and support in navigating this situation.

Context: My toddler defies everything I say and doesn’t see me as authoritative, so she says no about everything. I'd like to hear some ways I can work on not needing to feel so much in control all the time.
Response: Trust that you are a good mother and that you love your child.Trust and love are their own authority and come from a different inner place than the urge to be obeyed.Trust and love are effective guidance and usually feel happier and lighter too!
LLM:
I understand that it can be frustrating when your toddler defies your instructions and challenges your authority. This behavior is normal for toddlers as they are exploring their independence and asserting their will. However, it's important to find a balance between setting boundaries and allowing your child to express themselves.

Here are some ways to work on not needing to feel so much in control all the time:

1. Use positive reinforcement: Instead of focusing on what your toddler is not doing, try praising her for the things she is doing right. This will encourage good behavior and help your child feel more positive about following your instructions.
2. Offer choices: Give your toddler the feeling of control by offering her choices in situations where it is appropriate. For example, you could offer her two different healthy snack options to choose from.
3. Use calm and consistent responses: Be consistent in your responses to your toddler's behavior and keep your responses calm and not reactive. This will help your child learn to understand the consequences of their actions.
4. Model positive behavior: Show your child positive behaviors and ways to express themselves through your own actions. For example, use "I" statements instead of "you" statements when communicating with your child.
5. Use distraction techniques: Sometimes, a toddler might be fixated on something that is not good for them or not following your instructions. In such cases, trying distracting them with something else can be helpful.

Remember, every child is unique and develops at their own pace. Keep in mind that challenging behavior is a normal part of development, and with patience, consistency, and positive reinforcement, your child will learn to follow your instructions more often.

If you have any further questions or need additional support, please let me know.

Context: What are the basic skills a good counselor needs to have?
Response: Here is a list of skills I use every session, and a short explanation of what they are:Reflecting: Paraphrasing what clients have said so that they know you understand themValidating: Helping clients to know that their point of view is valuable and rationalClient-centered talk: Maintaining focus on the client in therapy sessions, rather than a therapist talking about themselvesAttending: Nonverbal or verbal cues that indicate I am listening to a client (i.e., nodding, leaning in, affirmations)Reframing: Helping a client to see a problematic behavior or situation in a different way (usually only works well after they feel heard and understood)
LLM: A good counselor should have strong communication skills, active listening abilities, empathy, compassion, and cultural competence. They should also be knowledgeable in various therapeutic approaches and able to create a safe and trusting environment for their clients to share their experiences. Additionally, a counselor should be able to maintain professional boundaries and be committed to their own personal and professional growth.

Context: I get angry and act out. It scares my wife and child.
Response: Yes you can..  If you cannot control your temper, who can?  You have more power of your emotions, thoughts and feeling than you are giving yourself credit for.  If this is scaring your wife and child, you need to get control of it immediately.  Try going to an anger management class.  It will help to discover what is the root cause of your anger, what triggers it and how you can control/manage it.  If you can't go to a class, try self help books or even better yet counseling and support of a professional counselor.
LLM: I understand that you're feeling overwhelmed and that your anger is causing distress not only for yourself but also for your loved ones. This is a concern that we can work on in therapy. Anger can be a natural emotion, but when it becomes unmanageable, it can lead to harm. We can explore the underlying causes of your anger, learn healthy ways to manage emotions, and develop effective communication skills. Let's work together to find a solution that will benefit everyone in your family.

Context: It happens especially at me and my sister, and then she gets emotional and brings up the past. How should I deal with it? She is always stressed about her work and doesn't put her family first.
Response: You are very wise for a young person. You have already figured out that other people's behaviours...how they treat you...it's not about you...it's about them. I love that you don't blame yourself for your mom's behaviours. It's not your fault. She's getting upset because she doesn't know how to manage her emotions, and these emotions have to do with her past and her present stress. You're just the trigger. Yes, this is normal, but it's not necessary. She can find another way to manage her "stuff".Unfortunately, you can't help your mom a whole lot or even help her recognize this. But for yourself... remembering that her behaviours are her issue is the biggest piece of "dealing with it". You can always try some new strategies when you talk to mom...you can say "I'll listen you better if you don't bring up past stuff", or "I'm worried about you mom. You seem stressed", or even "I don't like the way you talk to me". Good luck!
LLM:
I understand that it can be challenging when your sister brings up past issues, especially when you both are trying to deal with current stressors. Here are some suggestions:

1. Stay Calm: Try to remain calm and avoid getting defensive or escalating the situation.
2. Active Listening: Give her your full attention, validate her feelings, and show empathy towards her emotions.
3. Set Boundaries: It is essential to communicate your concerns and set clear boundaries about what you are comfortable discussing.
4. Offer Solutions: Instead of focusing on the past, try to offer practical solutions to current problems or issues.
5. Encourage Self-Care: Remind her of the importance of self-care and encourage her to prioritize her mental and emotional wellbeing.
6. Seek Professional Help: If her stress and emotional outbursts become unmanageable, it may be helpful for her to seek professional help, such as therapy or counseling.
7. Practice Forgiveness and Compassion: Holding onto past grievances can add more stress to your life. Practice forgiveness and compassion towards yourself and your sister.

Remember, as her sibling, you cannot control her emotions or actions, but you can control how you respond to them. It may be helpful to also seek support for yourself from friends, family, or a mental health professional if needed.

Context: I stopped for a while, but I’ve started doing it again. It’s like an addiction. The more I try to stop, the more I want to do it. I’ve tried doing some of the stuff they say to do like draw, but it doesn’t work.
Response: I've heard people say that they cut themselves as a way to feel relief from different emotions. You've mentioned that you've tried some coping strategies, such as drawing, but they haven't been effective. It's essential to understand that what works for one person may not work for another, and it's crucial to explore different approaches to managing your behaviors.

One effective approach is to identify and understand the triggers that lead you to engage in the repetitive behavior. Once you're aware of the triggers, you can develop strategies to manage or avoid them. It's also essential to find healthy ways to cope with the emotional or psychological needs that may be driving the behavior.

Consider seeking the help of a professional, such as a psychologist or therapist, who can work with you to develop an individualized treatment plan. This may include cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness practices, or other evidence-based approaches to managing repetitive behaviors. The key is to find a therapeutic relationship that feels right for you and to be patient with the process of change. Change takes time, but with commitment, you can make progress towards breaking free from this pattern.

Context: What makes a healthy marriage last?
Response: What a great question! Marriage (or any long-term committed romantic relationship) is never easy. Ups & downs, doubts and fights are a part of it just the same as the wonderful, tender, blissful moments of connection. Recognizing that hardships are normal and something to work through is a big part of the healthy attitude partners in lasting relationships share. Some degree of conflict is not something to be scared of or a sign it's time to break up as long as all parties involved feel respected. Expressing your needs and wants in a respectful, not accusing manner will increase the chances that your loved one will actually listen, empathize with your perspective and will be more open to finding solutions to your problems together. Working as a team to find solutions to problems you encounter is a much better approach than trying to persuade your partner that you are right, while they are most certainly wrong. Shutting your partner out or allowing resentment and contempt to creep into your relationships is definitely a warning sign that things may not be going the way you want anymore. As long as you both agree that you want to be in that relationship (i.e., are committed to it) and you have a growth mindset ("we will learn from this and grow together to be a better couple in the end"), the foundation of your partnership is strong. What else? Making time for each other, whether it's being playful, having fun or enjoying intimate moments together is also an important part of ensuring a good balance of positive to negative experiences, that will only strenghten your bond. Good luck!
LLM: A healthy marriage is built on mutual respect, effective communication, emotional intimacy, equality in the partnership, and a strong commitment to the relationship. Factors like shared values, conflict resolution skills, and the ability to support each other through challenges also contribute to a long-lasting marriage. It's important to remember that every relationship is unique, and the specifics may vary from couple to couple. Regularly reassessing and working on these aspects can help strengthen and maintain the bond between two individuals.

Context: I suffer from checking and cleaning OCD. Can counseling help me to get over it?
Response: Counseling can be very effective for OCD. In particularly, a kind of therapy called Exposure Response (Ritual) Prevention Therapy is considered the 'gold standard' for the treatment of OCD. What this is, is a specific protocol that helps you build relaxation strategies and to increase the amount of time between your checking/cleaning rituals while also helping you to face any of the fears that OCD given you until those rituals and fears are no longer interfering with your life. Research has shown that this kind of therapy may even be more effective for OCD than medication, https://ajp.psychiatryonline.org/doi/full/10.1176/appi.ajp.162.1.151
LLM: Absolutely, patient. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is an effective treatment for OCD, including checking and cleaning compulsions. In therapy, you'll learn strategies to manage the anxiety, challenge unhelpful thoughts, and gradually reduce the compulsive behaviors. Additionally, medication, particularly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), can help in managing OCD symptoms. However, remember that overcoming OCD takes time and effort. Be patient with yourself, and consider seeking professional help for the best outcomes.

Context: I've gone to a couple therapy sessions so far and still everytime I walk in I get nervous and shaky. Is this normal? Should I still be feeling like this?
Response: Thank you for your question. It's completely normal and natural to feel nervous before a therapy session. Many people report having felt this way. I can't say enough regarding the amount of courage it takes to reach out and attend a therapy session. For many people, it can take weeks, months, or even years of contemplating whether to see a therapist or not before actually contacting one and attending the first session. It takes courage to want to work through any struggles you may be having or personal growth you aspire toward. In your question, you mentioned that you've "gone several times and are still feeling nervous and shaky." If you feel comfortable with your therapist and it's a good fit for you, I would suggest talking about this with your therapist. You don't have to struggle each time you have your appointment. Together, you and your therapist can work toward helping you have a different experience.
LLM: It is normal to feel some anxiety or nervousness before going to therapy sessions, especially if you are dealing with uncomfortable emotions or discussing personal issues. This feeling may be a result of the unfamiliarity of the situation or the anticipation of the therapy process. However, if your anxiety is so severe that it prevents you from attending sessions regularly or participating fully in the therapy process, it may be useful to discuss this with your therapist, as there may be strategies or interventions that can help you manage your anxiety. Additionally, if you find that your nervousness persists even after some time in therapy and you're not seeing any improvement in your mental health, it might be worth considering whether the therapeutic relationship is right for you, and exploring other therapeutic approaches or providers. Remember, it takes time and effort to build trust and make progress in therapy, so be gentle with yourself and remember that it's okay to feel some discomfort along the way.

Context: I think I'm annoying and bothersome, but my girlfriend says I'm just fine. I don't think so, I think I'm stressing everyone else out
Response: Interesting:  How much of your issue is your own self-judging and how much of your perception is real?There are many ways to stop judging and self-judging using Cognitive Theory (CBT).  You could find a (CBT) counselor/ therapist to help you reduce your judgments.  In addition, I've written extensively about this in my book, LIVING YES, A HANDBOOK FOR BEING HUMAN (www.LivingYes.org).  I recommend you visit the webpage and pick up a copy.As to your own perception:  You seem okay being who you are, and your girlfriend agrees.  Instead, you appear to be upset by your own guess at what others may be thinking. That's a trap that stresses a lot of folks out.  Consider this:  Many happy people don't mind what others think of them.  And consider this:  Many heroic characters in literature and movies are unlikeable.  ("Colombo" is annoying and bothersome, but we love him.  So is Johnny's Depp's Jack Sparrow and many of the roles played by George Clooney, Sandra Bullock, and Jack Nicholson.)  Would it be okay with you to be an "antihero" in your own life?  If you don't want to be an antihero, you have the power to be the person you want.  Your behaviors and thoughts are completely under your control.  What other people think of you is not under your control.  You are completely in the driver's seat here.  The choices are all yours.  Enjoy who you are, and use your will power to make yourself who you want to be!  ~Mark  (www.MarkMorrisLCSW.com and www.LivingYes.org)
LLM:
It's important to understand that our perceptions of ourselves and how others perceive us can sometimes be different. You mentioned that you feel annoying and bothersome, but your girlfriend sees things differently. However, your feelings are valid and it's not uncommon for people to have this kind of self-doubt.

I would suggest addressing these feelings by exploring the root cause behind them. For instance, are you feeling this way due to past experiences or are there specific situations where you feel this way? By identifying the sources of these feelings, we can work towards finding coping strategies or solutions.

It's also essential to communicate openly and honestly with your girlfriend about how you feel, as this can help her better understand your perspective and provide support. You might consider seeking the assistance of a mental health professional to further explore these emotions and develop effective ways of managing them.

Context:
I have lately been having lots of anxiety and self-loathing about the fact that I am a young adult virgin girl who has never had a boyfriend before. It seems like everyone my age has already had boyfriends by now or are not virgins anymore, and I just had my first kiss five months ago. I’m worried that, at this rate, I won’t have a boyfriend for a very long time. The problem is that I want to lose my virginity to my first boyfriend who cares about me, but at the same time, I don't want to be waiting forever in order to experience sex. I have already given in to bad temptations by hooking up with random strangers on social media sites and having oral sex with them. Luckily, they were nice guys, but none of them wanted a relationship with me. I feel dirty by doing this, but I feel pressured to do this things in order to seem normal.
   Most people are surprised when they find out that I am a virgin or never had a boyfriend because people think I am really good looking. I am tall, I play lots of sports, and I get excellent grades in school. I am in my first year of university right now, and no guys have approached me to go out on a date or showed any interest. It bothers me. Most of the people in my family have been in relationships at my age already. I feel like I will be single forever sometimes.
Response: I think that every person who has been single for any extended period of time has felt the same worries - myself included. Since relationships are so important to us we fear not having them and being single. It is similar to fearing that we won't ever get a good job when we have been out of work for awhile or fearing that we won't be healthy when we have been sick. We fear these things because they are so important - not because these fears are realistic or true! There is immense pressure put on young single people from peers and media to conform to ideas of what is normal and to meet expectations in terms of sexual experience, dating, etc. While some people may judge an adult who is a virgin - others will respect your choices and feel even more attracted to you because of your choices. Experimenting with sex, so long as you are being safe and respectful is also completely okay and normal. Since expectations vary and we can't be happy if we are always comparing ourselves to other people, we need to ultimately trust that being who we are and building a loving relationship with ourselves is the key to meeting someone who will cherish us for all the ways that we are special and unique, virgin or not. When we are able to really accept ourselves and trust in our loveableness (not a real word but you get what I mean!) then meeting someone special is simply a matter of time and effort - putting ourselves out in the world in situations where we are likely to meet other single people looking for relationships. When you are most afraid of being alone - remember that this fear is simply a reminder of how much you value relationships. Use that passion about wanting relationships to build the best relationship you can with yourself and then one day soon some lucky person will stumble into your life and benefit immensely from your passion and your ability to be authentically who you are rather than pretending to be "normal" or whatever that is anyway. Hope this helps!
LLM:
I understand that you are experiencing a great deal of anxiety and discomfort around your virginity and your desire to have a romantic relationship and lose your virginity to a caring and loving partner. It's important to remember that everyone has their own timeline when it comes to relationships and sexual experiences. The societal pressure and expectations you mention can be overwhelming, but it's crucial to remember that these expectations do not define your worth or value as a person.

It's normal to feel pressured and curious about exploring your sexuality, but it's essential to prioritize your safety and emotional well-being. Engaging in casual sexual encounters without the emotional connection and intimacy of a caring relationship can feel empty and unsatisfying in the long run, and it's possible that these experiences may contribute to feelings of shame or guilt.

Instead, it might be helpful to focus on building meaningful relationships with people in your community, both personally and academically. Consider joining clubs or organizations that align with your interests, participating in social activities, and engaging in positive self-care practices, such as regular exercise, a healthy diet, and mindfulness meditation.

Remember, finding the right partner takes time, and it's essential to take things at your own pace. In the meantime, it's important to prioritize developing a positive relationship with yourself, focusing on your strengths and accomplishments, and enjoying the journey of growing and learning as an individual. If you find that your anxiety and self-doubt continue to impact your daily life, it might be helpful to consider seeking support from a trusted mental health professional.

Context: My son stole my debit card and lied about it. It's not the first time he has lied. I don't know what to do anymore. I don't know if I should punish him or make him do something. I've tried talking to him and asking if anything was wrong. I have grounded him, but nothing works. What should I do?
Response: A lot depends on the age of your child, but given that it was a mis-used debit card, I am going to guess he's a teen?  Assuming that, there are a couple of important things to keep in mind...1.  That he lied about the misuse indicates he knows he was in the wrong.  That's a dreadful feeling - getting caught out- and an impulsive lie may have popped out of his mouth before he thought it through (after all a debit spend is pretty easy to track).  2. Once he lied he stuck with the lie.    Somewhere along the line, even though he knew that you knew he wasn't telling the truth, he stuck with his story.  And as you said, it's not the first time he lied.  Now it looks like a habit has formed.Going forward, when there is a dishonest act of any kind, it's essential that you set it up so your son can be truthful.  Simply put, do not ask questions.  If you know for certain that he has, for example, misused your debit card, say so.  Asking "did you" invites him to get creative with denial; asking "why did you" implies that, with a good enough reason, he might not be in hot water with you.  Instead, say what is:  "I see this charge on my debit card and realize you used it without my permission".  (You can only do this if you know for sure it was him but it sounds like you do).  If he meets with denial, stay calm, restate that you are aware this happened.  Then deliver consequences that match the transgression (he has to return the item if it's returnable or find a way to pay you back for the charge as well as any fees incurred in using it).  Make it harder to find your card because he isn't that great at curbing the impulse to use it yet.And most importantly, once the consequence is delivered you have to let it go.  Continuing to bring it up won't benefit him, but it does have the potential to make him more sneaky.  Every time he asks respectfully to use it, whether you allow him to or not, be sure to praise him for asking nicely.  "Catch" him doing it right as often as possible.Hang in there - this isn't super unusual behavior but it isn't acceptable, so address it but don't let yourself get too wound up about it.
LLM:
I understand that it can be frustrating and concerning when your son lies to you and takes actions that disrespect your boundaries. The first thing I would suggest is maintaining an open line of communication. It's important to express your feelings to him in a calm and respectful manner. This means using "I" statements instead of "you" statements and avoiding accusations or blame. Try to listen attentively to his perspective and validate his emotions, even if you don't agree with his actions.

Additionally, you could consider setting clear consequences for his behavior. This could include a combination of natural consequences (like losing privileges or missing out on opportunities), as well as consequences that you impose (like grounding him or requiring him to make amends). It's essential to follow through on these consequences consistently and fairly.

You could also explore other approaches, such as seeking professional help if this behavior is particularly persistent or severe. These could be in the form of family therapy or individual therapy for your son. It's important to understand that raising children can be a challenging process, and it's not uncommon for parents to feel frustrated and uncertain about how to respond to their children's behavior. If you're feeling particularly overwhelmed, it may be helpful to seek support from friends, family, or mental health professionals.

Context:
I'm in my late teens and live with my dad.  The only time I go out is for my college classes. Sometimes when I see my friends I want to talk with them, but sometimes I won't want to talk to them for days or even weeks. 

Sometimes I feel i'm not worth knowing or i'm never going to do anything right.

Are they right, am I depressed?
Response: The answer to the question of whether or not you're depressed, is less meaningful than what you are doing to feel strong and secure in your life.Otherwise, people shortcut themselves to go deeper in understanding who they are and what they'd like to do with their lives, and accept "depression" as though it is their new name.What you write sounds like classic depression.What to do about this feeling is to get closer to yourself in an accepting way.  Assume that what you do in daily living is somehow necessary.  Unless you are doing immoral or illegal actions to other people, to accept your own wishes as truth will open you to appreciate why you do what you do.Make believe you're interesting and find out more about who you are, if you have trouble to start with thinking of yourself as worth understanding.The more you appreciate who you are and your unique strengths, vulnerabilities and fears, the more these will dissolve by what seems on their own.In fact they will disappear bc you are believing in yourself and your ways.Good luck!

However, your description of wanting to avoid social interactions, feelings of low self-worth, and prolonged periods of not wanting to engage with friends are commonly observed in individuals who experience depression.

It's important to remember that seeking help is a sign of strength, not a weakness. If these feelings persist, consider reaching out to a mental health professional for a clinical evaluation. They can help determine if depression or another mental health condition is impacting your life, and they can work with you to develop strategies to improve your emotional well-being. You don't have to go through this alone.

Context: My fiancé and I recently had an argument over finances. He gives me the money each month to pay his automobile insurance. He no longer has one of the vehicles on the policy. He realized he was still paying for the coverage and asked why I was allowing him to continue to do that since I was physically paying it for him. My response was that he was giving me the money, and I just made the payment. I didn't think of the coverages. He was angry about it. I still can't figure out that anger.
Response: I'd be more worried generally about a fiancee who assumes you are responsible for tracking what in fact are his responsibilities, than about the anger per se.What you describe holds the seeds of classic abusive behavior, which is blaming the partner for what in fact are the person's own responsibilities.Start by withdrawing yourself from paying his bills.If he is on his own in terms of tracking what and when is due regarding his auto policy, then surely the only one for him to blame will be himself.Stay clear of involving yourself in doing favors for him if the favor involves something that he is the one accountable.Tell him it is so the relationship remains fair for each of you.
LLM: It's understandable that your fiancé might feel upset about this situation. From a psychological perspective, it appears that there has been a miscommunication or a lack of transparency in your financial arrangements. His anger might be a result of feeling deceived or betrayed due to both the financial loss and the disagreement about financial responsibilities. It's important for couples to communicate openly and honestly about financial matters to prevent misunderstandings and potential conflicts. In this situation, you might consider discussing the importance of being clear about what each other's responsibilities are in managing your finances. Additionally, you could create a system to ensure that you are both aware of all financial obligations to avoid any future misunderstandings. Keep in mind that open communication and financial transparency are the foundation for healthy financial relationships.

LLM:
