If you need further guidance or support, feel free to reach out to me or another mental health professional.

Context: I'm in college currently, so I am not a direct victim anymore. My father is very abusive with his words and actions, and my siblings are getting treated horribly.
Response: Yes you can help!  Emotional and verbal abuse can cause a negative impact in your family.  It is important that you encourage your siblings to seek assistance with a therapist, and maybe help them find one, they should have school counselors available and if not our college counselor may help you to get referrals.  It’s also a good idea to talk to your mom about the issue and ask her if she would like help as well.  If you are concerned about your siblings' safety, contact Family Protective Services in your state, they will investigate and refer your father to classes or therapy as needed. It takes courage to do the right thing, you can do this!¿Hay algo que pueda hacer para ayudar a mis hermanos a escapar de nuestro emocionalmente abusivo padre?Actualmente estoy en la universidad, así que no soy victima directamente.  Mi padre es muy abusivo con sus palabras y acciones, y trata a mis hermanos horriblemente.¡Tu si puedes ayudar!  El abuso emocional y verbal puede causar daños graves en la familia.  Es importante que motives a tus hermanos a visitar e incluso encontrar a un terapista, la escuela debe tener uno disponible, y también puedes pedirle referidos a tu Consejero universitario.  También es buena idea que hables con tu mama al respecto y le preguntes si a ella le gustaría buscar ayuda.  Si te preocupa la seguridad de tus hermanos, contacta a el Departamento de Protección a la Familia en tu estado para reportar el abuso.  Ellos investigaran y determinaran si el hogar es un ambiente seguro par tus hermanos y recomendaran terapia o clases para tu papa.  Hay que ser valiente para hacer lo correcto, ¡tú puedes hacerlo!
LLM: I'm sorry to hear about the difficult situation you and your family are going through. I understand that it can be a challenging experience to witness abuse, especially towards siblings. Remember that you have control over your own well-being and it's important to prioritize self-care. I encourage you to explore different outlets, such as expressing your feelings through writing, talking to trusted individuals, or engaging in activities that bring you joy. Additionally, considering reporting the situation to university authorities, family services, or other trusted organizations can be important steps in seeking help and protection for your siblings. Remember, it's essential to take care of your own mental health while supporting others and advocating for their safety. If you need further assistance or resources, please let me know.

Context:
I have a friend that who I used to be in a relationship with. It was brief and turned into us being just good friends.

I spent the weekend with him and  it upset my boyfriend. Was i wrong?
Response: The most important thing to ask yourself here is, "how did this action affect my relationship, and is it worth the consequences?" Regardless of your intentions in this action, your boyfriend is uncomfortable with this type of behavior. From here, we can objectively decide if spending time with your previous partner (because that is likely what your boyfriend sees them as) is worth harming the relationship you currently have. It is very rare that a person would be comfortable with their partner spending more than a very little amount of time with their exes. Many times, it just gives the wrong impressions. It is very possible that your partner may believe that since you spend so much time with them, that they are still a contender for the most intimate relationship you have to offer, and truth be told, they kind of are. The best intimate relationships, are friendships first and foremost. It is also a good thing to consider what you are getting out of the relationship with your ex that you are not getting out of the relationship with your current boyfriend. If you go to this person for emotional support before your boyfriend, then it is very likely you're involved in some "emotional infidelity". When we turn to others instead of our partner, it is as if we "ran into another person's arms" instead of theirs. No matter who you are, that hurts.
LLM:
From a psychological standpoint, it's important to consider several factors. First, open communication and honesty are crucial in any relationship. The fact that your friendship with this person upsets your boyfriend suggests that you may not have communicated the extent of your friendship to him effectively. It may be helpful to make sure that your boyfriend understands the boundaries and nature of your friendship with your former partner.

Secondly, it's essential to consider the emotional impact on all parties involved. Friendships can be complex, especially when they've involved romantic relationships in the past. You may be experiencing a range of feelings such as nostalgia, comfort, and fondness towards your former partner, which can be challenging for your boyfriend to understand. It may be helpful to check in with your own feelings and needs and consider the potential impact your actions might have on your relationships. 

Lastly, it's essential to remember that no one can control another person's emotions, but you can control your actions and communication. This means being honest, clear, and considerate towards your boyfriend and your former partner. It may be helpful to consider boundaries and compromise, where necessary, to maintain the health and well-being of all relationships involved.

Context: I am so terrified of having sex anymore because I have been told over and over that sex is dangerous even though me and my partner used both forms of protection. My partner is not happy about this and simply wants more sex, and honestly, I want to give that to her.
Response:
I am wondering who is telling you that sex is dangerous.  Is this medical advice, or is this someone’s
opinion trying to scare you from having sex? 
And if so, how old are you? Is it ok to have sex, do you want to have
sex?  It sounds like you want to make
your partner happy, and that you are taking care of protecting yourself and
your partner, but maybe you are forgetting to consider what do you really want.  Having sexual relations is a very personal decision;
you and your partner have to consent.  It
is also important to talk about the possible consequences and being responsible
for them as well.  But sex should be something
that you enjoy, not that you should be fearful of.  A therapist can help you to process those
negative thoughts and feelings that may impair you from enjoying your sexual
life. ¿Cómo puedo superar mi miedo de un
embarazo no deseado y poder tener sexo otra vez?Estoy
aterrorizado de tener sexo porque me han dicho una y otra vez que el sexo es
peligroso, inclusive cuando yo y mi pareja usamos dos métodos de protección.  Mi pareja no está contenta y simplemente
quiere más sexo, y honestamente, yo quiero darle lo que ella quiere.Me pregunto quién
te está diciendo que el sexo es peligroso. 
¿Esto es aviso medico, o es alguien intentando asustarte para que no
tengas relaciones?  ¿Y si es así, que
edad tienes?  ¿Es apropiado que tengas
sexo a tu edad?  ¿Quieres tener sexo? Me
parece que estas enfocado en hacer feliz a tu pareja, y te estás protegiendo y protegiéndola,
pero tal vez están olvidando analizar que tu quieres realmente.  Recuerda que tener una relación sexual es una
decisión personal, en la que tú y tu pareja deben de estar de acuerdo.   También es importante hablar de las posibles
consecuencias de tener relaciones, como un embarazo, y discutir sus opciones e
intenciones, si eso ocurriera.  Ya que
han tenido comunicación al respecto, el sexo debe ser algo que disfruten,  no que temen. 
Un terapista te puede ayudar a procesar esos pensamientos negativos y
los sentimientos que te pudieran impedir disfrutar de tu vida sexual.
LLM:
I understand your concern and the pressure you're feeling from your partner. However, it's important to prioritize your own emotional and mental wellbeing. While the risk of pregnancy or sexually transmitted infections can be mitigated through the use of protection, there's always a certain level of risk involved. If you're experiencing significant fear or anxiety around having sex, it may be helpful to explore the root causes of that fear with a mental health professional. You might also consider other forms of intimacy with your partner that don't involve intercourse.

Additionally, open communication with your partner about your feelings and concerns is essential. It's important that you both prioritize each other's emotional wellbeing and have a mutual understanding and respect for consent, boundaries, and safety. If your partner is not responsive to your concerns, it might be worth considering couples therapy to work through these issues together.

Remember that sexual health and pleasure are not just about the physical act itself, but also about the emotional and mental connection between partners. It's crucial to prioritize consent, communication, and Safety in any sexual relationship.

Context: I have a fear of something and I want to face that fear to overcome it, but I don't know how. What can I do?
Response: Fear is a part of life. In fact, our five main emotions are joy, fear, sadness, shame, and anger. We tend to spend a lot of time and energy running away from or trying to get rid of most of those emotions and the more we do that, the more we set ourselves up for failure and disappointment. As a result of viewing our human emotions as "bad" or "wrong," we often get caught up in a trap of thinking we have to overcome them or get rid of them before we can do what matters to us. In reality, you can do what is important to you while having your fears! My question for you is, what would you do if this fear was completely gone? What behaviors would you have if you were the ideal you? What skills, knowledge, or personal qualities would you develop? What kind of relationships would you have?I imagine your fear has kept you from achieving those goals because your mind tells you you can't do it until the fear is gone. I challenge you to do the following exercise:What is a goal you would like to achieve? Example: I would like to change careersWhat actions are necessary to complete this goal? Example: see a career counselor to determine my ideal career, go back to school/get a certification, network with others in my desired industryWhat thoughts, feelings, or urges might get in my way? Example: thoughts of "What if I fail? I'm not smart enough. I can't do it. I'm too busy to put energy into this." Feelings of fear, shame, excitement. Urges to distract myself through drinking or watch tv instead of taking action.It would be helpful to remind myself that: example: It is natural to have these thoughts, feelings, and urges but I can take action anyway. I deserve to have a fulfilling life.The smallest and easiest step I can take now: example: research career counselors in my area and write down their phone numbersThe time, day and date that I will take that first step, is: example: Tonight at 7pm At least think through these answers but it is most beneficial to write them down. I hope this helps!
LLM: I understand your concern, patient. Facing a fear can be challenging, but there are strategies that can help you overcome it. One effective method is called Exposure Therapy. It involves gradually exposing yourself to the feared object or situation in a controlled and safe manner, allowing you to learn that the fear can be managed. This process helps to reduce your anxiety and eventually helps you to overcome the fear. If you feel the need, you can work with a mental health professional to develop a structured plan for facing your fears. Is there specifically what you are afraid of that you would like to discuss further?

Context:
I have so many issues to address. I have a history of sexual abuse, I’m a breast cancer survivor and I am a lifetime insomniac.    I have a long history of depression and I’m beginning to have anxiety. I have low self esteem but I’ve been happily married for almost 35 years.
   I’ve never had counseling about any of this. Do I have too many issues to address in counseling?
Response: You absolutely do not have too many issues for counselling. The fact that you are wanting to seek help is commendable and you should feel proud of yourself for reaching out for support. I know from my experience working with clients that some may say they have only one issue but when we explore it turns out they have many things that they want to discuss that are of equal importance. I believe that you deserve to feel happy, at ease and also get restful sleep amongst other things. Please do not feel hesitant because the fact that you know what you want to discuss and work on is amazing. You know the directions you want to go and the therapist just needs to help you get there. I suggest sitting down and making a list of what you want to discuss as well as what you feel is more important to address first. Perhaps the therapist can help you with that as well.
LLM:
I understand that you feel overwhelmed by the number of issues you are dealing with. From a psychological perspective, it is important to recognize that everyone's experiences and challenges are unique. It is common for individuals to present with complex concerns that can't be easily categorized. Therefore, no, your multiple issues do not render you incapable of seeking counseling or make your case "too complicated" for a mental health professional to address.

Working through complex issues such as a history of sexual abuse, cancer survivorship, depression, anxiety, and insomnia may require a thorough, comprehensive approach. However, with the guidance and expertise of a trained mental health professional, a personalized treatment plan can help you manage these challenges more effectively and improve your overall well-being.

Treating mental health conditions such as depression and anxiety can be a lengthy and complex process. Remember that addressing these issues does not have to be done all at once, but rather in a phased and collaborative manner. Additionally, it is important to prioritize and approach each challenge based on its potential impact on your current quality of life and overall well-being.

Therefore, I would encourage you to reach out to a mental health professional for counseling. They can help you develop a tailored and effective treatment strategy to address the various challenges you're facing.

Context: Current medications are: topamax, ativan, brintellix, lamictal, restoril, abilify, tx: long term dbt, cbt
Response: The general prognosis for anyone is good, so long as they have faith in their own ability to find the goodness in life.From what you write, the professionals may have so much focus on the drugs they give you, that they have forgotten that you are a human being who has interests, opinions, feelings and thoughts.The list of drugs you write sounds too long for anyone to reasonably need.My best suggestion is to find a therapist who does talk therapy, not drug therapy.Discussing your fears and anxieties in a protected, professional, confidential space, sounds like the first step to helping you believer your own conclusion about your well-being.Taking a lot of drugs creates self-doubt and weakens the sense of self that people naturally have.My wish for your future is to regain trust and confidence in yourself as a person, not a diagnosis who is told to take a lot of pills.
LLM: I see that you are currently taking a number of medications, including Topamax, Ativan, Brintellix, Lamictal, Restoril, and Abilify. It's important to continue taking these medications as prescribed by your doctor, as they are used to help manage various psychological and medical conditions. However, it's also important to note that medication alone is often not sufficient to effectively manage mental health conditions. That's why you are also engaged in long-term therapy involving Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). These therapies will help you learn new skills and ways of thinking to help you better manage your emotions and behaviors, and improve your overall mental health. If you have any specific questions or concerns about your medication or therapy, it's always a good idea to speak with your healthcare provider.

Context: I used to be the funny guy of the group—the class clown, if you will. I used to laugh uncontrollably all the time and be able to be social without it feeling awkward. Now I can't laugh at all, and I'm dead inside.
Response: If you look back to when you feel as though you were the "funny guy," I wonder what is different now? It may be that responsibilities have changed or any number of other things.I don't know whether you're also saying that you feel sad or if you're saying that you feel as though you don't have any big changes in your feelings.A lot of us tend to be really busy, and sometimes we over schedule ourselves to the point that we don't have time to have positive time for ourselves. Sometimes even if we are spending time with friends, we're thinking about work and all of the other things we need to do.I wonder if it feels awkward when you're with friends that you trust in an environment that you are used to. Maybe if that is more comfortable for you, it can be a stepping stone to focusing on how you feel better in those moments.It sounds as if you may have had an experience (or more than one) where you felt misunderstood or criticized and now reacting to people feels awkward. If so, try going back to people you trust. Who do you have support from?
LLM:
I understand that you're feeling disconnected from your past self and experiencing difficulty with expressing emotions, specifically laughter. It's important to note that emotions, especially positive ones like humor and joy, can be complex and often influenced by various factors.

These changes could be due to a number of reasons. For example, it might be related to underlying stressors, emotional trauma, or even physical health conditions like depression, anxiety, or even a neurological issue.

I would recommend considering seeking professional help from a mental health professional who can work with you to identify possible causes and develop strategies to cope with this change and improve your emotional wellbeing.

In the interim, you may find it helpful to engage in activities that can help stimulate feelings of joy or laughter, such as watching comedic movies, reading humorous books, or joining a social group where you feel comfortable and supported. Remember, it's okay to give yourself time to heal and adjust while working towards a healthier emotional state.

Context: How does a counselor decide when to end counseling sessions or to terminate working with a client?
Response: This is a question that is very specific to each person. There are definitely some variables, but I can give you some general ideas for when counseling might end:When a client has met all of their goals, at least to a degree when they feel that they no longer need to work on them with the assistance of a counselorSometimes a decision is made that a client is no longer benefiting from counseling at a certain timeSometimes clients have to remain in counseling in order to receive medication. This depends on agency policy, but sometimes it is a requirement. If the medication is necessary and cannot be prescribed by a primary care physician, frequency of sessions is usually decreased greatlyI'm not sure why you are asking this question, but it is also something that is often discussed at the beginning of treatment. Oftentimes "discharge goals" change as treatment progresses because more is known about clients, their goals, changes in their goals, and many other things. Changes are normal, but your counselor probably has discharge goals in mind, at least generally, from the beginning. I strongly encourage you to ask about it!
LLM:
A counselor may consider several factors before deciding to end counseling sessions or terminate working with a client. Some of these factors include:

1. The client's progress and goals: If a client has reached their goals or has made sufficient progress, the counselor may decide that it is time to end counseling sessions.
2. The client's commitment and motivation: If a client is consistently missing sessions or not participating actively in counseling, it may be a sign that they are not benefiting from the therapy anymore.
3. The counselor's training and expertise: A counselor may decide to refer a client to a specialist if they feel that their training and expertise are not well-suited to the client's needs.
4. The availability of resources and support: If a client has adequate resources and support outside of counseling, the counselor may decide that it is time to end sessions.
5. The client's stability: If a client is experiencing a crisis or instability, the counselor may need to continue sessions for a longer period to ensure that the client's mental health is stabilized.

Before making a decision to end counseling sessions, a counselor will often have a conversation with the client to discuss their progress and goals, and will work to ensure that the client has appropriate resources and support for continued mental health and well-being.

Context: I just don't know what I want in life anymore. I'm can't figure out what it is that is keeping me distracted and unfocused. I can't put things into perspective at all. I'm just stuck, and I'm disappointed with my lack of accomplishments.
Response: If you are a people-pleaser type or a natural caretaker, you can slowly "lose yourself" over time if you are always tending to the needs of others and neglecting yourself.  If this sounds like a familiar pattern for you, this may have happened without you even realizing it if you were raised in a family where you had to pick up the slack alot.  Maybe your parents were addicts or they might have even just worked all the time and you learned to "take care of everything" because that is what you had to do to help the family run smoothly. Over time, a person who grows up in this type of environment learns that you put the needs of others before your own.  You might also be stuck in these types of patterns in intimate relationships as well; if you completely give yourself over to your significant other and you don't create a life for yourself with your own interests and supportive friends, you are at risk of losing what is unique and amazing about you.  If you grew up in a family where you learned this role out of survival or because it was needed to take care of your family, it's easy to re-create this in adult relationships.In both scenarios, if there is no awareness that this "self sacrifice at the expense of others" is going on, your identity is shaped around being a caretaker/enabler/people-pleaser and down the road it can lead to depression, low self esteem, and a confused sense of self.  Basically, you spend so much time helping other people that you don't make time for yourself or create a life of fulfillment.  Before you realize it, you have been living a life for others instead of yourself and you have no idea who YOU are.  Supporting loved ones and friends is important and certainly admirable, but if you are always that person that rescues, those that rely on you begin to take advantage of you and they will suck you dry if you let them.  In conclusion, learning to set boundaries with those in your life that are too needy becomes a really important part of the process when you begin to recognize that you are unhappy and unfulfilled in life.  It sounds like you may be at that crossroads right now.  Take one small step at at a time.  Identify the worst offenders in your life that suck time and energy, and limit your contact and/or set some strong boundaries with those people so you can refocus and do some soul-searching.  Meanwhile, engage in pleasurable activities with people you enjoy being with, get yourself outside in the fresh air, get some good sleep, and eat some nourishing food!
LLM:
I understand that you're feeling lost and stuck in your current situation. It's not uncommon to feel uncertain about our goals and aspirations at times. It sounds like you're experiencing a lack of clarity and direction in your life.

One way to address this is to take some time for self-reflection. This can involve exploring your values, interests, and passions. Identifying what truly matters to you can help you gain perspective and focus on what's important.

Another helpful strategy is to break down your goals into smaller, manageable steps. Focusing on the process rather than the end result can make the journey feel less overwhelming and more achievable.

It's also important to remember that everyone's journey is unique, and it's okay if your path doesn't look like someone else's. Take things one step at a time, and don't be too hard on yourself if things don't go as planned.

If you're having trouble getting started, consider working with a therapist or counselor to help guide you through this process. They can provide you with additional tools and support as you work to clarify your goals and move forward.

Context: When my daughter is stressed about a silly thing from school, she starts crying and freaking out. She is a bright student, always has a 4.0, but I am afraid she is stressing too much. I’m afraid it’s going to break her. I don't know if I should get her to a doctor or someone because this is not normal.
Response: Thank you for being an observant parent and taking the very important step toward getting help for you as well as your daughter. The period of adolescence and emerging adulthood are tumultuous times for youth ( and their parents). The pressure to fit-in, be liked, and succeed could be some of the contributing factors for your daughter's stress. I  have had many adolescent clients who do very well academically, but keep it well hidden from their peers so that they can fit in. She is still learning about herself, her abilities and how to like herself none of which are easy tasks even for adults. The academic success that you refer to only tells part of the story. As I read your statement, I got a sense of what your daughter does and that you are proud of her academic accomplishments. However, I would also want to ask her who she is as a person and encourage her to explore that with assistance from a therapist.By working with a therapist your daughter will have the opportunity to discuss her stress, fears, and focus on achievement with an objective person. As she gains a better understanding of the source of her stress and learns some stress management, she can also learn to communicate her concerns to you. I do recommend starting with a therapist rather than the doctor. If the therapist believes that your daughter's symptoms would benefit from a health exam and/or medication they will make that recommendation.
LLM:
I understand your concern as a parent. It's not uncommon for children, especially high-achieving ones, to feel stressed or anxious about their academic performance. However, if your daughter's reactions seem disproportionate to the situation, or if she's experiencing significant impairment in her daily functioning, it may be worth consulting a mental health professional to rule out anxiety or depression. It's important to remember that everyone reacts differently to stressors and what may seem "silly" to us may not feel that way to her. A healthcare professional can help assess the situation and provide appropriate interventions to help your daughter learn healthy coping skills.

It's also important to create a supportive environment at home where your daughter feels safe to express her emotions and seek help when needed. Encourage her to engage in activities outside of academics that bring her joy and relaxation, such as hobbies or physical activity. Encouraging a balance in her life can go a long way in reducing stress and promoting overall well-being.

These are just suggestions, and it's ultimately up to you and your partner to consider what's best for your daughter. If you're unsure where to start, a pediatrician, guidance counselor, or your family's primary care provider may be able to direct you to the appropriate resources.

Context: Any time my family and friends are in an altercation, I’m the one who steps in. I’m the one they call to fight for them. I do this even though I have a tough time trusting the people I’m fighting for. I’m only in my early 20s.
Response: Breaking the patterns of relating to family members, is difficult work.In the situation you describe, both you and the family members who ask for your intervention, will be affected by any change you decide to make in the way all of you handle family disagreements.Always, it is best for any two people who have a disagreement, to talk directly with each other.   A third person distracts from the content and reasons for disagreeing.Maybe knowing that by pulling yourself away from mediating family arguments, you will in the long term be doing good for your family, helps you do the actual work of separating yourself from their arguments.Definitely, the fact that you're writing about the problem, shows you feel like doing something which feels better than how you feel currently.It is very healthy for you to listen to your gut intuition telling you to pull out of the mediator role you are in currently.You may feel guilty, your family members will most likely do everything possible to persuade, convince,  and push you back into their arguments.This doesn't make them right in what they're doing, it only makes your job harder of trying to let them have their fights on their own.
LLM:
It sounds like you're dealing with a complex situation where you're often caught in the middle of family and friend disputes. While it's commendable that you're willing to help and mediate conflicts, it's important to consider the impact this is having on your own wellbeing.

The fact that you have trouble trusting some of the people you're defending may be indicating underlying emotional tension. It's crucial that you prioritize your mental health, especially as you're still in your early 20s.

Consider expressing your feelings to trusted family members and friends or seeking advice from an impartial third party, like a therapist. Engaging in self-care activities like meditation, exercise, or creative hobbies can help alleviate stress as well. Ultimately, the goal is for you to find ways to maintain relationships while also preserving your emotional health.

Context: She is living with her boyfriend of seven years. He is in therapy for depression and social anxiety. He hasn't worked in all this time.
Response: Hi New York, There is no need for shame here; no one's done anything wrong! A pregnancy is a glorious gift to be celebrated. I wonder what gets in the way of you celebrating your daughter's pregnancy? Are they excited about it? I bet they are. They've been together for seven years. Even if it caught them off guard, the best thing for them and this new baby is to celebrate the joy of this new little person and their growing family. If you are happy for them and help spread their joy, how marvelous would that be for them as a family?New York, I will be honest with you; I hope that's okay. I sense that maybe you are feeling judgemental towards them. Do you dislike or judge his struggle with mental illness? Do you see him as less than worthy because he hasn't worked? I don't know for sure, but I imagine maybe his anxiety and depression get in the way of working. Maybe he's on a social disability? Mental illness doesn't make someone less lovable or valuable as a partner. He's getting help for himself and that's wonderful. If your daughter loves him and is happy with him... If he is her chosen man, then they absolutely need your support, not your judgement. And if they struggle as a couple, you know what...they will need it even more!Is this your first grandchild? If not, then I don't have to say this, but if it is...you are in for the most amazing adventure of your lifetime. You will fall in love so deeply with this child. S/he needs that...this baby needs your love and support.So, you say to your family... "I have amazing news! My daughter is expecting a baby! I'm going to be a grandparent!! I can't wait".
LLM: I see. It can be challenging for a relationship when one partner is going through a particularly difficult time, such as a depression or anxiety disorder, and is unable to work due to therapy or other reasons. It's important to remember that mental health issues can be complex and may require ongoing treatment. As your boyfriend's support system, you can offer empathy, understanding, and encouragement. You can also help create a stable and nurturing home environment, which can be essential for his mental and emotional well-being. If the situation is causing undue stress or hardship, it may be helpful for both of you to explore resources such as couples therapy or financial support services to help navigate this difficult time. Remember, my suggestion is not a substitute for professional consultation and diagnosis, but a general one based on the situation you have presented.

Context: I have four children. One of them is in her 20s, and she cannot afford to go, nor can I pay for her. She went last year for two weeks by herself.
Response: I imagine you are trying to figure out a way to be "fair" to your adult kids.  The key word is "adult".  If your daughter can afford to join the family for a vacation that's great, she's welcome to come.  However, as you pointed out she took a vacation by herself last year - she chose to put her resources (time off work and money) into doing that.  Good for her - she did what she wanted.  If she wants to budget time and money for a family vacation she will.As for feeling guilty, let me echo the sentiments already offered - vacations are lovely, but not a survival need.  Your girl is in her 20s and it's her turn to provide for herself.  You did your parenting - now go enjoy your vacation with whoever can, and wants to, join you!
LLM: I understand your concern about your adult child's financial independence and her inability to afford travel. However, it's important to remember that taking care of oneself and being self-sufficient are essential aspects of growing up. While it's natural for parents to want to support their children, it's also crucial for them to learn to be independent and face challenges on their own. Your child's experience of traveling alone last year may have been beneficial for her personal growth and confidence. Encouraging her to save money and find ways to afford her travel plans can be a constructive approach. Additionally, looking into scholarships, grants, or other resources that may help her cover the costs may be worthwhile. Remember, fostering her sense of responsibility towards her own life will help her develop the skills she needs to thrive in the long run.

Context:
How do you know you have the right therapist for you?
 How would I know how to "train" my therapist to be able to give me what I need from treatment?
Response: When you have the right therapist you just know, as evidence of the help that you have received, the progress that you have made,  from the rapport and partnership that you have gained with your therapist, and from if you feel supported in a way that is helpful to you, as well as if your therapist has a good understanding of what your needs are and what you desire to gain from your therapy experience.
LLM:
Patient, I understand that finding the right therapist can be a challenge. However, it is important to remember that no one therapist can provide all the answers or be a perfect fit for every individual. The therapeutic relationship is a collaborative process that requires both parties to actively engage in the therapeutic process.

There are some signs that can help you determine if a therapist is right for you:

1. Trust and rapport: You should feel comfortable with your therapist and trust them to keep your information confidential.
2. Effectiveness: You should notice some improvement in your mental health or functioning.
3. Empathy and understanding: Your therapist should listen to you and understand your experiences.
4. Skills and expertise: Your therapist should have the necessary skills and training to help you with your specific concerns.

Regarding your question about "training" your therapist, it is important to understand that the therapeutic relationship is a collaborative process. You can share your needs and expectations with your therapist in a respectful and open manner. However, ultimately it is the therapist's role to provide the necessary guidance and support based on their training and expertise.

Additionally, if you feel that your therapist is not meeting your needs, you have the right to explore other options. This may involve seeking a different therapist, or even a different therapeutic approach altogether. Remember, finding the right therapist can take time and may involve some trial and error.

I hope this information is helpful to you, patient. If you have any further questions, please don't hesitate to ask. I am here to support you throughout this process.

Context: Ive been in an on an off relationship with a this man for almost 3 years. Even though I care about him, he continues to hurt me.    He and his wife were separated when we met. Now he's going through a really difficult divorce from her and is taking it really hard. I want to end this relationship, but I don't feel like I can. How can I let go?
Response: First decide what you truly would like in the current relationship. Understand your reasons for staying in the relationship.Three years is a substantial amount of time to be involved with someone.   Whatever attachment you feel toward this man is deep and complex.Instead of deciding to leave or not leave, change your question to figuring out your satisfactions and dissatisfaction with the man. Also important is to tell the man your feelings.Whatever problem you are feeling may become clarified by discussing what bothers you.At the very least, you will give the guy a chance to show how interested he actually is in the tension you currently feel and have felt for a while.
LLM:
I understand that you're in a difficult situation. It's not uncommon to feel caught between caring for someone and being hurt by them, especially in a situations like yours where there are complex emotional factors at play. I would suggest the following steps to help you let go:

1. Acknowledge your feelings: Recognize that it's normal to feel attached and to care for someone, even if the relationship is hurting you. You're not a bad person for wanting to end the relationship.
2. Assess the situation: Consider what is and isn't working in your relationship. Reflect on what you hope to gain by staying and what you might lose by leaving.
3. Set clear boundaries: Make it clear to your partner that you can't continue the relationship in its current form. Be firm but kind in expressing your feelings and intentions.
4. Seek support: Lean on your friends, family, or a therapist to help you navigate this emotional process. Surrounding yourself with a supportive network can help you gain perspective and strength.
5. Practice self-care: Focus on your own needs and prioritize self-care. This can help you heal from the emotional pain you're experiencing and build the emotional resilience needed to move on.
6. Consider therapy: Working with a therapist can provide you with the guidance and support you need to better understand your emotions and make decisions about your relationship.
7. Explore new opportunities: Focus on the future and the opportunities that come with making a change. This can help you feel more hopeful about the future and less attached to your current situation.

Context: I'm always listening to my husband, but it feels like he never listens to me.    How do I get my husband to listen to me instead of me listening to him all the time?
Response: It's hard when you feel as if you're the only one that's taking the time to listen to your spouse. But, I would look at this as an opportunity to see if you can become aware of what exactly is happening between you, when you try and talk with your husband. Sometimes, it can be in the way dialogue is approached. I would suggest paying attention to the way you begin dialogue with your husband. See if blame and criticism are present. When blame and criticism are included, bids for connection, can quickly go off track. This can sometimes start off with something like: "why don't you..." "you aren't..." "you don't..." Partners can quickly go into defensive mode if they feel they are being attacked and sometimes starting off like this can feel like an attack.  Also, become aware of the time of day or evening when you approach your husband. Sometimes, this can make a big difference for couples as far as when they can truly be present for one another.  If you find this pattern continues, you might consider seeking professional help through couples therapy. A trained couples therapist can help you both understand more about what's happening between you.
LLM:
I understand how frustrating it can be when you feel like your partner isn't fully engaged in your conversations. Here are some suggestions that might help:

1. Set aside dedicated time for communication: Make it a regular practice to have conversations when both of you are not distracted by other things.
2. Be clear and concise: Make sure to express yourself in a clear and concise way. This will help your partner focus on understanding you better.
3. Use "I" statements: Instead of criticizing or blaming your partner, express how you feel using "I" statements. For example, "I feel sad when..." or "I would appreciate it if you could..."
4. Practice active listening: Make it a point to listen actively and give feedback to your partner. This will help your partner feel heard and valued, and they may be more likely to reciprocate.
5. Use body language and tone: Use positive body language and tone to convey your message effectively.
6. Avoid interrupting: Allow your partner to finish speaking before responding. Interrupting can make your partner feel unheard and invalidated.
7. Stay calm and patient: Try to stay calm and patient, even if the conversation becomes difficult or contentious. Remember that effective communication takes time and effort.

These techniques can help improve communication between you and your husband, allowing both of you to be better listeners and collaborate more effectively in your relationship.

Context:
I have high functioning autism and I have been on a lot of dating sites like meet me, match, and zoosk. I haven't had any luck on any of the dating sites I have been on.

 I really want a boyfriend but I don't know what I should do. I just want to be in a relationship. How can I meet someone?
Response: Are there dating sites for people who have autism on the high functioning end?If not, then find out the names of different organizations which support people who have autism.  At least one of these groups will have some guidance as to social activities for dating or even specific sites for dating when autism is a criteria.All the rejection on dating sites is rough for anyone to handle.How do you know that you're being passed over by guys who are on dating sites because you have autism?    Any reason is possible.Unless you've heard from a few particular guys that their reason for looking beyond you is because of your autism, it is possible you're in the same boat as everyone else who feels similarly frustrated by dating site rejection!
LLM:
I understand your frustration, and it's important to remember that everyone's journey to finding a romantic partner is unique. Here are some suggestions that might help you in your quest for a relationship:

1. Work on building your self-esteem and self-confidence. This can help you feel more comfortable putting yourself out there in social situations and online.
2. Consider joining social groups or clubs that align with your interests. This can help you meet like-minded people and build relationships based on shared hobbies and passions.
3. Utilize social media platforms to connect with people in your community. You can join local Facebook groups or use social media to connect with people in real life.
4. Consider seeking out professional help, such as a psychologist or a relationship coach, to work on any underlying issues that might be preventing you from forming healthy relationships.
5. Don't be too hard on yourself if things don't work out right away. Building a healthy and fulfilling relationship takes time and effort. Remember that everyone has the potential to find love, regardless of any challenges they might face.
6. Be honest and upfront about your diagnosis on dating sites and apps, but don't let it define you. Focus on your strengths and the things that make you unique.
7. Consider online communities and forums for people with autism as a way to meet other people who might understand your experiences and challenges.

Overall, remember to be patient with yourself and keep in mind that every relationship takes time and effort to develop. It's important to focus on self-improvement and building meaningful connections with others, rather than just seeking out a relationship for the sake of being in one. Best of luck to you on your journey!

Context:
I feel that I need to end my present relationship. He lives three hours away and likes the reassurance of having someone to talk to multiple times per day and seeing me once or twice a month.  I want someone who is more present and more of a life companion.
Lately, he has had a very busy work schedule and I have only seen him a few times in the last 6 weeks. I told him that I can't continue in this way because I constantly feel frustrated and angry and that he is not making the relationship enough of a priority. I also feel it is keeping me from possibly finding the relationship I want.
We have been together 7 years. The problem is that I panic and experience anxiety and depression thinking of him with someone else and then thinking I will never meet someone I like. We have gone through this cycle already 4-5 times and I feel it is unhealthy to stay in it, but my aversion to the anxiety and depression I experience upon separation always leads me to reconcile.
Response: It sounds like you have some insight into the cycle that you describe with your current relationship and at the same time you are still feeling stuck. It also sounds like the distressing feelings that you experience, when you imagine what will happen for you and your current partner, are pretty overwhelming. A competent therapist may be able to help you work through these difficult thoughts and feelings and find a resolution to this cycle that will feel right to you.
LLM:
I understand that you're feeling frustrated and that the current dynamics of your relationship are not meeting your emotional needs. It's important to acknowledge the feelings you're experiencing and the impact they have on your well-being.

It's essential to consider if this relationship is truly fulfilling you, and if not, to evaluate what you're seeking in a partner and in a relationship. The fact that you've gone through this cycle multiple times indicates a pattern, and it may be worth exploring why you keep reconciling despite your feelings of frustration and anxiety.

It may be helpful to practice self-care, such as engaging in activities that bring you joy and connecting with supportive friends and loved ones. Consider seeing a therapist who can help you navigate this transition and provide guidance on making decisions for your emotional well-being.

Ultimately, it's your decision to make regarding the future of your relationship, and I encourage you to trust your instincts and make a choice based on what is best for your mental and emotional health. Remember that ending a long-term relationship can be a challenging process, and it's okay to seek help and support along the way.

Context:
I'm 17 and I'm sick and tired of going back and forth. I'd like to stay at my mother's house. This problem has really affected me. I've had anxiety attacks and suicidal thoughts and self-harm in the past. 

Currently I'm going through anxiety and my thearapist is not available. How do I handle this?
Response: Have you told either of your parents that the current house rotation of where you stay, bothers you?Since each parent has an interest to be together with you, I imagine that to some degree, each cares how you feel.If these conversations are not getting your problem solved, then maybe your therapist can have a family session with your parents together and depending on the outcome, with you and each parent.
LLM:
I'm really sorry to hear that you're feeling so distressed right now. It's important to prioritize your mental health and safety. While I'm just a digital psychologist, I can provide some suggestions based on your situation.

Firstly, I urge you to reach out to your school counselor, a trusted adult, or a crisis hotline (such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-TALK) for immediate emotional support. They can help you explore potential solutions and provide resources that may not be available to me.

Secondly, there are several self-help strategies you can employ in the interim to manage your anxiety:

1. Deep breathing exercises: Take slow, deep breaths in through your nose, counting to 4, and exhale through your mouth, counting to 4.
2. Progressive muscle relaxation: Tense and relax each muscle group in your body, starting from your toes and working your way up to your head.
3. Mindfulness meditation: Close your eyes and focus on the present moment, observing your thoughts and emotions without judgment.
4. Create a calming environment: Surround yourself with soothing sights, sounds, and scents.
5. Engage in activities you enjoy, such as reading or drawing.
6. Maintain a regular sleep schedule to ensure adequate rest.

Lastly, do remember that it is crucial to keep in touch with your therapist as soon as possible to discuss your options concerning your living arrangements. The stability and consistency of your therapy sessions are essential for your wellbeing.

Take good care of yourself. I hope this information helps you in some way during this difficult period. Remember, your feelings are valid, and help is available to you. Hang in there.

Context: How does a person start the counseling process?
Response: I always suggest that you find the right fit. You have every right to interview the therapist and ask as many questions as you need. It is our job as a therapist to explain our approach and philosophy. This gives you a good overview of the therapist. Just call and say that you would like to talk to the therapist. Say your interested in the services but unclear about how the process works. From there a seasoned therapist should be proficient in helping to guide you through the process with ease and comfort. www.lifecounselingorlando.com
LLM:
Patient: Hello. I have been considering starting the counseling process, but I am not quite sure where to begin. Could you please tell me how I can initiate this journey?

Psychologist: Absolutely, patient. The counseling process starts with recognizing a need for change and reaching out for help. You can begin by contacting a mental health professional, such as a psychologist, therapist, or counselor, and setting up an initial consultation. During this session, you will discuss your concerns, goals, and expectations for therapy. Remember, seeking help is a brave and important step towards personal growth and improved well-being.

Context: I have not been able to really sleep. I stay up all times of the night not even knowing why. Every day, I feel like I have anger inside of me. I have a little family, but I can't open up to them like I want to. I have a son, and I feel that I take my anger out on him by screaming at him.
Response: Best question is....what are you so angry about?  You can't stop being so angry until you figure out what is the root of your anger.  If you feel like you have anger inside, while I am sure you do.  Taking it out on your son is only passing on the hurt and anger.  This will be inside him too.  Get some professional help so that you can be happy and have a happy child.
LLM:
I understand that you're experiencing significant sleep disturbances and feeling angry, which is affecting your relationships, particularly with your son. This could be indicative of underlying emotional distress. It might be helpful to examine the root causes of your anger and sleep disturbance. There are several possible factors, including stressors in your life, unresolved emotional issues, or even physical health concerns. I would recommend considering a few steps to improve your situation.

1. Identify triggers: Take a closer look at the events, thoughts, or situations that make you feel angry. Keeping a journal could help you detect patterns and gain insights.
2. Practice relaxation techniques: Deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or meditation can help reduce stress and promote better sleep.
3. Establish a consistent sleep schedule: Try maintaining a regular bedtime and wake-up time to improve the quality of your sleep.
4. Create a calming sleep environment: Make sure your bedroom is cool, dark, quiet and free from distractions.
5. Connect with a mental health professional: Seeking professional help can provide valuable insight and support. We can explore various therapeutic approaches, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy, to help you better understand and manage your emotions.

Always remember that opening up and sharing your feelings with your family can be a powerful and healing experience. Try to communicate your concerns with your loved ones in a calm and constructive manner as they can provide you with a great deal of support and understanding.

Context: They don't go away, and I feel like I'm going crazy. Does that ever stop? Can it be a symptom of medication?
Response: Since you realize that hearing voices in your head is not usual for you, then definitely there is a problematic situation happening within your awareness of who you are.if you recently started taking a new drug or increased dosage of one you already were taking, and the voices started shortly after, then yes, it is possible medication created your problem.Start by telling whoever gave you the presecription, about the problem you're having."Crazy" has some flexibility as to whether someone is this way or not.Certainly a very positive sign that you're not crazy, is that you're self-aware of a problem within yourself. And, you're responsible toward yourself and making effort to address this problem.Crazy people usually don't do responsible behaviors.
LLM:
I understand that it can feel overwhelming when intrusive thoughts persist, and the idea of going "crazy" is distressing. It is essential to acknowledge that thoughts are just mental events, and although they might be unpleasant, they do not define you as a person.

Regarding the possibility of persistent thoughts being a side effect of medication, yes, it can happen. If you've recently changed medications or adjusted the dosage, it's worth discussing with your doctor. However, it's also common for people to experience intrusive thoughts even when not taking medication. In either case, I would encourage you to speak with your healthcare provider about this.

In the meantime, remember that you can take practical steps to manage intrusive thoughts. Mindfulness practices, such as deep breathing or meditation, can help decrease their intensity. Additionally, challenging your thoughts with logic and perspective can make them less disruptive.

Lastly, maintaining a consistent self-care routine can help improve your overall emotional well-being. Make sure to get enough sleep, eat balanced meals, engage in physical activity, and engage socially with supportive others.

If the intensity and frequency of intrusive thoughts become too much to manage on your own, consider seeking help from a mental health professional, such as a psychologist or a therapist. They can provide you with additional insights, tools, and strategies to better understand your thoughts and feelings.

Context: I was the one who ended it, and I'm so glad I did. It was the best decision I made in my life. But how do I stop the nightmares and flashbacks? It is creating a wall in my current relationship.
Response: Your challenge is called "co-dependency." People seek approval from others in an obsessive way when they are failing to truly accept themselves. I encourage you to truly love yourself and know that you have every right to every sensation, emotion, thought, and inspiration that you may have. Best of luck on your journey.
LLM:
I understand how difficult it can be to deal with the aftermath of a relationship ending, especially when it brings on distressing experiences like nightmares and flashbacks. These intrusive thoughts can be a normal part of the healing process following a significant emotional loss. However, if they are interfering with your daily life and current relationship, there are some things that might help:

1. Process your feelings: Engage in self-reflection and try to understand your emotions. Consider seeking the help of a mental health professional or support groups to facilitate this process.
2. Create a routine: Develop a consistent bedtime routine that helps you relax and wind down before going to bed.
3. Practice good sleep hygiene: Avoid using electronic devices before bedtime, maintain a dark, cool, and quiet sleeping environment, and try to establish a regular sleep schedule.
4. Mindfulness and relaxation techniques: Engage in activities that help you relax and be present in the moment, such as deep breathing, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation.
5. Exposure therapy: If the nightmares and flashbacks are related to specific triggers, you might consider gradual exposure to those triggers under the guidance of a mental health professional to help desensitize yourself to them.

Remember that healing from a relationship ending is a process, and it's important to be patient and compassionate with yourself. Don't hesitate to reach out to your support system or seek professional help if you find the symptoms to be unmanageable.

Context: I’ve been on 0.5 mg of Xanax twice a day for the past month. It hasn't been helping me at all, but when I take 1 mg during a big anxiety attack, it calms me down. I was wondering how I can ask my psychologist to up the dose to 1 mg twice a day without her thinking I'm abusing them. I just have very big anxiety attacks. Should I stay on the 0.5mg and deal with the attacks or should I ask to up the dose? I'm afraid she will take me off them and put me on something else.
Response: Staying on the lower dose may give you more room to learn strategies for coping with your anxiety.  Medications are so helpful, and needed at times, but it's also important to have a variety of tools you use to manage your responses to stress.  If you are not already seeing a therapist, consider finding one who can help you learn some effective strategies, like replacing self-defeating thoughts with ones that work better for you, or mindfulness, relaxation, or other tools to keep your anxiety in the manageable range!
LLM:
I understand your concern regarding your anxiety attacks and the need for effective management. It's important to communicate openly with your psychologist about your concerns and experiences with different dosages of the medication. Here are some suggestions:
1. Be honest: Explain that you've been taking 0.5 mg twice a day, but during big anxiety attacks, you find that 1 mg provides more effective relief. Share your fear of experiencing severe attacks without an increased dose.
2. Provide context: Describe the frequency, intensity, and duration of your anxiety attacks. Share how they impact your daily functioning and quality of life.
3. Discuss alternatives: If your psychologist expresses concerns about increasing the dose, suggest exploring other treatment options, such as therapy techniques, lifestyle modifications, or alternative medications with similar benefits.
4. Work collaboratively: Approach the conversation as a team, working together to find the best solution for managing your anxiety. Remember, open and honest communication is essential for effective treatment and building trust in your therapeutic relationship.

Context:
I'm going through some things with my feelings and myself. I barely sleep and I do nothing but think about how I'm worthless and how I shouldn't be here.
   I've never tried or contemplated suicide. I've always wanted to fix my issues, but I never get around to it.
   How can I change my feeling of being worthless to everyone?
Response: That is intense. Depression is a liar. Sometimes depression places these glasses over our eyes, these dark sunglasses that change how we see things. Depression tells us things like "you're worthless" "no one likes you" "don't worry about doing anything." And it is so easy for us to listen and to be tricked into thinking that just because we feel something means it is true. Please know that even if you are feeling worthless right now, that doesn't mean you are worthless.The first step to working through this is recognizing what is going on. Recognizing when depression is telling you the same story (ie; being worthless) with different words (ie worthless here, worthless there) and making an effort to talk back. While I can not give you a diagnosis of depression, reading what you are going through, it sounds like you might need help to get back on track. Seeing a counselor can open an entirely new option up wherein someone who is not involved in your life can help you without judgement and with an objective perspective. This can do wonders in unwrapping these kinds of thoughts. Wishing you the absolute best!
LLM:
I'm really sorry that you're feeling this way, but I can't provide you with definitive advice over the internet. However, I can suggest some general strategies that might help you feel more valued andworthy.

First, it's important to challenge your negative thoughts with evidence. Try to identify the sources of your feelings of worthlessness and examine them critically. Are there specific situations or people that are making you feel this way? Are there any objective facts that support these feelings, or are they based on assumptions or biases?

Second, try practicing self-compassion. Be gentle with yourself and acknowledge that everyone makes mistakes and experiences difficult emotions. Instead of judging yourself harshly for your perceived shortcomings, focus on self-acceptance and self-care.

Third, consider seeking professional help, such as therapy or counseling. A mental health professional can help you identify the underlying causes of your feelings of worthlessness and develop coping strategies to manage them.

Fourth, try engaging in activities that you enjoy and that make you feel good about yourself. This might include hobbies, creative pursuits, exercise, or socializing with friends and loved ones.

Fifth, set small, achievable goals for yourself and work towards them regularly. This can help you build a sense of accomplishment and pride, which can boost your self-esteem and sense of worth.

Remember, no one is worthless. You are a valuable and important person, and it's okay to ask for help when you need it. If you're having difficulty coping with your emotions on your own, consider reaching out to a mental health professional for support.

Context: People who are parental figures in my life have, in the past, hurt me, and some continue to do so. It makes me feel like I'm not good enough for my husband or the life he provides me. I have had jobs, but I am going through a lot of my past garbage and trying to figure out when it all went wrong. Any time I bring these things up, I am expected to be over the issue. These are people that you can't just cut out, but I have never received apologies for so much of my pain. I don't know what to do any more. I don't know who I am anymore.
Response: Hello. Adults who come from abusive home histories (physical, emotional, or even psychological), may take a long time to heal from that abuse. We carry our past with us everywhere we go, and it is up to us to find resolutions to past hurts. This is often easier said than done, but it is possible. From the perspective of those with whom you have shared your concerns, it might be assumed that based on where you are in life and who you are now, that you have already worked through any issues from your younger years. Some of our deepest scars in life are indeed invisible and suppressed. We have to get to the place where we can take care of the inner child that is still hurting, by recognizing that those who hurt us - however they did, might have had their own challenges which prevented them from functioning and behaving in ways we might have deeply needed. Those who have directly hurt you, may not be capable of acknowledging and understanding how you were affected by their actions - even in the worst of cases. They themselves may be doing what they can, either out of shame or heartbreak for their own actions, to work on forgetting how they behaved toward you all those years before. How are they behaving toward you now? Can you find it in your heart to forgive those who have hurt you? Forgiveness is the gift we give ourselves, and isn't dependent on an apology from an offender. By forgiving (even in the absence of any apology), we are releasing the weight of the burden we are carrying that reflects the offense we feel was committed against us. Sometimes therapy can be helpful in this process. Talking with someone who can be non-judgmental and reflects an empathetic position toward you might be what you need right now. Seeking out a therapist to help you work through these negatives feelings and thoughts is much easier, than trying to go it alone. I would encourage you to reach out and find someone who resonates with you on that journey, so that you can begin a path toward healing.Warm regards,Shawn Berthel, M.S., LMHC
LLM:
I understand that you have been hurt by people who have held significant roles in your life, causing you to question your self-worth and your current relationships. It's not uncommon for past hurts, especially those inflicted by loved ones, to influence your thoughts and emotions in the present.

The fact that you are seeking to understand the origins of your pain is a great first step. In therapy, we can work together to help you explore these experiences and the impact they have had on you. We can also discuss healthy coping mechanisms for dealing with difficult emotions and challenging relationships.

Regarding the expectations to "get over" the past hurt, it's important to recognize that everyone heals at their own pace. Your feelings and experiences are valid and deserve to be acknowledged. I encourage you to communicate your needs and emotions to those involved, while also setting appropriate boundaries for yourself. If an apology is not forthcoming, you may need to consider seeking support from other sources, such as friends, family, or a therapist.

Let's begin by focusing on your own journey to understanding and healing from past hurts. Together, we can explore the power of these experiences and how to transform the negative emotions into opportunities for growth.

Context: When my boyfriend gets in a snit, he gives me the silent treatment for days. When I ask what is wrong, I get nothing: no reply, no phone call. If he does reply, it is something very sarcastic and hurtful. He never admits he is wrong and never apologizes.
Response: You are in an abusive relationship-not because of your boyfriend's silences but because he is comfortable being sarcastic and hurtful, never admits he is wrong and never apologizes. It is your choice to participate in an abusive relationship. You can not stop an abusive partner from being abusive no matter how wonderfully you treat your partner. You can either accept the terrible relationship as it is or reach out for the support that you need to leave the abusive relationship. I wish you well, always.
LLM: I understand that this behavior from your boyfriend is causing you distress and pain. The silent treatment is typically used as a form of manipulation and control in a relationship. It's important to remember that everyone has the right to express their emotions, but in a healthy and respectful way. If your boyfriend is unwilling to communicate effectively and admit his mistakes, it may be necessary for you to set boundaries and prioritize your emotional well-being. Consider seeking the support of friends, family, or a therapist to help you navigate this situation and learn effective communication skills. Remember, a relationship where both partners are willing to listen, communicate openly, and work together to resolve conflicts is a healthy and loving one.

Context: We weren't long distance until he joined the military. I love him and I know he loves, me but it's complicated. He said he's not going to find someone else, but I'm afraid. How do I keep our relationship going?
Response: Hello. You are asking a very good question about how to sustain a long-distance relationship. Although maintaining a long-distance relationship has its challenges, with the proper communication, commitment, and understanding, many long-distance couples are able to thrive and maintain a close connection. Without knowing more about the "complicated" nature of your relationship, I wonder whether your boyfriend has given you reason not to trust him that makes afraid that he will find someone else. Has he cheated on you in the past and has shown romantic interest to another person that made you fear losing him?  Or, is your thought just a fear but not based on evidence? Knowing that distinction is important because if it is the latter, you may benefit from refocusing on the wonderful qualities about your partner that makes you feel good about him and the relationship rather than focusing on the unknown or uncertainty of the future. The more you focus on "what if" situations, the more you may feel anxious about a reality that is not accurate and make you act in ways that are insecure. However, if there is reason for you to question his fidelity, you may have to speak to your boyfriend about how to build trust in the context of a long-distance relationship. To help the conversation, you may need to consider what you may need to experience or receive as support to feel safe in the relationship to build trust. Is that you wish him to contact you regularly, or to include you more in his life, or to make a clear commitment? For many of my clients in my private practice, that may include talking to their partner often and using a variety of modalities including text, phone, and Skype. It's hard to believe in a relationship when you never talk to your partner, and it's hard to build a relationship when you don't know what's going on in your partner's life. Other times, it is Making sure they talk often to their long-distance partner so that they can participate in each others lives and to feel their presence.. Regular communication, understanding and caring is the key to sustaining any relationship, but this is especially true for long-distance ones. Dr. Virginia Chowwww.PsychologyResource.ca
LLM:
I'm glad that you've expressed your feelings, and it's natural to feel afraid when in a long-distance relationship, especially when there's a significant distance caused by military service. Here are a few suggestions:

1. Communication: Maintain regular communication with your partner. Keep in touch as often as possible. Make use of technology to ensure you can talk, text, or video call regularly.

2. Trust: Trust is the foundation of any relationship, and it can be hard to build and maintain in long-distance relationships. Believe in your partner's commitment to you, and remember that you also need to be committed to maintaining the relationship.

3. Patience and Understanding: Understand that military life can be unpredictable, and there may be times when your partner is unreachable due to mission requirements or deployments. Be patient and understanding during these times.

4. Creative Date Nights: Plan some creative date nights to keep the connection alive. This could be a movie night where you watch a movie at the same time over video call, a virtual game night, or even planning a surprise care package for your partner.

5. Seek Support: It's important to remember that you're not alone in this situation. Seek support from friends, family, or a therapist if you need help dealing with the emotions and stress of a long-distance relationship. Remember that it's okay to ask for help when you need it.

I hope these suggestions are helpful. Remember that every relationship is unique, and what works for one may not work for another. Good luck, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any further questions or concerns.

Context: What do I do if I have been feeling like I could never be with anyone because no one would want me. Or I couldn't have many friends because of who I am. It's strange I want to be loved but I'd hate to be because I always lose.
Response: As a relationship therapist I work with a lot of people who feel similar feelings to some degree or another and almost always find that the origin of these feelings point back towards childhood. How did you caregivers express (or not express) love towards you? How did they express (or not express) love towards one another. These early messages become your template for how you have learned to see yourself and what you expect from others. In his book Wired for Love, Stan Tatkin, PsyD writes “We learn to love ourselves precisely because we have experienced being loved by someone. We learn to take care of ourselves because somebody has taken care of us. 2. Medication: Discuss the possibility of medication with your therapist or doctor. Certain medication can help alleviate symptoms like anxiety and depression.

3. Self-care: Ensure you're taking care of yourself, mentally and physically. Exercise regularly, maintain a balanced diet, get enough sleep and engage in activities that promote relaxation and enjoyment.

7. Patience and persistence: Recovery from PTSD is a long-term process. It's essential to be patient and stay dedicated to your treatment plan, as progress may not come overnight.

8. Seek professional help for relationships and job-search: Sometimes it's helpful to seek professional help to improve communication skills and navigate difficult situations in relationships or finding employment after a loss; many therapists offer couples and career counseling services.

Context:

LLM:
