Context: I struggle with depression as well as pretty intense mood swings throughout the month. I experience highs where I feel amazing and energetic and then lows where I lack focus, energy, and generally have a more dark outlook on my life. How can I live a more balanced life?
Response: Are you certain your highs and lows are directly related to your cycle?  It's possible that there are at least some contributing factors, even if they are  as a result of hormonal fluctuations.For example, at the start of your period, do you have that "I feel great" feeling, or are you tired and down?  Mid-cycle (assuming your periods are regular), do you find yourself napping or ready to run a race?  Either way, how you feel may be leading you to behaviors that contribute to your changes in energy and optimism. Let's say that the few days before your period, you feel cranky, bloated and want salty food.  Your natural inclination might be to isolate, stay inside and eat chips.  The next day, you feel even more tired, cranky and bloated.  It STARTS with a hormonal symptom, but what you do with that can change how you  end up feeling.  So if you notice feeling cranky, bloated and craving salt, what if you pull up a restorative yoga video online, spend an hour being restful and centered in your body and have a good meal with a healthy balance of fats, proteins and carbs, with fresh veggies and fruits before you turn in early to give your body the rest it is asking for ?  That sets you up to feel MUCH better!And those "on top of the world days" - who doesn't love them??  But even those days, be mindful of how you are treating yourself.  Exercise for sure, but don't do twice the workout you normally would just because you can!  You might feel super energy and skip meals which sets you up for poor sleep and feeling crummy after a day or two.All that aside, if you have a couple rough days before your period, pay attention to what is bugging you.  Christiane Northrup, MD, likens our menstrual cycle to the tide.  When the tide is out (just before your period), you see all the garbage cluttering up your ocean floor, but you don't have the energy to address it, so there it stays, bugging you.  At the height of physical and emotional energy (usually mid-cycle/ovulation), the tide is back in and you don't see all that annoying stuff you saw before.  Since you have good energy at this time, take advantage of it by doing some "clean up" on the things you saw there when you felt crummy.  Maybe it's that conversation you have been putting  off with your partner, or having the long-delayed closet clean out, or searching for a job that feels/pays/fits you better.  Whatever it is, those "PMS blues" may hold important messages for you.If taking good care of yourself, staying tuned in to your needs and keeping an eye on the "tides" don't help, then  see your doctor.  Something else may be going on - our hormones all work together like a symphony - it only takes one to be out of tune to throw the whole thing off!
LLM:
I'm glad you're reaching out for help, and I understand how difficult it can be to manage depression and mood swings. Living a more balanced life involves consistent self-care and making lifestyle changes to support your mental health. Here are some suggestions based on your concerns:

1. Maintain a consistent routine: Establishing a reliable daily schedule can help regulate your energy levels and mood. Try to go to bed and wake up at the same time every day, eat your meals at regular intervals, and include regular physical activity in your routine.

2. Prioritize self-care: Make sure you get enough rest, eat a healthy diet, and engage in activities that you find enjoyable and relaxing. These may include hobbies, exercise, reading, practicing mindfulness or meditation, or socializing with friends.

3. Seek professional help: If your depression and mood swings are significantly impacting your daily life, it might be helpful to work with a healthcare professional. They can help you develop coping strategies, identify triggers, and explore treatment options such as therapy or medication.

4. Practice stress management techniques: Techniques like deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, and visualization exercises can help you react more adaptively to stressors and improve your overall well-being.

5. Engage in activities that bring you a sense of purpose: Fulfilling work, volunteering, or engaging in hobbies that give your days structure can help elevate your mood and provide a sense of accomplishment.

6. Connect with others: Maintaining strong relationships can improve your mood, help you feel more supported, and provide valuable opportunities for socialization.

7. Set realistic goals: Identifying attainable goals and breaking them down into manageable tasks can help you feel more in control of your life and boost your motivation and sense of achievement.

Remember, living a balanced life can be a challenging journey, but with the right support and commitment, it's possible to create positive changes in your life and manage your depression and mood swings effectively.

Context: My best friend and I were pranking her friend, and I told her to tell him that she likes him. She said no, but I forced her. After she told him, he told her that he likes her too. Their friendship is ruined because of me. She won't forgive me, and I feel really guilty. I feel like crying.

Context:

 How would I know how to "train" my therapist to be able to give me what I need from treatment?

Context: After I told them, they yelled at me.
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. I am very sorry that this was your experience with your family. I truly wish I could give you some things to say to them that would change their reaction, but unfortunately I can't do that. Their acceptance of you is a journey, just like coming out is a journey for you. Sometimes families initially respond this way, but after time they become more accepting and even supportive. Unfortunately, there is no way to know for sure if that will happen. So, here are a few things I would suggest:Surround yourself with people and things that remind you that you really are okay as you are. That won't take the place of your family's acceptance, and I won't even pretend that it would, but it may help during those days when it hurts the most. And that is really important. Patience is not something that many of us are really good at, but this type of situation sometimes forces you to have some. When families first learn something like this, they may go through a period of shock. And then sometimes even grieving. They may even be worried if they are of a particular religion where being bisexual is forbidden. These things can take time for them to reconcile, so it may just take some patience. If it is safe for you, try to talk to them, but if it becomes painful or hurtful then you have the right to walk away. I do recommend setting up some personal boundaries for yourself, and perhaps learn some assertiveness skills if you feel you struggle with them. The reason I feel that this is important is that people sometimes take a lot of abuse from family members and friends in times like you're describing. They may want to be accepted so much that they put themselves in hurtful positions over and over again hoping that their families will come around. They may, or they may not. And if they don't, you deserve to live a life free of abuse and to feel confident and assured of who you are. To be surrounded by folks who remind you of that. If you start seeing this pattern and you are struggling, chatting with counselor may be a great idea. I am bias probably, but I think chatting with a counselor is ALWAYS a good idea. :) Hope this helps. Be well. Be you. Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM: I'm sorry to hear that you had a difficult experience. It's understandable to feel upset when someone reacts negatively to something we have shared. However, it's important to remember that people's reactions often have more to do with their own emotions than with us as individuals. It might be helpful to talk more about this experience in our sessions and explore ways to manage your feelings and communicate more effectively with others in the future. Would you like to discuss this in more detail?

Context: I've been hurt by a man for five years. He doesn't involve me with the family or kids. Everyone in the family is against me. There is a Mass today for a family member, and he never asked me to go. I'm to sit home alone now and Christmas too. He expects me to sit alone while he enjoys being with the family. We are in our 50s, and it hurts that he won't even think of me or involve me as part of the family. He doesn't even care. I am leaving him as soon as possible, but I hurt so bad that I didn't know who to turn too. I'm now in this low funk of depression, and I'm scared because I do stupid things to myself and give up. I have no friends because I was a truck driver. I've been getting serious headaches and can't sleep. I don't eat; I've lost my appetite. This has been going on for a couple weeks now.
Response: Hi Florida,I get the sense that, aside from this relationship, you're quite isolated in general. It's sad that you're feeling excluded at Christmastime and family events. While I would suggest that you don't really know what he or his family are thinking or feeling (you assume they're against you, and that he doesn't care), he is certainly behaving in a very distant, excluding manner and it's understandable that you would feel rejected and hurt. How long has this part been happening? Has he always acted this way, or is it more recent? Did something happen recently? You mentioned doing "stupid things to yourself", and I don't know what that means. If you are engaging in self-harm he may be confused about what to do and he might need some time to think and be separate but is having trouble talking about this. Part of the problem, of course, is that you don't know what's going on for him. Certainly, you deserve the truth, and if he doesn't want to spend time with you, I would hope he would be honest. Have you been open to hearing the truth from him? Is there any reason for him to hesitate to tell you why he's being distant? Maybe you could ask him, if you are ready to hear.I'm glad to hear that you believe that you deserve a full, loving relationship, although it seems that moving forward might be difficult for you. Is it possible your happiness is a bit too dependant on this man? I'm just guessing, based on what you've said here.  It seems that it's possible your situation has led to some depression, and I would recommend a session with a qualified therapist to help you sort this out. You will need support, even if the relationship is repaired. Best of luck.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you're going through a painful experience right now. Losing a sense of belonging and connection within a family can be incredibly difficult, especially after a long-term relationship. It's understandable that you might feel sad, hopeless, and depressed due to the recent events.

Your physical symptoms, such as headaches, loss of appetite, and difficulty sleeping, are not uncommon when someone is experiencing emotional distress. These symptoms can also be signs of anxiety or depression. It's important to prioritize your mental and physical health during this time.

Regarding your feelings of isolation and lack of support, I would encourage you to reach out to trusted loved ones, if there are any, that might be able to offer a listening ear or additional support during this transition. You could also consider seeking professional help from a therapist or counselor to work through the emotional pain you're experiencing.

Moreover, engaging in activities that bring you joy, relaxation, or distraction from your negative emotions can also be helpful, such as hobbies or creative pursuits, exercise, or connecting with supportive community groups or online forums.

Please remember that healing takes time, but you don't have to face this alone. There are resources and people who care about you and will want to help you through this difficult period. Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any further questions or need additional guidance.

Context: I've been in a relationship for almost a year, and I absolutely trust my boyfriend. He treats me nice, checks on me, and tells me sweet things. However, when it comes to making life decisions, I feel he is very immature. I try to talk things out so we can compromise or make sound decisions together, but he lacks any ideas and prefers to just go with anything to avoid fighting. As far as dates, if I don't say anything, plan it, or have money to chip in, we will not go. I feel like I put in a lot of work supporting and sometimes spoiling, and all I get is a loyal man who will not cheat and say sweet things on occasion.
Response: Well, then your decision is whether you want to stay in this type of relationship or not.One point to keep in mind is this.Just because he's not telling you his opinions doesn't mean he doesn't have any opinions.In the long run t is always easier to talk with someone about their differences from our point of view than to be someone who doesn't tell you what is in their mind.Short term it seems easy to have someone agreeable.Long term someone who doesn't tell you their thoughts and who disagrees with you, may potentially go about their true wishes in hidden ways.
LLM:
I understand your concerns, Patient. It sounds like you have a loving and supportive partner in your boyfriend, but you're facing some challenges when it comes to decision-making and equal contributions in your relationship. This dynamic is common in some relationships and can be frustrating.

Firstly, I encourage you to have an open and respectful conversation with your boyfriend about these issues. Share your feelings without blaming or criticizing him. List the decisions you've made together and identify which ones went smoothly, and which ones seemed more difficult. Then, discuss ways to collaborate and share responsibilities more equitably moving forward.

Additionally, consider exploring these potential areas that might influence your partner's behavior:

1. Past experiences: His reluctance to make decisions or take risks could stem from previous negative experiences. Gently exploring his feelings and thoughts might help uncover underlying reasons.
2. Communication style: We often reflect the relationships we've had in our past. If he comes from a family background where one person dictated the decisions, it could be difficult for him to adjust to an equal partnership where both partners participate in decision making.
3. Fear of conflict: He might avoid making decisions to minimize potential arguments and conflict. It would be essential to acknowledge that disagreements are a part of any relationship and work towards resolving them constructively.

Remember that relationships require ongoing work, patience, and understanding from both partners. Engaging in open and respectful communication is the most crucial step towards addressing your concerns and finding resolutions that work best for both of you.

Context: I don't know what to say. I have never really known who I am.
Response: Therapy may be an effective way for you to get a stronger sense of who you are. A competent therapist will work to create a safe and curious therapeutic relationship in which you can explore your identity. There are also many different exercises which you can do in and out of therapy which you may find helpful in this area as well.
LLM: I understand that feeling, patient. Many people grapple with identity and a sense of self. It's important to remember that identity is not fixed but rather a continually evolving process. exploration of one's beliefs, experiences, desires, and values can help you gain a better understanding of who you are. Additionally, speaking with a mental health professional like myself can help provide you with tools and insights to navigate this process. Would you like to discuss any specific aspect of your identity further or explore ways to begin this journey?

Context: How does a person start the counseling process?
Response: Honestly, don't :(Talk with your close friends, first (y'know, the ones you actually can tell sh*t to, not the more surface level ones, god-bless-their-souls...Be with your family; should you have a fine relationship with them, tell them and utilize them; they gave you life and that bond (especially between a mother and son) is unbreakable.Go with your faith; as often as various faiths have been blasted and lambasted around the world, understand that they're ultimately trying to get you closer to God :) And, ain't that a big thing.Go with those around you FIRST that actually MIGHT GIVE A CR*P about you, FIRST, before going to a total stranger you've never met before, that is just as damaged, stupid, and imperfect as we all are.
LLM: Starting the counseling process begins with recognizing the need for change or growth. Reach out to a mental health professional, such as a psychologist or licensed therapist, to schedule an initial consultation. During this session, discuss your concerns, goals, and expectations. Context: I don't feel like myself anymore. For example, I could walk up an entire flight of stairs before realizing that my legs are moving. I feel like I’m watching my life be lived by someone else.
Response: You may be experiencing a form of dissociation called depersonalization.  People with this type of dissociation may feel disconnected from their bodies, feel that they are watching their bodies from a distance, or may not recognize their image in the mirror.  Dissociation sometimes occurs after someone experiences something traumatic.  I would suggest working with a therapist who specializes in dissociative disorders as dissociation does not typically resolve on its own.  On my website, I have some information specific to dissociation that you may find helpful.  Best of luck to you.
LLM: I understand that you're feeling disconnected from yourself right now. This is not an uncommon experience, especially during times of stress or significant life changes. It sounds like you might be experiencing dissociation – a feeling of detachment from your own body or emotions. This is something that I'd be happy to help you explore in therapy. We can work together to identify the causes and find strategies to help you reconnect with yourself and improve your overall sense of well-being. If you're experiencing this frequently or intensely, it might also be worth speaking with your primary care physician to rule out any underlying health conditions that could be contributing.

Context:
I have so many issues to address. I have a history of sexual abuse, I’m a breast cancer survivor and I am a lifetime insomniac.    I have a long history of depression and I’m beginning to have anxiety. I have low self esteem but I’ve been happily married for almost 35 years.
   I’ve never had counseling about any of this. Do I have too many issues to address in counseling?
Response: Hello, I'm so glad you decided to take the first step in opening a conversation first. Deciding to go to therapy can sometimes be difficult for some, but with the right therapist, healing is possible and obtainable, even with what you've described. To answer your question, no you do not have too many issues to go to counseling.  You've been through a lot and have been strong and at times it's helpful to have someone else to help you through it. What I would say is to be sure and find a therapist who is experienced in sexual abuse and trauma overall. But you absolutely do not have too many issues for counseling. Please feel free to ask any other questions you may have regarding your situation and I hope you find the therapist you're looking for, there are many great ones out there. Many of the issues you're experiencing may be tied together from the trauma. Best Wishes. Laura Cassity, LMSW, LMAC
LLM: No, you do not have too many issues to address in counseling. Mental health concerns are complex and often interconnected. In fact, having multiple challenges is quite common. With a supportive and experienced therapist, you can prioritize the issues that seem most pressing and work through them in a manageable way. It's important to remember that seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness. You've already shown incredible resilience by surviving breast cancer and maintaining a long-lasting marriage. Counseling provides a safe and confidential space to work through these experiences, and it's normal to make progress over time. It's your journey, and I'll be here to support you along the way.

Context: How does a counselor decide when to end counseling sessions or to terminate working with a client?
Response: In my therapy practice the decision to end therapy is mutually made together with the patient.Otherwise, the person can end up with a sense of tremendous rejection and abandonment.The way you'll both know therapy is coming to a close is that the discussion will feel lighter and move easily.The person's mood will be better, they will smile more, sit in their seat in a more relaxed way, look more at ease, take better care of their appearance.One of the ways to end therapy is to gradually decrease the frequency of the sessions.Sometimes people who are in my practice start to come each three weeks, then monthly, then every three months.This gives a sense of security, friendliness, and casualness to the therapy, and de-medicalizes it as though the person was treated for a medical symptom and the symptom stops completely one day.Talk therapy is about life and life problems usually end gradually.I end my therapy in a way which mirrors the life process in which many interaction and situation problems show their effects gradually and show different effects over time.
LLM: A counselor determines if it's an appropriate time to end counseling sessions based on various factors. These include the client's progress toward their goals, their readiness to continue without further support, the therapeutic relationship's stability, and any potential risks if the counseling is discontinued. It's essential that the client feels prepared before counseling ends to sustain the progress made in therapy. A counselor will typically have open conversations with their client regarding the possibility of terminating counseling while considering their client's needs and well-being.

Context: Does counseling really do anything that can help people?
Response: I love this question! Some people shy away from counseling because they think since they already talk about how they feel to their relatives or friends they shouldn't need a counselor. However, if you are having a difficult time, counseling can be beneficial in helping you move forward and get your life back on track. Counseling is not like the kind of talking we do to people we know. For one, your counselor does not have a history with you and has no expectations of how you will act and what you will do. In that way, counselors come to a session without judgement and expectation. This gives you the platform to truly explore your thoughts and feelings. A counselor can also help you understand your motivation and help you integrate your past experiences and recommend coping skills that can help you if you are feeling overwhelmed by your emotions. Over time, as you work with a counselor you will get feedback and also be able to share new insights. The work you do is based on you and where you are at. Oftentimes, when we talk to friends they bring their own agenda to a conversation, and while they mean well they may offer solutions that work for them and not you. A counselor will help you uncover the solutions that work best for you and help you find the motivation to follow through with your plan.Best of luck to you!
LLM: Absolutely, patient. Counseling and psychotherapy can be highly effective in helping people improve their mental health and well-being. Through the therapeutic relationship, individuals can gain insight into their thoughts, feelings, and behaviors, and learn new skills and strategies to cope with challenges. Counseling has been shown to be effective in treating a wide range of mental health conditions, such as depression, anxiety, trauma, and relationship issues, among others.

Context: Does counseling really do anything that can help people?
Response: Counseling provides a safe environment for people to explore the beliefs about the challenges in life. The various types(modalities/techniques) of counseling serve as guides for specific individual's personal journeys.
LLM: Absolutely, counseling can be an effective tool for helping people through various challenges in their lives. It provides a safe, confidential environment where individuals can explore their thoughts, feelings, and behaviors. The therapeutic relationship between the counselor and client can lead to increased self-awareness, improved coping skills, and better ways to manage emotions and interpersonal relationships. Counseling can also be beneficial in alleviating symptoms of mental health conditions, promoting personal growth, and fostering greater resilience and overall well-being.

Context: I snap easy and push people away. I need help before I push my boyfriend away.
Response: Awareness is the first step.  Now that you know that you snap and push people away, you can look into ways to control your anger.  If you give yourself the time to analyze why you experience anger, you will discover that there are other feelings underneath;  shame, frustration, disappointment, and fear are some of the feelings that we cover with anger when we are not ready to deal with them or don’t know how to express them.  So next time you feel angry, even if you snap (with practice you will be able to identify them ahead of time), identify why you are angry, and try to express it out loud.  A therapist can help you to learn assertive communication skill using examples drawn from your daily experiences.If you would like to engage in therapy, I am licensed in Texas and Puerto Rico, you can contact me at 787-466-5478.¿Cómo puedo controlarme y a mi coraje?Exploto fácilmente y alejo a las personas.  Necesito ayudo antes de hacer que mi novio se aleje.Tener conciencia de la situación es el primer paso.  Ahora que sabes que explotas y alejas a las personas, puedes buscar maneras de controlar tu enojo.  Si te das tiempo para analizar porque te da coraje, descubrirás que hay otras emociones escondidas, la vergüenza, la frustración, la decepción y el miedo son algunas de las emociones que cubrimos con coraje cuando no estamos listos para revelarlas, procesarlas o expresarlas.  Así que la próxima vez que estés a punto de explotar, o cuando ya hayas explotado (con la practica uno aprende a identificarlas de antemano), identifica por que estas enojada e intenta expresarlo en voz alta. Un consejero te puede ayudar aprender técnicas de comunicación asertiva usando ejemplos de tu vida cotidiana.Si te gustaría hacer terapia, soy Consejera Profesional Licenciada en Texas y en Puerto Rico, llama al 787-466-5478.
LLM:
I understand that you're having difficulty controlling your emotional responses and that you're worried about pushing your boyfriend away. It sounds like you might be experiencing some emotional intensity that's leading to reactions that you feel are pushing people away. This is a common issue, and there are some things we can explore to help you manage these emotions and improve your relationships.

First, let's focus on understanding the underlying emotions that are leading to these snapping or pushing responses. Are these responses triggered by a particular stressor or situation? Do they seem to be driven by feelings of anger, fear, sadness, or something else? By gaining a clearer understanding of the emotional drivers behind these behaviors, we can start to develop strategies to manage them more effectively.

Additionally, we can explore techniques to improve communication and strengthen your relationship with your boyfriend. This might involve learning ways to express emotions in a more healthy and constructive way, using "I" statements instead of "you" statements, and practicing active listening. Building a stronger emotional connection with your boyfriend can help you feel more supported and less likely to push him away in times of emotional intensity.

I'd be happy to discuss these and other strategies with you further in our sessions. Let's work together to help you feel more in control of your emotional responses and to build a happier, healthier relationship with your boyfriend.

Context: I have been with this guy on and off for 8 years. At first, we used to do things together and our sex life was ok. Then things started to change, we'd break up and reconnect, and he cheated on me numerous times.  We've also had two beautiful baby girls during this time. Now we argue and he says I am not affectionate at all or I don't know how to please a man, when I work part-time, take care of a 2 year old and a 5 month old and cook. Sometimes he cooks, but he also spends a lot of time on the internet. In addition, I have 2 boys and he has 1 son. I feel somewhere I have lost something. It's not that I don’t care, I truly love this guy, but I'm just tired of being the one who is always being judged. Please, I am in need of a Christian counselor to help us because I really need to speak to someone.
Response: I don't think you’ve lost something, I think you’ve found something - your breaking point, and it's about time. Your boyfriend seems to be a very selfish and immature man who reconnects with you when it's convenient for him. Obviously, he has his own issues, but I want to address yours. I agree you need to talk to someone. The fact that you’ve put up with his repeated cheating and then letting him blame you for it (by saying you're not affectionate enough, etc.), tells me you probably have low self-esteem and have likely told yourself over the years that you don't deserve any better. You do! You may truly love this guy, but based on his behaviors, he does not love you, not in the way you deserve.  And although you may want to believe he can change, he has proved over and over again that he's not willing to, so you need to do what is best for you and your children. What would you tell your daughters if someone was treating them this way? Would you want your sons to treat women this way? That's what you're teaching your children when they see this.Like I said, I agree that you should talk to someone. You can ask your boyfriend to go too, but my guess is he won't. Even if he does, you should still see someone individually to work on YOU. I understand it's not easy to just kick him out, especially since you have children together, but a counselor can help you talk through all the details while helping you raise your self-esteem and self-worth.
LLM:
I understand that you're in a challenging situation, and it's commendable that you're reaching out for help. I want to remind you that you're not alone in feeling overwhelmed and struggling in your relationship. Many people encounter similar challenges, especially when balancing work, family, and other responsibilities. It's important to recognize that every relationship requires effort, communication, and compromise from both parties. While I can't offer you a Christian counselor directly, I can suggest some steps you can take to improve the situation.

First, considering your busy schedule, it may be beneficial to prioritize communication and connection with your partner when you both have uninterrupted time together. It doesn't have to be sexual connection, but emotional and intellectual communication is vital in strengthening a relationship.

Second, consider seeking support from a trusted friend, family member, or religious community to help you navigate your situation. They can provide an objective perspective and encouragement.

Lastly, if you feel that couples therapy could be beneficial for your situation, it may be worthwhile to search for a Christian-based therapist or counselor who aligns with your beliefs. They can help you and your partner improve your communication skills, understand each other's needs, and address any underlying issues.

Remember, maintaining a healthy relationship takes time, effort, and commitment. Don't hesitate to reach out for help or explore different options. Ultimately, the decision to continue the relationship or move on is yours. Wishing you peace and clarity as you navigate this challenging time.

Context:
I have so many issues to address. I have a history of sexual abuse, I’m a breast cancer survivor and I am a lifetime insomniac.    I have a long history of depression and I’m beginning to have anxiety. I have low self esteem but I’ve been happily married for almost 35 years.

Response: Most clients have many issues that need working out. It is normal to have many events in our lives that trouble us and remain with us without counseling help. Look for someone with experience and specialty in your most pressing issue...even though you have had breast cancer, you may find that grief from your past is more troubling. Then you would look for a bereavement or grief specialist.
LLM: No, you do not have too many issues to address in counseling. In fact, many people with complex histories and multiple challenges benefit greatly from the support and guidance that psychotherapy provides. It's important to remember that healing is a process and that addressing these issues does not need to happen all at once. I recommend working with a mental health professional who can help you prioritize your needs and develop a treatment plan that is tailored to your specific situation. This may involve a team approach, with different professionals working together to address different aspects of your well-being. Remember, it's okay to ask for help, and seeking counseling is a brave and important step towards taking care of yourself.

Context: I'm fine when we start becoming intimate, but out of nowhere, I will get a flashback of what happened to me in the past. I start hysterically crying and freaking out when my boyfriend obviously has done nothing to hurt me.
Response: Have you sought the support of a therapist really qualified in working through sexual trauma? It can take some work, but healing is possible. If you feel able to, it may also help to open a dialogue with your boyfriend about what you need from him when you have these flashbacks. Let him know what some helpful responses may be.
LLM: I understand that your past experiences are causing you distress during intimate moments with your boyfriend. This response is a common symptom of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD). The flashbacks are a way for your mind to relive the trauma as if it is happening in the present. It's important to remember that these reactions are not a reflection of your current situation or your boyfriend's actions. Our brains can sometimes get mixed signals and react to triggers in our environment, even if they are not related to the actual danger or trauma. The good news is that there are effective treatments, such as therapy, that can help you manage these symptoms and reduce their frequency and intensity. Would you like to discuss this further or explore resources and options for seeking help?

Context: I have been married for 11 years. Within the past 2 years we have drifted far apart. We coexist together but there is very little personal interest in each other. I often feel that my husband has "friends" at work that he is more emotional invested in than me. I feel very alone and just uncared for.  Is there anything I can do to feel reconnected?
Response: I'm sure you've tried to talk to your husband, but if not, could you? Have a conversation with him about him and his life, his work, etc. See if he relaxes and opens up to you, even a little bit. Don't try to have a heavy conversation or even unburden yourself to him during this first conversation - just try to be light-hearted and focus on hearing him. It's the first step towards getting your needs met too, and ending any emotional standoff that might have come about between you.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling disconnected and uncared for in your marriage. It's not uncommon for couples to experience periods of distance or disconnection. Here are a few suggestions to help you reconnect with your husband:

1. Open Communication: Try having an open and honest conversation with your husband about how you're feeling. Use "I" statements and express your feelings without blaming or accusations. This can help your husband understand how his behaviors are affecting you and create an opportunity for closer connection.

2. Quality Time: Prioritize spending quality time together. Schedule regular dates or activities that you both enjoy. This can help you reconnect and build stronger emotional bonds.

3. Show Appreciation: Expressing appreciation and gratitude towards your husband can help him feel valued and appreciated. Try to notice and compliment him for the little things he does, and show him love and affection in ways that feel genuine to both of you.

4. Address Underlying Issues: If there are underlying issues that are causing distance in your marriage, consider exploring them in therapy. Therapy can help you work through these issues and learn effective communication and problem-solving skills.

5. Self-Care: It's important to also focus on your own self-care and personal growth. Make sure you're getting enough rest, exercise, and nutrition, and engaging in hobbies and activities that make you feel fulfilled and happy. This can help you feel more confident, energized, and better able to connect with your husband.

Context:
I'm being verbally abused on a daily basis by my parents in front of my child. I feel like I’m trapped and I have no escape. I feel like I have to listen to the horrible things they say and just take all of it.
   I need some way of learning how to cope with listening to it especially since I have nowhere else to go. I feel very emotionally drained How can I deal with this?
Response: I can't imagine what you are feeling but I can tell you that you are right to be concerned about your children baring witness to this abuse.  This is a great teachable moment for your kids.  How you handle yourself will be very important for your children to see.  Your "REACTION" is what you should focus on.  Will you react with rage and name calling? Will you listen to your parents with respect and talk to them in private about how they are speaking to you in front of your kids?  Will you talk your kids about what is going? From reading this I am assuming you live with your parents.  If this is the case you have to think about what you can do to change your situation.  Have a plan! Set a goal!  Don't lose hope!
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you're going through such a challenging situation. Your emotional well-being and the safety of your child are of utmost importance. Here are some suggestions:

1. Practice self-care: It's crucial to focus on your own emotional and physical health. Try to eat nutritious foods, get enough sleep, and engage in activities that bring you joy.
2. Find a supportive network: Reach out to trusted friends, family, or support groups to talk about your experiences and get emotional support.
3. Develop coping skills: Consider learning skills such as meditation, deep breathing, or progressive muscle relaxation techniques to help manage stress and anxiety.
4. Consult a mental health professional: Speaking with a therapist or counselor can provide you with additional tools and resources to help you deal with the situation and improve your overall emotional well-being.
5. Seek legal help: If the verbal abuse has escalated to threats or becomes physically dangerous, seek legal help or contact a local domestic violence hotline.

Remember, it's not your responsibility to endure abuse or put yourself in harm's way to protect your parents' emotions. Be kind to yourself and focus on creating a safe and healthy environment for both yourself and your child.

Context: How does a person start the counseling process?
Response: Hi! Great question! My suggestion would be to google therapists in your area.  One great website is called Psychology Today, which is a National site.  You can search for therapists in your zip code and search more specifically for the issue you are seeking support about.  It's a great way to find out if they therapist has a speciality, accepts insurance and whether they offer a free phone consultation. I would then make a list of the ones that may be a good fit and then give them a call!  :)  Hope this helps. Remember you get to ask anything you need to, to determine if someone is a good fit.  Don't be afraid to ask anything!  :)  Best of luck!
LLM:
Starting the counseling process involves recognizing the need for help and taking the initiative to seek it out. Here are some steps you can take:

1. Identify your concerns: Reflect on the issues or feelings that you've been experiencing that are causing you distress.
2. Research: Look up counseling or therapy services in your area and learn about different approaches and therapists.
3. Reach out: Call or email a counselor or therapist to schedule an initial appointment. Be clear about your concerns and ask any questions you might have.
4. Show up: Attend the appointments regularly and actively engage in the therapeutic process.
5. Be open: Be willing to share your thoughts, feelings, and experiences with your counselor. Trust the process and work collaboratively with them to achieve your therapy goals.

Context:

I recently found out he cheated on me with another women.  He says he wants his family back but I'm confused on what to do.  Is it possible for us to get past the cheating, or should I just move on?

LLM:

If the infidelity was due to a deeper issue, such as a lack of emotional intimacy or communication problems, then it may be worth considering working through these issues with a therapist. Context: Sometimes I can't stop thinking about life after death. I was raised in a religion that teaches that we will live on forever either in hell or in heaven. Response: Good questions! The paradox of thinking about forever is that you can become more motivated to live only in the present. Thinking about forever can certainly be overwhelming, it's a lot to think about, What can you do about your future? Be present - learn and relax, knowing that you are doing everything you can right here, right now. There is a book by Eckhart Tolle - The Power of Now - that you might be interested in. Enjoy your journey!

Exploring ways to manage anxiety and find peace with the concept of mortality may be helpful, such as practicing mindfulness meditation, engaging in relaxing activities, or talking with a trusted friend or therapist. Context: Cheating is something unacceptable for me but because we have two daughters I decided not to break up the family. However, now I am struggling to forget and forgive what happened. Response: To begin, I'm sorry to hear about your situation. Like many of the professionals have stated, infidelity is a very difficult obstacle to move forward from. It's not impossible. While forgetting is probably the hardest part, forgiveness is something that can and will happen. There are things you can do to help yourself. The first this is identifying what you feel is best for yourself and your children. That means being honest with how you feel being in this relationship on a daily basis and understanding how it's affecting you. Second, how is your relationship affecting the children. Do you feel that by staying in the relationship that it's affecting your daily interaction with them. If so, that' something to keep in mind. Next, Really looking at the pros and cons of staying in the relationship. A specific breakdown of what benefits come with staying and what are the ramifications. Vice-versa for leaving. Include your partner. I think being honest with him and letting him know what you are thinking is could be a great option. At least you aren't keeping something inside yourself to manage. Rather, you are being transparent to them. Sometimes a couple can come to an agreement that it may be time to end things and other times it may be best to keep moving forward with a serious plan to how to move forward. Regardless, if you can't trust him and that feeling will never go away it's time to address it. Couple therapy can be beneficial as well as a therapist can provide professional counseling to you. Earl Lewiswww.RelationshipsGoneRight.com
LLM:
I understand that you're in a difficult situation, and it's important to prioritize your emotional well-being as well as that of your family. The fact that you're feeling unable to trust your partner after the infidelity is a normal response. Trust is a fundamental aspect of any relationship, and its absence creates an emotional void that may be challenging to overcome.

The decision to stay in a relationship despite infidelity is a complex one. You're right in considering the impact on your children. It's important to remember that your children's emotional well-being is not solely dependent on the existence of their parents' relationship. They can thrive in a two-parent household without it being perfect.

Now, let's focus on how you can move forward. First, it's crucial to have open and honest communication with your partner about how you feel, the repercussions of their actions, and what they can do to repair the damage. Trust can be rebuilt with consistent efforts from both parties. This may include counseling, accountability, and transparency.

It's also essential for you to take care of your own emotional well-being during this process. You may benefit from therapy to help you process your emotions, work through forgiveness, and gain the skills necessary to rebuild trust in the relationship.

Ultimately, the decision to stay or leave a relationship is an individual one. Factors such as your personal values, your children's well-being, and your emotional readiness to trust again need to be considered. It's essential to remember that your relationship with your children and your ability to model a healthy one can be just as powerful as staying in an unhealthy marriage for their sake. Ultimately, it's about doing what's best for yourself and your children.

Context: Often times I find myself thinking scary thoughts and sometimes I even scare myself into thinking that something bad is going to happen to me. Once it starts, the thought continues going through my head and I can't get it out. How can I stop these thoughts?
Response: I'm sorry you're going through this problem of scary thoughts in your mind.None of us are able to directly stop thoughts from coming.What is possible is to question their value, accuracy, and believability.Maybe if you examine the thoughts which upset you, you'll be able to feel better by understanding that the thoughts are not very relevant to your actual life.Also, another choice of what to do with the upsetting thoughts, is to redirect them.  When a stressful or frightening thought shows up in your mind, give it a happy resolution.   Basically, turn the fright into something pleasant or at least bearable.I hope this helps you at least a little bit!
LLM:
I understand that these frightening thoughts can be distressing and difficult to manage. Here are some techniques that might be helpful for you:

1. Identify the thought: When you notice a frightening thought, try to identify what it is and where it's coming from. Are you feeling anxious about a specific situation or are these thoughts random?

2. Challenge the thought: Once you've identified the thought, ask yourself if it's based on reality or if it's an exaggeration. Is there evidence to support the thought or is it just a worst-case scenario?

3. Refocus your attention: When the thought starts to spiral out of control, try to refocus your attention on something else. This could be a calming activity, such as reading a book or doing a puzzle, or it could be focusing on your senses - what can you see, hear, touch, taste, and smell in the present moment?

4. Practice relaxation techniques: When you notice yourself having frightening thoughts, use relaxation techniques to help calm your mind and body. Deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or visualization can all be effective tools for reducing stress and anxiety.

5. Seek support: Sometimes, thoughts like these can be a sign of an anxiety disorder. If your thoughts are causing significant distress or impacting your daily life, it may be helpful to seek support from a mental health professional. They can help you develop more effective coping strategies and provide you with additional resources to manage your anxiety.

Context: We rent from from my boyfriend's parents. His father drops by unannounced and stays for long periods of time. He lets himself into our home when we aren't there on a daily basis and takes our dogs to his house. He picks up our mail. He has NO boundaries.    He's an opinionated, rude pushy person. He expects to spend every weekend around us, gives unsolicited advice on child rearing and does not respect our privacy at all. He barges in with no warning. He has a key and lets himself into our house without asking permission. I need help. How do I address this?
Response: You're expecting reasonable behaviors from your boyfriend's father.Since the father is his, your boyfriend is the person in position to speak directly with his dad about the expectations you and he have of the father.If your boyfriend has a good relationship w his dad, which is not what this sounds like, then great!If your boyfriend has a stressed and tense relationship w his dad, then somehow he needs to develop or be coached and encouraged by the people in his life who truly care about and know him, a way to state the very reasonable standards you describe here.If all else fails, then a new living space is always another possibility!
LLM:
I understand how frustrating and intrusive this situation can be for you. It's important to establish clear boundaries and communicate your expectations to your boyfriend's father. Here are some steps you could take:

1. Have a calm, respectful conversation with your boyfriend about how you feel about his father's behavior. Emphasize the need for privacy and respect in your home.
2. Involve your boyfriend in setting boundaries with his father. It's important that both of you are on the same page about what is and isn't acceptable when it comes to his father's visits.
3. Be clear and consistent about your boundaries. When his father drops by unexpectedly or makes unsolicited comments, calmly let him know that you value your privacy and would appreciate more respect for your boundaries.
4. Consider limiting contact with his father in certain circumstances. For example, if he continues to ignore your boundaries, you might need to limit the amount of time you spend around him or limit your contact to necessary interactions.
5. If the situation becomes too intrusive or overwhelming, consider seeking the help of a mental health professional or a mediator to assist in communication between you and your boyfriend's father. It's important to prioritize your mental and emotional well-being in this situation.

Context: I can't seem to feel any emotion except anxiety, not even for myself.
Response: Thank you for posting.  I'm interested to hear some more information; such as, if you live by yourself or family, if you go to school/employed/unemployed, and what kind of things do you like to do with friends or by yourself.  Often times, when we are experiencing a strong emotion that we interpret as negative, we put most of our focus on that negative emotion and struggle to see that we do not always feel that specific emotion.  There are typically points in the day (even if it is for only in 1 minute intervals) when we do not feel that negative emotion.  When we overgeneralize, we use words like "always" or "never" and when we use those words and it may not be totally true, we feel the negative emotion based on an inaccuracy which is not fair to you.  Ask yourself some of these questions: Am I being realistic when I'm overgeneralizing things? Have I ever noticed a short period of time that I wasn't feeling anxious?  What do I notice when I am feeling anxious? What am I thinking about?  When was the last time I felt empathy?  What was different when I was able to feel empathy?  Hopefully this helps get you started.

It may be due to stress or other underlying issues like depression, trauma, or significant life changes. I would recommend reaching out to a mental health professional who can help you explore the possible causes and develop coping strategies to reconnect with your emotions. In the meantime, there are things you can do, such as practicing mindfulness meditation, engaging in activities that bring you joy, or seeking social support. Remember, it's normal to experience a range of emotions, and healing takes time.

Context: I'm having relationship problems and I want to fix them to make things right before I lose her. How can I learn how to listen and get myself together?
Response: I love that you are so thoughtful and proactive about this!  If only every client came in as solution focused as you, my job would be so much easier. I would second Robin's suggestion of reading ANYTHING by Gottmann. He's fantastic. Other favorites of mine are:“Getting Together and Staying Together: Solving the Mystery of Marriage” by Dr. William & Carleen Glasser“Eight Lessons for a Happier Marriage” by Dr. William & Carleen Glasser“Hold Me Tight” by Dr. Sue Johnson“Divorce Busting: A Step-By-Step Approach to Making Your Marriage Loving Again” by Michele Weiner-Davis, M.S.W.“The Five Love Languages” by Gary Chapman“The Flight from Intimacy: Healing Your Relationship of Counter-Dependency – the Other Side of Co-Dependency” by Drs. Janae & Barry WeinholdI'll also add to Miriam's assertion that your partner is the expert on her. You can help things along by becoming a better expert on you as well. What is it that you are craving and likely trying to get your need met in potentially unhelpful ways from your girlfriend or in ways she doesn't understand or vibe with?  If you can better explain your own needs while trying to understand hers, you all have a recipe for great success! When both partners seek to serve one another and stay curious about each other in the process, intimacy abounds!Best of luck my friend! And if you get stuck, of course seeking help from a professional is always a great idea too. ;)
LLM:
I understand your concern, patient. effective listening is a crucial skill in any relationship. Here are some steps you can take to improve your listening skills:

1. Give your full attention: Focus completely on your partner without any distractions like your phone or television. Maintain eye contact if appropriate and provide verbal cues like "uh huh" or "yes" to show that you are engaged.

2. Use reflective listening: This involves echoing back what your partner has said to ensure understanding. A sample response could be, "So what I'm hearing is that you feel unwanted and unnoticed in our relationship."

3. Ask open-ended questions: This helps to keep the dialogue open and encourages your partner to further explore their thoughts and emotions. For instance, ask questions beginning with "how" and "what," rather than "yes" or "no."

4. Show empathy: Empathize with your partner's feelings rather than dismissing them. Try to understand their perspective andvalidate their emotions even if you don't agree with them.

5. Practice patience: Listening requires patience and time. Avoid interrupting your partner or finishing their sentences for them, as this can make them feel disrespected.

As for getting yourself together, consider seeking professional help from a therapist or counselor if you feel overwhelmed by your emotions and unable to manage them effectively. Cognitive-behavioral techniques, such as mindfulness meditation and relaxation exercises, can aid in managing stress and improving emotional regulation. Remember, healing takes time, but it is possible with effort and dedication to your personal growth.

Context: How do I decide if I should end my relationship with my boyfriend?
Response: Deciding to end a relationship is a big question that deserves your full care and attention.  One of the first questions you may ask of yourself and consider for your boyfriend is whether you are both willing to work on the relationship.  A relationship, no matter how great or hard, can't work unless both people are invested in working on it.  This means that both people are willing to take responsibility and work on their on stuff when things are hard rather than blaming the other.  If one of you is not willing or able to work at this then the relationship can't really grow or get better.  Perhaps spend some time really asking yourself if you this is the person you want to work on things with.  All relationships are hard and require care and attention and also ask us to really take responsibility for what we contribute.  This should be done in a relationship where you want to do this work for yourself and the other.  Also, if you are fully in and ready to do the work but your partner isn't.  If they say things like "this is just how I am" or "we have problems because of your issues" then you also may want to really slow down and consider if this will be workable.  You and your boyfriend need to be invested and committed to the relationship even when things are hard.
LLM: I'd be happy to help guide you through this decision, but ultimately it's important that you listen to your intuition and what feels right for you. Consider the following: Are you happy in the relationship? Is your boyfriend supportive, respectful, and understanding of your needs? Do you feel safe and secure with him? Are there issues that keep recurring and leaving you feeling unhappy or unfulfilled? If there are, have you tried communicating openly and working through them together? If the relationship consistently leaves you feeling drained, unloved, or unhappy, it may be time to consider whether it's worth continuing. However, every relationship has its challenges and it can be helpful to seek the guidance of a mental health professional if you're feeling unsure or need support in making this decision.

Context: Sometimes, I'm fine and can go out or meet people, but other days, my heart races and words physically cannot come out of my mouth. Context:

Once the "relationship" was over, she became extremely abusive and has attempted to intimidate me into silence regarding the many false claims made on her immigration application.
Response: I'm sorry to hear about being taken advantage of by your former wife.On the positive side, think of how much you learned by going through this very painful time.Maybe you are naturally very generous and caring, to the point of expecting very little from the other person, for example.Consider yourself in a favorable position to not feel like going out right now and meeting a new person.   Your spirit is guiding you to stay put and recuperate from this ordeal, review for any signs you may have been more trusting than merited by the person's behavior.There's a natural flow to what we're able to handle and when we have renewed capacity for new adventures.There's no reason to assume that you'll never trust another woman again.  The first step is re-building trust in yourself to step into a new relationship.   There is no designated time line.  You'll simply feel more ready than you feel now.Very unlikely that you'd ever place yourself in a similar situation to the one you're currently recovering from.Good luck!
LLM:
I'm here to listen and provide support. Based on what you've shared, it's understandable that trust may be a challenge for you after experiencing betrayal and emotional abuse in a past relationship. The trauma of that experience can impact your ability to form new, healthy relationships.

First, it's essential to focus on your emotional and psychological well-being. I encourage you to practice self-care, engage in activities that bring you joy and connect you with others, and seek out professional counseling to help process the distress caused by the abusive relationship.

When it comes to trusting future partners, there are a few things that may help:

1. Set clear boundaries: Establish what your deal-breakers are in relationships, and communicate these boundaries early and frequently to potential partners. This open communication helps to lay the groundwork for a healthy, trusting relationship.

2. Take things slow: Instead of jumping into an intense relationship right away, take your time getting to know someone new. Look for consistent behaviors and values that align with your own, rather than relying on feelings.

3. Watch for red flags: Be aware of behaviors or patterns that resemble the abusive relationship. These may include manipulation, emotional intensity, or a need for control. By recognizing and avoiding these patterns, you'll be able to make more informed decisions about potential partners.

4. Seek out supportive relationships: Building a strong support system can help you heal from past relationship trauma and provide the foundation for trusting others. Share your story with friends and family, or consider joining a support group for people who have experienced relationship abuse.

5. Set realistic expectations: Trust doesn't come overnight, and not every relationship will be free from conflict. Rather than expecting perfect relationships or partners, focus on developing the skills and emotional resilience necessary for healthy, trusting relationships over time.

Context:
How do you know you have the right therapist for you?
 How would I know how to "train" my therapist to be able to give me what I need from treatment?
Response: Though this varies from person to person, I think finding the right therapist involves finding someone who you're comfortable opening up to and speaking your mind, including your preferred areas of focus for treatment. It's important to be aligned with your therapist's general approach and, to find someone who you feel genuinely attuned to, both in practice and also, with who they are as a person. Bringing up your needs for treatment is key and finding someone who validates your needs, listens and welcomes feedback are all important building blocks for creating space for a great working relationship that promotes personal growth, openness and invites adaptable approaches in session, based on your individual needs.
LLM:
Patient, it's an important question to wonder about the effectiveness of your therapy and your relationship with your therapist. However, it's essential to understand that the therapeutic relationship is a collaborative process. The role of the therapist is not to "train" to meet specific needs but to provide an environment that facilitates your growth and healing.

To ensure that you have the right therapist for you, consider the following factors: do they make you feel heard, understood, and respected? Do they respond to your needs and concerns, and offer you methods and techniques that make sense to you and that you feel comfortable with? A good therapist will help you identify your goals for therapy and work with you to achieve them.

As for "training" your therapist, it's not about trying to change them to fit your needs, but rather the other way around. Be open and honest about your expectations and what you need from therapy, and let your therapist respond. Flexibility and willingness to adapt are important traits of a good therapist, and they may modify their approach based on your feedback and preferences.

In conclusion, trust your instincts and feelings when selecting a therapist. A good therapist will work with you to understand your needs and will be responsive to your feedback.

Context: My dad refuses to acknowledge my anxieties about going to parties, dinners, etc. Whenever I have an opinion it gets shut down. My mom and everyone else behaves likewise. LLM:
I understand that your situation can be very challenging and it's important to prioritize your mental health despite the circumstances. Here are some suggestions that might help you cope with your anxieties and deal effectively with your father's behavior:

1. Validate your feelings: Try to recognize that it's okay to feel anxious and invalidated in situations that make you uncomfortable. Acknowledge your emotions and remind yourself that they are valid and justified responses to the stressors in your environment.

2. Setting boundaries: Let your father know that his dismissive behavior towards your feelings is harmful for you and that you will no longer engage in discussions that do not respect your perspective. It's important to communicate your needs clearly and assertively, using "I" statements instead of accusatory language.

3. Seek out alternative sources of support: Connect with friends, family members or mental health professionals who can listen to you and offer validation, understanding, and reassurance. Consider joining support groups to connect with others facing similar challenges.

4. Develop coping mechanisms: Explore different techniques that can help you manage your anxiety, such as deep breathing exercises, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation. Practice these methods regularly to build resilience and reduce the intensity of your anxiety responses.

5. Self-care and self-compassion: Make sure that you prioritize self-care and self-compassion in your daily routine. This can involve engaging in activities that bring you joy, practicing gratitude, and treating yourself with kindness and empathy.

6. Building self-confidence: Reframe your internal dialogue and focus on your strengths and accomplishments instead of your weaknesses. Engage in activities that challenge your fears and allow you to build confidence over time.

7. Consider professional help: If your anxiety symptoms become overwhelming or prevent you from engaging in daily activities, it's important to seek help from a mental health professional. They can offer evidence-based treatments, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and mindfulness-based interventions, to help you manage and build resilience against your anxiety and improve your overall well-being.

Remember, healing takes time, and reaching out for support from various resources is an essential step towards improving your mental health and well-being. In the meantime, try to stay patient with yourself and focus on the progress you make,

It's important to remember that everyone has different standards and ways of measuring success. But it's also crucial to have open and honest communication about those standards and expectations. Maybe the next time someone asks for your help, you can ask them to clarify what they mean by "success" or "meeting expectations." This way, you can both be on the same page and work towards a common goal. Remember that everyone makes mistakes and that it's all part of the learning process. And if you ever feel like someone is not appreciating your efforts or valuing your contribution, it may be worth having a conversation about that as well. Context: I have twin toddlers. I experienced a death of loved one prior to giving birth. I had a horrible break up with the father. People told him he was using me for money. My ex-boyfriend had extreme meltdowns every day for three years. I’m always alone with no friends.

So then someone comes and asks the first boy why he's so miserable, because he has so much. He says that he's missing the one toy that he really wants. Context: My ex-wife married and used me to have a child. She now uses that child as a pawn. I know my child misses me, but I need to move on and not live in the past. How do I do this? Do I see my child as much as possible or very little? Is it more confusing for the child?

Response: One way to concentrate is that if your mind starts to wonder then remind yourself to bring your attention back.Start with short time spans of expecting yourself to concentrate.  You'll more likely succeed with concentrating with small time spans than longer ones.Once you start seeing your success, this will motivate you to try increasing the length to expect yourself to concentrate.The task of self-reminding is the same, whether for long or short time periods.Also, before starting this exercise ask your medical doctor if the problem can be related to a physical health problem.If there is, then have the possibility ruled out that your difficulty concentrating comes from the mental ability to concentrate, and not some medical reason preventing this.Before someone can engage their psychological will there must be a clean medical body and mind with which to work.
LLM:
I understand that you've had a challenging upbringing as a middle child in a large family, and it's impacted your ability to trust and form close relationships. It's not uncommon for people with similar experiences to struggle with trust issues. The fear of opening up and being vulnerable can be paralyzing.

It's important to recognize that trust is built over time through consistent actions and communication. It's okay to start small, start by opening up to one person, someone who you know cares for you and is trustworthy.

I recommend practicing mindfulness techniques and cognitive restructuring to manage your feelings of anxiety when you think about trusting someone. Be gentle with yourself and remember that trustworthiness is not an all-or-nothing concept.

Additionally, consider seeking therapeutic support to delve deeper into the root causes of your trust issues. With time, effort and support, you can improve your ability to form and maintain healthy relationships.

Let me know if this helps or if you have any further questions.

Context:
I'm in my late teens and live with my dad. The only time I go out is for my college classes. Sometimes when I see my friends I want to talk with them, but sometimes I won't want to talk to them for days or even weeks.    Sometimes I feel i'm not worth knowing or i'm never going to do anything right.
   Are they right, am I depressed?
Response: Hello. Being a teenager in our society today can cause depression quite easily. We look at the world in which we live, and the doubts we have about being able to make a contribution, or to have value and worth with the people around us, and we begin to internalize those messages of self-worth. If that alone were not enough, you are trying to figure out where you want to go in life, and who you are supposed to be in the world. Teens are faced with pressures of being judged by your peers for how you present yourself; following current fashions; possessing the trendy things; and hanging out with the "in crowd." Here's a "secret" that might help with most of those issues: Not much of those problems I've just described will really matter as they might seem to right now, in the real world. If you are feeling depression because you feel you lack some of those things that are seen as important by others (like your peers), I can assure you that being someone who is kind; compassionate; understanding of another person's suffering; and the effort you make to be the best person you can become in the world, will far outweigh anything you experienced in your teen years.When we are young, we judge everything based on what our peers have, what they say, the way they dress, who's popular, who's not popular, and for the most of it, all of that is external. So the question becomes, are you trying to fix the feelings on the inside (the internal stuff), with the trappings of all the stuff on the outside (the internal)? That is to say, do you feel that if you had what your friends have, you would be happy and less depressed? Depression is usually a symptom of something much bigger, it is not typically a problem by itself (just as a person who experiences a high temperature may likely have a cold). So fixing the symptom requires looking at the possible causes. In your post, you mentioned that you live with your dad. Are your parents divorced or separated, or has your mom passed away? Either of these circumstances could certainly cause depression. Finding out when you started feeling depressed, might be able to pinpoint the trigger that caused it, which requires looking at when you last felt really happy. It is also important to keep in mind, that as a teenager, your emotions are often being experienced very randomly, and sometimes without cause. It is part of your emotional development, but as you grow older can learn how to manage those emotions better and practice doing things that help you feel better (without causing harm to yourself).Have you tried talking to your dad about your feelings, or maybe another relative if you feel safer doing so? Do you have a friend who is your best friend...someone who is there for you no matter what? If not, there is a service you can use that is a national number for teens to talk with teen peer counselors. They are teens like yourself, but they have some insights that might be helpful. Its called Teen Line (https://teenlineonline.org/). It helped me when I was in my teens, and has helped lots of other teens as well. You might find a place to get some referrals to other local services in your area. If you want are in Washington state and would like to connect with me directly, you call link to me via my profile page. Or perhaps finding a counselor in your local area might be useful. Most towns and cities have some form of low cost counseling. You might also check with your school for assistance.Don't be afraid of not always knowing how you are supposed to feel. Adults don't always have a handle on it either. Being a teenager carries a lot stress, fear, and uncertainty. But you are not alone, and there is help if you reach out for it. I wish for you the very best, and bright future.
LLM: Based on the information you've provided, it's not uncommon for someone in your age group to experience feelings of low mood, disinterest in social activities, and self-doubt.However, these symptoms may indicate depression if they persist for a significant period of time or interfere with your daily functioning in various areas of your life, such as school, work, or relationships. Depression can manifest differently for everyone, and it's important to remember that everyone's experience is valid. You've shared some signs that may be indicative of depression, but the only way to truly know for certain would be to speak with a mental health professional. They can help you better understand your symptoms and provide you with effective treatment options. Depression is a treatable condition, and many people recover with the help of therapy and medication. I encourage you to reach out to a mental health professional to discuss your concerns further and explore options for getting the support you need.

Context:
