But now I can never hang out with my friends at my house or invite them over because it would be around my parents and my friends would see there is nothing there on my chest. 

How do I fix this?
Response: I get how you feel; you wish your body looked different and you're embarrassed that you tried to make it look different and people may notice. You don't say how old you are. It's likely that, if you are young, your body will change as you mature, but in general, we're given the bodies we're given and I'm glad to see that there may be a desire in you to accept your body the way it is. You seem to want to stop disguising it. Bodies come in all shapes and sizes and every woman knows how it feels to struggle with accepting what her's looks like. Many men go through this as well, although women are more often targeted with cultural messages about how we're supposed to look. Young people often believe that others are noticing and judging them. In fact, other people are far more preoccupied with who might be judging them than whatever's going on with you. I suggest you wear whatever bra you want to wear, and if someone is rude enough to comment on it, you can ignore them and talk about the weather or the latest pop song that you love and they'll get the hint. But my bet is they won't even notice. :)
LLM:
It's important to understand that the value of a person is not determined by their physical appearance. Your friends most likely value and appreciate you for who you are, not just for how you look. You mentioned that you've been wearing a bra that's too big to give the illusion of larger breasts around your peers, but this behavior may be causing you distress when it comes to inviting friends over to your house.

Let's explore some coping strategies together. One idea would be to focus on the many aspects of your personality and the talents you possess that make you unique. If you're feeling insecure about your appearance, try practicing self-acceptance. This can involve setting positive self-statements, focusing on your inner values, and practicing self-care routines. Additionally, consider addressing any underlying feelings of inadequacy that may be contributing to this behavior. It may be helpful to seek support from a therapist who can assist you in working through these emotions and building your self-worth. Remember, it's normal for young people to feel self-conscious at times, and it's an opportunity to learn and grow.

Context: I've recently thought that i could be transgender but I've never had the mind of a girl.
Response: Hello Frostproof,Thinking about becoming transgender and being transgender are slightly different.  This is a topic that is best discussed with a therapist that specializes in LGBTQ issues.  From what you have written it sounds as if you still have some confusion as to where you fit in with the "labeling system".  While labels provide a means to quickly identify and classify, they can also feel restrictive or confining.  Instead of focusing on fitting into a specific classification, explore who you are as a person and celebrate your positive qualities and those things that make you uniquely you!  There are many options for you to find a therapist and e-therapy is also an option.  You can try Breakthrough an online service.   Here is some reading for refection that you may find helpful.  Yin Yang Woman Man http://dld.bz/ejVK2  What does it mean to be a woman or a man?
LLM: It's important to understand that being transgender is about feeling a deep sense of identity as a man or a woman, and experiencing discomfort or distress about the sex you were assigned at birth. Gender identity is not necessarily related to your gender expression or the roles you take on in society. Your feelings of gender are valid and it's not necessary to fit a certain stereotype to be transgender. You may find it helpful to explore your gender identity further, possibly through talking with other transgender individuals or a therapist who specializes in gender identity issues. They can provide you with more accurate and detailed information and support you in your journey of self-discovery. Remember, it's a personal exploration and you'll come to understand your identity in your own time and way.

Context: I have known her for years. She was dating my brother-in-law when we met. My kids think of her as their aunt. On Halloween 2014, I lost my mom to cancer. My mom and dad were still married when she passed away. My friend was there for me through that and my own cancer diagnosis. She has been a very big part of both me and my kids’ life, but now last month, my dad told me that he really likes my friend and wants to marry her. She’s like a sister to me. My kids hate the idea.
Response: How are you measuring whether or not your dad understands your wish?Your dad understanding your wish does not mean he will necessarily follow through with what you want.Based on what you describe about your relationship to the woman in question, your discomfort with the idea of your dad marrying your friend, is reasonable.Have the conversation with your dad that includes your viewpoint, and also ask him for his impression of your feelings.Its even possible he already has considered your feelings and hasn't yet told you.It is also possible that a conversation on the subject, as well as your dad following through with his decision to marry your best friend, may develop in a very positive way.Part of the current uneasiness you feel may be from fear of how a change in circumstances and introducing new relationship dynamics might feel.Given that the person who died was your mom, maybe your emotions are not yet ready seeing your dad with a replacement spouse. Try to see the situation from the point of everyone, including yourself, who is involved in this major change.  Maybe having a family discussion about acceptance and readiness to accept a new partner for your dad, would open new empathy for all of these family members.
LLM:
I understand this is a difficult situation for you and your family. It's natural to feel conflicted and have strong emotions towards this situation, especially since your friend has been like a family member to you for a long time.

Your kids, having grown attached to her as an aunt figure, might feel particularly protective and resistant to this change. This is a complex emotional issue, especially when significant relationships are involved.

In such cases, open communication is essential. Convene a family meeting to discuss everyone's feelings and concerns about the new relationship. This can provide a sense of understanding, closure, and a chance to find common ground within your family.

You might also consider professional help, such as therapy or counseling, to facilitate this process and help everyone cope with the change. Ultimately, the health and happiness of everyone involved should be the top priority. Remember, each person's feelings are valid and deserve to be acknowledged in this situation.

Context: I have a lot of issues going on right now. First of all, I have a lot of trouble sleeping at times, while other nights I sleep too much and still feel quite tired. I’m also noticing increased irritability and experiencing anxiety attacks that last for hours. Is there something wrong with me and if so what should I do?
Response: It sounds as if you may be experiencing a bout of anxiety and/or depression. Sleeping difficulties, irritability and anxiety attacks all are correlated with these mood disorders. And it’s actually quite common for someone to deal encounter this during their lifetime. Also, keep in mind that just because you are experiencing this now does not mean that you will be dealing with it the rest of your life. Many times, it can be a single episode and if treatment is received, you can help prevent any recurrent episodes.However, you may want to first talk with your primary physician to rule out any medical issues that could be causing these problems. There may be a reason for the sudden onset of these symptoms that can be addressed quickly. On the other hand, if it is determined that you are experiencing anxiety and/or depression without a medical reason, there are two methods that are very helpful in your treatment:#1 Talk therapy with a certified therapistHe/She will be able to address the concerns you’re experiencing and discovering if there is an underlying issue that may be causing it.#2 MedicationThere are many medications that can assist you with the feelings that you are experiencing. Your doctor will be able to help you find the right one that works for you. If one doesn’t work, keep trying. There are many different options to address your specific needs.Also, a combination of these two options is extremely beneficial!Thank you for your question. I sincerely hope that you feel better soon!
LLM:
Based on your description, you may be experiencing symptoms of anxiety and sleep disturbances, which can be quite common in today's fast-paced world. Anxiety can manifest in various ways, such as irritability, increased heart rate, and difficulty concentrating, among other things. Additionally, sleep disturbances, such as insomnia or hypersomnia, can further exacerbate anxiety and decrease overall well-being.

These symptoms can stem from a variety of sources, including stressors in your personal or professional life, underlying mental health conditions like generalized anxiety disorder, or physical health issues. However, it's important to note that everyone experiences these symptoms at times, and they do not always indicate a serious condition.

To help manage your symptoms, here are a few suggestions:

1. Practice good sleep hygiene habits, such as sticking to a regular sleep schedule, avoiding screens and caffeine before bed, and creating a relaxing sleep environment.

2. Engage in calming activities before bed, such as reading, taking a warm bath, or practicing deep breathing exercises.

3. Consider implementing stress-reducing techniques, such as mindfulness meditation, progressive muscle relaxation, or engaging in regular physical activity.

4. If your symptoms continue despite making these lifestyle changes, consider seeking support from a mental health professional, who can help determine the underlying causes of your symptoms and provide appropriate treatment, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy or medication.

Remember, it's normal to have ups and downs in life, but if your symptoms persist and interfere with your ability to function in daily life, it may be worth reaching out for additional support.

I hope this information is helpful to you, and please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns.

Context: I've gone to a couple therapy sessions so far and still everytime I walk in I get nervous and shaky. Is this normal? Should I still be feeling like this?
Response: If you have only been to a couple of sessions, it makes sense that you might still feel apprehensive at first. Therapy is hard work! You may be talking about some things that you never talked to anyone about before. Opening up to stranger can be scary. After you feel comfortable with your counselor, and trust has built up and you feel understood in session, you should feel more relaxed. I would encourage you to talk to your counselor about feeling a little shaky. Sharing what the process is like for you is important in your work. If you still feel unsure about sharing after several sessions, it is important to take a look at that and try to understand where that may be coming from. For example, are you and the therapist not a good fit? But, again if you are just starting out in working together, feeling a little bit nervous makes perfect sense, and I encourage you to give it a bit more time and to talk to your therapist about how you are feeling.
LLM: Yes, it is normal to feel anxious or experience nervousness before therapy sessions, especially if you are new to therapy or if you have significant emotional concerns. These feelings can be a result of fear of judgment, fear of the unknown, or anticipation of potential emotional discomfort. It's important to remember that these feelings will usually decrease over time as you become more comfortable with the therapeutic process and your therapist. If the anxiety is severe or persists despite progress in therapy, it may be helpful to discuss this with your therapist, as there may be additional strategies or interventions that can be employed to help you manage your anxiety.

Context: I saw my mother cheating on my dad before. I said something to her, and she said it was in the past. Yesterday, I saw her texting dirty things to the same guy. Should I talk to her again? I don't want my parents to go their separate ways, I just want her to stop. it's not fair.
Response: You as the child in the middle of your parents' problems is the worse position to be in.  I can only imagine your hurt, pain and confusion on what to do.  Honestly, this is a hard question to answer without knowing your age, your parents' ability to handle hearing information from you and your maturity to handle what may happen in the end.  One thing I would like to note....your parents may be in the same house but if your mother is cheating, they have already gone their separate ways.
LLM:
I understand that it can be difficult and painful to witness infidelity in your parents' relationship, and even more so when it seems to be ongoing. However, as a psychologist, I cannot make the decision for you on whether to confront your mother again. I can offer some guidance on how you might approach this situation.

First, it's important to understand that adult relationships are complex and multifaceted. Your mother's decision to continue interacting with her past infidelity partner may be driven by a variety of reasons that you may not fully understand. Moreover, her response to your previous confrontation may have been defensive or dismissive due to a number of reasons as well.

Before you decide to talk to your mother again, I'd encourage you to consider the following:

1. Reflect on your motivations for wanting to confront her. Are you trying to protect your family, or are you hoping to alleviate your own emotional pain? Understanding your own motivations can help you approach the situation in a more intentional and focused way.
2. Consider the potential consequences of your actions. How might your parents react to your intervention? What impact might it have on their relationship and on your own emotional wellbeing?
3. Seek out additional support. Talking to a trusted friend or family member, or working with a therapist, can provide valuable perspective and guidance as you navigate this situation.
4. Understand that change may take time. Healing from infidelity is a complex process, and it may take time for your parents to make significant progress. Be patient and offer your support as they work through their challenges.
5. Consider alternative approaches. Instead of confronting your mother directly, you might explore other ways to express your concern and support for your parents' relationship. For example, you could offer to talk to a neutral third party, such as a therapist or trusted family friend, about your concerns.

Ultimately, the decision of whether or not to confront your mother is a personal one that you will need to make based on your own values, motivations, and emotional wellbeing. As your psychologist, I support you in whatever decision you make, and I am happy to provide additional guidance and support as you navigate this challenging situation.

Context: When my daughter is stressed about a silly thing from school, she starts crying and freaking out. She is a bright student, always has a 4.0, but I am afraid she is stressing too much. I’m afraid it’s going to break her. I don't know if I should get her to a doctor or someone because this is not normal.
Response: It sounds as though your daughter is struggling to manage her anxiety and she is having really big reactions to what you view as small problems or issues. What have you tried with her in the past? As exhausting as it might be, a good first step is to empathize and validate her feelings.
LLM:
I understand your concern, patient. It's natural for parents to want to protect their children from unnecessary stress. However, it's important to note that experiencing stress is a normal part of life, even for bright and high-achieving students. The frequency, intensity, and duration of the stress should be considered when determining whether it's necessary to seek professional help.

If your daughter's episodes of crying and freaking out are frequent, intense, and longer-lasting, and if they're interfering with her daily activities, such as going to school, then it might be a good idea to consider seeking help from a mental health professional.

However, if her reactions to stress are isolated incidents, and if she's able to manage her stress through healthy coping mechanisms like exercise, good nutrition, or relaxation techniques, then it may be sufficient to continue to support her at home, and encourage her to practice stress-management skills when needed.

Regardless of the decision you make, it's always a good idea to check in with your daughter about how she's feeling, and validate her emotions by acknowledging the difficulty she's experiencing. This can help her feel heard and understood, and can go a long way in helping her feel more resilient when faced with stress in the future.

Context:

What should I do? It's becoming annoying and making me anxious. Help me out
Response: It sounds like keeping the secrets has become a problem for you now. Would you want to know the secrets?- How has keeping these secrets affected your own mental and physical health?Once you have looked at the problem from all angles, you will be able to better make a decision on whether it is right to tell them or not.

However, I also recognize the concern and potential consequences that may come with sharing the secrets.

A possible solution may be to consider seeking the help of a mental health professional, such as a therapist or counselor. They can provide a safe and confidential space for you to explore your thoughts and feelings about the secrets and help you navigate the potential consequences of sharing or not sharing them.

However, it's also important to consider seeking support from a trusted friend, family member, or professional if the secrets involve harming yourself or others. Your well-being and safety are of the utmost importance. I would suggest considering seeking professional guidance as a first step.

Context: I feel like I hate myself physically and emotionally sometimes. How can I start accepting myself and be more confident?
Response: The answer would require a more in-depth knowledge of you and your situation. A lot of times, these feelings are the result of the people in your life treating you a certain way. You internalize it and accept it as your reality. The first step is to evaluate the people close to you, especially your parents. Even if you are an adult, think back to your childhood. Children who grow up in an unstable home often grow up to be adults with insecurities and emotional problems. The obvious, such as being abused, can certainly lead to a person having little to no confidence and self esteem, but there are other situations that might surprise you to hear they can be damaging to a person.You may have never been physically or sexually abused, but what about emotionally? Were you yelled at, berated, put down? Were you told you'd never amount to anything? Were you compared to siblings and felt like you always fell short, couldn't live up to expectations? When you hear something over and over again, you start to believe it.Maybe you weren't yelled at. Maybe it was the opposite. Studies show that children who grow up with a parent who is depressed show signs of emotional neglect. A chronically depressed mother, for example, may have seemed cold, detached, emotionless. She may have been less likely to show interest in a child's life, not give praise for accomplishments or show support by going to ballgames or performances. If one of your family members were chronically ill while you were growing up, chances are, a lot of the attention went to them, which could have led to your needs not being met.Any of these situations could cause a person to grow up feeling unimportant, unheard, unloved, or like they don't matter.Maybe nothing I've described here fits your situation. If you can't pinpoint what has caused you to feel this way on your own, a counselor can help.I am not saying "blame it on your parents" or telling you there's nothing you can do to change it! Quite the opposite! Understanding WHY you feel that way is a first step towards making the changes needed to feel better. Cognitive behavior therapy focuses on cognition - figure out the WHY. Then behavior - the HOW.
LLM:
I'm sorry to hear that you're struggling with self-hatred. It's important to remember that self-acceptance and confidence aren't things that can be achieved overnight, but rather a journey towards self-love and self-compassion. Here are some suggestions that may help you:

1. Practice self-compassion: Treat yourself with kindness and understanding, just as you would a good friend. You might find it helpful to repeat phrases like "I'm doing the best I can" or "This is a difficult time for me."
2. Identify negative thoughts: Challenging and reframing negative thoughts is an essential part of self-acceptance. When you notice a negative thought entering your mind, ask yourself if it's true, and if not, replace it with a more positive and compassionate thought.
3. Engage in self-care: Take care of your physical, emotional, and social needs. Eat healthy meals, get enough rest, and engage in activities that bring you joy and relaxation.
4. Surround yourself with positive influences: Seek out supportive relationships and environments that help foster self-confidence.
5. Set realistic goals: Break down larger goals into smaller, manageable steps. Celebrate your accomplishments and learn from your setbacks.
6. Consider therapy: If your feelings of self-hatred persist, it might be helpful to work with a mental health professional who can provide you with additional support and guidance.

Context: Is it normal for people to cry during therapy, or is it just me?
Response: Yes, many people cry during therapy!  Crying can offer a type of release as your body reacts to the input of your autonomic nervous system. Some people release endorphins when they cry, which can make you feel better than before.  If you're concerned about how much you cry during therapy, it might be good to discuss with your therapist.  They may be able to make some suggestions of how to honor your feelings in a way that works for you.
LLM: It is absolutely normal to cry during therapy. Expressing emotions through tears is a sign of emotional processing and growth. Many people find that therapy provides a safe and supportive space to explore difficult emotions, and crying is a natural response to the intensity of that emotional work. If you have any concerns or questions about your emotional experiences in therapy, feel free to bring them up with your therapist. We're here to support you every step of the way.

Context: I'm a teenage girl, and my dad is an alcoholic. I hate being at home with him because he just stresses me out. He can be picking me up from basketball practice or piano lessons, and half of the time, I don't even know if he's sober or not. I refuse to get into the vehicle with him and walk home sometimes. I've either been barely sleeping or I over sleep, so I'm always tired. I live in a small town, so there's no one I can really talk to because I'm not really that close with my family.
Response:
Hello, and thank you for your question. You are clearly a
resourceful young person to reach out like this to find help. Good for you. I
have a few ideas and perhaps some other counselors will add some thoughts, too.

First, good for you for taking care of yourself and making
really good decisions – such as not getting into the car with dad when he is
intoxicated. It is really important that you consider your safety above all
else. 
To answer your heading question, yes, it is quite common for
people to blame themselves for other people’s actions. There are various
reasons why we do this. For one thing, someone may tell us that their behaviors are our fault. And depending on things like our relationship to that
person, past experiences, and our self-esteem, we may actually start to believe
it. What is important to remember is that your parents have the responsibility
to be sure that you are safe and
cared for, not the other way around. 
As for the other things you mentioned, I am concerned about both
your physical and emotional safety and well-being. It sounds like your father
has an alcohol abuse problem, and living with someone who is struggling with
addiction can be hard for anyone, let alone a teenager who is also trying to
deal with things like school. 
You may want to see if your school has a counselor or
someone you can talk to about what is going on. I will tell you that in some
states, what you have told me may be something that a counselor would have to
report to child protection services, but not necessarily. If you are concerned
about that, ask the counselor what they are obligated to report. 
If you don’t know a counselor, think about any other adult
that you would feel comfortable talking to. They may have some ideas. 
Sometimes individual states have non-profit chat lines or
hotlines for teens to call if they are struggling with things. You may be able
to find something like that in your state. I do warn your about doing random
searches, not every website is good, so be careful. 
This is clearly a tough spot for you to be in, so one of the
things that I encourage you to do while searching for support is to focus on
taking care of yourself. Sometimes things like this can really bring us down and
we stop doing the things we enjoy. Try really hard not to let that happen. If
you have interests, do them. If you catch yourself thinking too hard about
things, try to find something fun to do to distract yourself. Try to take care
of yourself in other ways, such as eating well. Exercise can really help when
we get stressed. 
I hope some of this was helpful. Good luck.
 
Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPC, NCC
LLM:
I can understand how difficult and stressful it can be for you to deal with your father's alcoholism, especially given that it can be unpredictable and disrupt your daily life. It's important to prioritize your safety and well-being. Here are some things you can consider:

1. Communicate with your school counselor or a trusted teacher about what's going on. They may be able to offer you support and provide resources.
2. Look into local support groups for teens dealing with similar issues. Reach out to them online if necessary.
3. If you feel that you're in danger or that you can't handle the situation on your own, contact your local child protective services or a helpline.
4. Try to establish a routine for yourself, such as sticking to a regular sleep schedule and practicing relaxation techniques, like deep breathing or meditation.
5. Consider speaking with a mental health professional, who can offer you additional support and help you develop coping skills.

Remember that you're not alone in dealing with this issue, and it's okay to seek help from others. Don't hesitate to reach out when you need it.

Context: I'm a teenage gay girl, and I'm in love with a girl a few years older than me. She loves me too, but we're not physically together at the moment. I've been thinking about being transgender soon too, but my family doesn't accept me, so I honestly don't know what to do about that because I feel like I have to constantly hide the real me. It hurts so much to hide the real me. What can I do?
Response: They would probably be helpful to speak with a local mental health professional about this, not because there's anything wrong with the real you, but because having these conversations can be difficult and you may choose to talk about your real self in different ways with different people.Maybe you could talk about your concerns with your significant other and discuss different ways you may be able to discuss your relationship that fit with how each of you defines your love for one another.Because you are saying openly that you are gay, and it sounds like you already identify yourself as gay, as compared to considering being transgender "soon," it may be worth considering disclosing the fact that you are gay now and disclosing the idea that you are transgender after you have worked through that yourself. I would like for you to be able to be true to yourself and show the real you. It could also be that you already know that you are transgender, but with the way that you  phrased it here, I'm not certain.Here is a link to a hotline that you may want to consider using. The Trevor Project is about helping teenagers who identify as LGBTQ: http://www.thetrevorproject.org/Here is another national site where you may be able to find a local chapter: https://www.pflag.org/I don't know what you mean when you said that your family will not accept you, but since you are concerned about your family's reaction, I would suggest that it may be helpful to have a therapist or some other support in place before telling your family. The decision of whether or not to wait longer to tell them is ultimately yours, but I wonder if you have a likelihood of explaining what you are experiencing in a way that would be truer to you and also perhaps easier for your family to follow if you had time to process it with supportive people first.Please remember that you are not alone in this. LLM:
I understand how difficult and painful it can be to feel like you have to hide parts of yourself from the world. First and foremost, I want to validate your feelings and let you know that it's normal to feel confused and uncertain about your identity, especially during the teenage years.

Regarding your romantic relationship: Communication is key. It's important to have an open and honest conversation with your partner about your feelings and concerns. Consider discussing the challenges you're facing and how you both can work together to create a safe and supportive environment for your relationship to flourish.

As for your gender identity feelings: It's important to remember that your identity is a deeply personal aspect of who you are. Exploring your gender identity is a process, and there is no right or wrong way to do so. I would encourage you to take your time, educate yourself about what it means to be transgender, and reach out to LGBTQ+ support groups and resources to help you navigate these complex emotions.

Regarding your family: It's important to remember that not everyone understands or accepts us for who we are, and that's okay. You have the power to choose who you surround yourself with and who you share your true self with. If coming out to your family isn't safe or feasible at the moment, consider reaching out to supportive friends, mentors, or online communities for guidance and support. Remember, your identity is valid, and you deserve to live your life authentically and honestly.

Context: I haven't uttered one word to my boyfriend in days. Now I'm over it and don't know how to approach the situation?

If you find yourself becoming upset again, I encourage you to take a 20 minute break and then come back to the conversation once you are no longer heated. Studies show that talking or discussing arguments are more effective once you have cooled down and you are more likely to hear/understand what your partner is trying to convey.
LLM: It's understandable that ending a silence in a relationship can be intimidating, even if the reason for the silence no longer exists. Before approaching your boyfriend, it's essential to reflect on your intentions. Do you want to apologize for the silence, express how you're feeling, or simply check-in on the relationship? Once you've clarified your intentions, consider selecting an appropriate time and place for the conversation, and convey your message in a respectful, open, and honest manner. Remember, effective communication is a skill that takes practice, so be patient and persistent with yourself and your boyfriend as you navigate this situation.

Context: I would like to be able to have more positive relationships in the present.
Response: Hi Chapel Hill, I like your goal; research is telling us more and more that one of the most important keys to happiness is having healthy relationships. If we feel successful in that area, it can give us the confidence and hope to tackle anything life throws at us.There are personal barriers we all have to having healthy relationships; it's our "stuff", our "baggage" (not a steam trunk, right...you have a cute little Gucci bag!). You hint that people may have hurt you in the past. Maybe fear says "stay away...hold onto resentment or you will be hurt again!". Resentment, or simply focusing on the past is certainly a barrier to improving any relationship. Learning to forgive ourselves and others is so important. And remember...forgiveness isn't about saying something was okay; it only means "It happened, I can't change that, and I don't want to carry it (anger, hurt, resentment) around anymore, so I let it go." Or...sometimes fear tells us that people won't accept us; this keeps us isolated for sure! But really, all fear wants is power over us. Fear is a trickster! It tells us that if we let go of the past, we will be rejected or hurt. It says "I'm trying to protect you!", but what it REALLY wants is to paralyze and isolate us all so it can feel powerful. Once you pull the sheeps clothing off of fear, you can see that it doesn't help you at all!Once you see where your personal barriers are, and you start to refute those thoughts in your mind, you can begin to build new ideas about yourself and other people that are based on compassion instead of fear... Everyone deserves forgiveness and compassion.No one deserves to be judged by their worst moments.If I look in the rearview mirror, I can't move forward.We are all beautifully imperfect.I can't change the past, but I have the power to make the future good.I wish you the best on your journey!
LLM: I understand that you want to improve your current relationships and build more positive connections. Here are some suggestions: Practice active listening, show genuine interest in others, communicate effectively and respectfully, set healthy boundaries, and express appreciation and gratitude. Also, consider seeking a therapist for further guidance on improving your interpersonal skills and relationships.

Context: I'm a teenager. My dad has been jail for the last five years. It's tough, but my mom really tries to give a normal life to my two sisters, my brother, and I. Context: I'm feeling rejected and frustrated. This is not new. The first month we were together, sex was about every day. Then it slowed down to once a month right away. I love my husband, but I am struggling with this.

Can you tell me more about what it's like for you?"When asking questions like this, it usually helpful to not overuse (and perhaps try to avoid using) the word "why." Context: My dad is always, and I mean always, cussing and screaming at me for no reason at all. He makes me feel stupid. He also compares me to my other siblings in a negative way and demeans me. Is this abuse?
Response: Yes, screaming and cussing at your child is considered abuse.Here are two points I suggest you consider for your situation.If your dad is ever calm when you and him are with one another,  ask for some time to talk about your relationship with him.Schedule it for sometime in the near future from your request.   This way he has time to consider his points of view on his relationship to you.  Even if he does no thinking about your relationship at all, he will not feel pressured by the surprise of suddenly being expected to talk about a topic he may prefer to prepare himself.My second point to you is to keep steady in your own views of who you are.  When a parent demeans and mistreats a child, the child is affected in a negative way.  Concentrate on loving yourself and keeping people in your friendship circle who care about you.
LLM: Based on the information you have provided, it does seem like you may be experiencing emotional abuse from your father. Emotional abuse can involve repeated criticism, demeaning comments, and making one feel inferior. Such behavior is not acceptable, and it can have detrimental effects on one's mental and emotional wellbeing. It's important to remember that no one deserves to be treated this way, and it's not your fault. I would encourage you to reach out to trusted adults, such as a guidance counselor, teacher, or healthcare professional, to discuss your situation and explore options for support and protection. Additionally, there are resources available that can offer further information and help for individuals experiencing emotional abuse, such as domestic violence hotlines and counseling services. I'm here for you if you need someone to talk to or to help you access these resources. Remember, you deserve to be treated with respect and kindness.

Context: I think I'm annoying and bothersome, but my girlfriend says I'm just fine. I don't think so, I think I'm stressing everyone else out

Context: I start counseling/therapy in a few days (I'm freaking out) but my main fear is that I'll cry and embarrass myself, is it something to worry about?

Instead, focus on the potential benefits of therapy, such as improved mental health and emotional well-being. Context: I have three children. The oldest is nine and not my biological child. The middle child is three and the youngest is one. Is this normal?
Response: This is your experience, your feeling and you are wondering if it is normal, which means is this common or the rule. This question about how you feel about your third child leads me to believe you are dis- turbed [etymology: agitated or stirred up].  Feelings are messages from our soul or spirit that something is not right.  So, though  it might be normal [common, the rule]  to look forward to a one year olds nap time when you have a three year old a nine year old, the dis- turbance or stirring up in you, is important and requires your attention, your listening, if you will to your higher self.

However, your feelings towards the youngest child may change as she grows older. Infants require significant attention and energy, which may influence your thoughts and emotions towards them. Moreover, it's natural for parents to develop different attachment styles based on their own personal experiences and emotional states.

If you find yourself consistently feeling disconnected or indifferent towards your youngest child, it might be helpful to consult a mental health professional or seek resources from parenting support groups to explore potential underlying causes and develop strategies to strengthen your connection with her. Remember, parenting is a journey with ups and downs, and it's essential to take care of yourself in order to effectively care for your children.

Context: I just about fall asleep then I can't help but sit up and walk around.
Response: Maybe you are afraid to let go of your problems for a few hours.Although you didn't write this, usually what keeps people awake or prevents them from falling asleep are worries about certain situations in their lives.If this describes you then. maybe talk with yourself to convince you that you are entitled to a good night's sleep.Really we all are and feeling rested from the day and life's tensions will better you to handle whatever the particular matters are.Also, maybe you will be more enthusiastic to go to bed if you have a comfy mattress and nice pillows!  Make your bed an inviting place because this will tempt you to enjoy relaxing enough to go to sleep!
LLM: That's a common symptom, especially for those who suffer from insomnia. It's called "wakefulness after sleep onset," or WASO for short. It's common for people to have a few minutes of wakefulness after falling asleep and before actually entering a deep sleep cycle. However, if you find that this behavior is preventing you from getting sufficient rest or is causing you significant distress, then it's important to speak with your doctor or a mental health professional about potential treatments, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy or medication. In the meantime, try establishing a regular sleep routine, avoiding caffeine and electronic devices before bedtime, and creating a relaxing sleep environment. Additionally, relaxing activities, such as reading or listening to calming music, can help prepare your mind and body for sleep.

Context: My ex-wife married and used me to have a child. She now uses that child as a pawn. I know my child misses me, but I need to move on and not live in the past. How do I do this? Do I see my child as much as possible or very little? Is it more confusing for the child?
Response: I am really glad you reached out. When parents get divorced, they still need to figure out how to have relationship so that they can parent their child. We might divorce our partners, however, we don't divorce our children. If anything our children need to know that we are still a stable force in their lives. Unfortunately, in some relationships children get used as a bargaining piece and I am hearing that may be happening in your relationship. You can move on from the relationship with your ex while still maintaining a relationship with your child. It is possible and it takes both parents to agree to do the right thing by their child. It is important for your children to have some consistency in his or her time with you. For example, if you and your ex don't have a parenting schedule it is something that you want to create so that you can not only decide for you when you will see your child, but your child can know when you will be available just for her. This will lay the foundation for consistency and allow your child to have some security in troubled times.I would highly recommend you sit down with your ex and discuss how you can both be a positive source in your child's life. How you can both be there to help raise her. If this is not a conversation that you can have on your own you might seek out help from the court. There are parenting experts and mediators who might be able to assist you.Remember, in any discussion keep the focus on what is best for your child and you will be moving in the right direction.Best of luck to you!
LLM:
I understand your situation, Patient, and it's a common issue when going through a divorce, especially when children are involved. It's important for you to prioritize your emotional well-being and find a balance that works best for both you and your child.

First, let's establish some facts. You can't change the past, but you can make conscious decisions for your present and future. Your main concern is your emotional well-being and providing a stable environment for your child, even if you're no longer in a romantic relationship with their mother.

The decision on how much contact you have with your child is a complex one, and there's no one-size-fits-all answer. It depends on your personal circumstances, the age and emotional maturity of your child, and the relationship you and your ex-wife share. Keep in mind that children need consistency and a strong emotional bond with both parents.

I'd recommend considering the following:

1. Open communication with your ex-wife: Try to discuss your concerns and find a compromise that is in the best interest of your child.
2. Set clear boundaries: Establish limits that provide stability and consistency for your emotional and logistical relationship with your child.
3. Reevaluate the situation regularly: Be open to reassessing the situation as your circumstances and your child's needs change over time.
4. Seek therapist or counselor assistance: If needed, consulting with a mental health professional can help you navigate these complex emotions and make decisions that are in the best interest of both you and your child.

Remember that your emotional well-being is essential, but minimizing the impact on your child should also be a priority. It's essential to focus on staying present in the moment, building a positive relationship with your child, and finding ways to move forward, without obsessing over the past.

Context: My fiancé and I come from a strong Christian background but both went off the "straight and narrow" once before. He is having a hard time accepting my past, especially that I'm not a virgin. He has a hard time in general accepting himself and others.    His insecurities are hurting our relationship. How can I help him let go of my past and decide to live in the present?
Response: For you and your fiance to move past this, he needs to accept you just the way you are.  Being that you mentioned that you both come from a Christian background, maybe you could start there, he needs to forgive and trust your love for him.  His insecurities stem from something bigger than you not being a virgin.  Since this is the man, you are willing to spend the rest of your life with, be patient and help him find the help he needs.  Talk about your commitment to him and reassure him that your history does not have to affect your relationship.  Lastly, Pre-Marital Counseling can help you and him to open up about other things that might affect the marriage later.God Bless You Both, Mirella~
LLM:
I understand that your fiancé is struggling to come to terms with your past and his own insecurities. It's important to note that everyone's journey in life is unique, and past experiences shape who we are in the present. Here are some suggestions that might help your fiancé:

1. Open communication: Encourage your fiancé to share his feelings about your past with you. Listen actively and validate his emotions, while also expressing your own.
2. Self-acceptance: Help him explore the roots of his insecurities and encourage him to work on self-acceptance. Suggest that he seek professional help if need be, but ensure that he understands that his worth is not defined by past mistakes or imperfections.
3. Practice forgiveness: Both of you need to work on forgiving each other for past mistakes and misgivings. This is a process that takes time and patience, but the result is an improved sense of emotional connection and understanding.
4. Live in the present: Encourage both of you to focus on the present and your shared future. This means setting aside discussions about the past and focusing on the present moment and your goals as a couple.
5. Seek support: Encourage your fiancé to build a support system. This could include friends, family, or even a therapist. Having a trusted source of support can make it easier for him to process his emotions and work through his insecurities.
6. Practice gratitude: Help your fiancé focus on the positive aspects of your relationship and his own life. Practicing gratitude can help shift his perspective and improve his mood.
7. Patience and understanding: Finally, be patient and understanding with your fiancé as he works through these issues. Change takes time and effort, and it's important to remember that everyone's journey is unique.

Context: My boyfriend is in Ireland for 11 days, and I am an emotional wreck.

Context: I am becoming a Water Safety Instructor but I didn't have enough for a proper swimsuit. I was told by a boy in class that my top was displaying everything. I was very embarrassed.
Response: Sometimes we make ourselves feel worse with our own thoughts about things we can't change. We know that it is possible to overcome embarrassment and self-blame, but why does it seem so difficult to overcome it sometimes? If you tune into your own thoughts, or your "internal dialogue", you'll notice that your thoughts are connected to your feelings. So people who are holding onto embarrassment and self-blame often have thoughts like, "How could I do that?", "I looked so stupid!" "Now people saw things I did not want them to see!" If we tune in more, we might notice we are thinking that this is terrible, horrible, and that we cannot deal with it. These are self-sabotaging thoughts that lead to shame, self-blame and embarrassment. If you look at the evidence, you can find more rational thoughts such as, " We all make mistakes", "People can look all different ways at different time - this incident does not define me!" and "If someone has a bad image or thought about me, it is really OK! People have all kinds of thoughts about other people, and it does not have to be a major problem." When you practice your rational thoughts often and replace the negative ones, you will start to feel better, more confident and your embarrassment will decrease quickly.
LLM: I'm sorry to hear that you felt embarrassed in your swimsuit class. It's essential to wear proper fitness gear when engaging in physical activities such as water safety instruction to ensure comfort and functionality. Regarding your concern about your top, it's important to remember that everyone's body shape and size is unique. If you feel your swimsuit is not providing enough coverage, you may consider purchasing a different style or size. You could also speak to a swimsuit specialist or look up resources online for guidance on finding the right fitting swimsuit. Your focus should be on your skills and effectiveness as a Water Safety Instructor, and not on the opinions of others. Confidence is key, and it's essential to prioritize your own wellbeing and comfort in the learning environment.

Context: I have a problem with binge eating, especially during times of stress. I binge eat and feel so overwhelmed with shame and disappointment that I end up going to the gym and trying to exercise the calories off. I am in my late 20s, and I have been dealing with this since I was a teenager. Are there any coping mechanisms I can use to break this cycle?
Response: The fact that you are aware of the issue and what causes it is a step in the right direction.  You can absolutely break the cycle but it takes a lot of effort.  Much like growing a physical muscle, developing coping mechanisms must be practiced (exercised) in order to gain strength.  Find healthy activities that you like to do that can help you deal with stress.  It appears that you are already going to the gym which is awesome; you want this to be a healthy experience not one done out of guilt.  Is it possible for you to incorporate food items that are healthy for you? Remember any changes you make must be practiced and developed over time.  You should seek professional help to assist you with getting to the root cause of your emotional connection to food. Normally these issues date back to childhood experiences however this is a generalization and I have little information to go on.  Above all else BE KIND TO YOURSELF! Shame is counterproductive. If you want something to grow you give it love and attention. Well wishes!!!!!!
LLM:
I understand that binge eating can be a challenging issue, particularly during periods of stress. It's important to remember that you are not alone, and there are effective coping mechanisms that can help you break this cycle.

Firstly, try to identify the triggers that lead you to binge eat, such as stress, emotional distress or specific food cravings. Once you become aware of these triggers, you can begin to develop alternative ways to manage them.

One effective coping mechanism is the practice of mindfulness, which involves staying fully present in the moment and focusing on your thoughts and feelings without judgment. Mindfulness practices, such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation, can help you to manage stress and emotional distress in a healthier way.

Another useful coping mechanism is practicing emotional regulation techniques, such as expressing emotions through writing or art, engaging in activities that bring you joy, or seeking support from friends, family or a therapist.

Additionally, you might find it helpful to establish a regular meal schedule, avoiding skipping meals or fasting for extended periods. Eating a balanced diet with plenty of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean protein, and healthy fats can help stabilize your blood sugar levels, thereby reducing the urge to binge eat.

Lastly, consider enlisting the support of a healthcare professional, such as a therapist or nutritionist, to help you develop a customized plan for managing your binge eating. Remember that it's normal for progress to be slow, and setbacks are a natural part of the process. Keep in mind that every step you take towards managing your binge eating is a step towards greater self-care and overall well-being.

Context: I got engaged, and everything was going well. Things went downhill at some point, and I broke off the engagement due to an opportunity to go to college. Context: Often times I find myself thinking scary thoughts and sometimes I even scare myself into thinking that something bad is going to happen to me. Once it starts, the thought continues going through my head and I can't get it out. How can I stop these thoughts?
Response: There are some great thoughts offered by others here. I would just add that typically the most natural response to fearful thoughts is to want to stop, avoid, or get rid of them - which doesn't work if you're really caught up in a cycle of OCD or other form of anxiety. In the long run, the more effective thing to do is the harder and less intuitive option: to have those uncomfortable thoughts on purpose. This may mean writing out in detail what the worst case fear you are thinking of is, and then reading it over and over again until it becomes boring. It may also mean pausing through the course of the day to merely observe all the thoughts going on, and realizing that thoughts are merely thoughts. They are not the same as reality, and the unpleasant ones can become a lot less scary when we realize we can coexist with them without them coming true.
LLM:
I understand that intrusive thoughts can be distressing and difficult to manage. It's important to remember that having scary thoughts does not necessarily mean that they will come true. Here are some techniques that might help in managing these thoughts:

1. Identify and challenge your thoughts: When you notice an intrusive thought, try to identify its source and the beliefs that underpin it. Once you understand where the thought is coming from, you can challenge its validity by asking yourself if there is any evidence to support it.
2. Practicing mindfulness and relaxation techniques: Mindfulness practices, such as deep breathing, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation, can help you focus on the present moment and reduce the anxiety that comes with intrusive thoughts.
3. Replacing your thoughts with positive and calming thoughts: Try to consciously reframe your negative thoughts into positive ones. For example, if you have a thought like "I am going to fail this test," you could replace it with "I have prepared well for this test and am confident in my abilities."
4. Engaging in activities: Engage in activities that you find enjoyable and that help take your mind off the intrusive thoughts. This could be reading a book, watching a movie, going for a walk, or engaging in a hobby.
5. Seeking support: If your intrusive thoughts are causing significant distress or interfering with your daily life, consider seeking support from a mental health professional. They can help you develop coping strategies and provide you with additional resources and support.

Context: My husband’s ex-wife married a man who was charged with seven felony counts of pandering involving a minor. He, by his own admission, is addicted to child pornography. My step-daughters are now all teenagers. They do not know. My husband’s ex-wife has kept this information from my step-daughters. The step-father has had issues regarding his pornography addiction recently. The ex-wife minimizes it by saying that he has "repented." It is a constant strain on my husband, knowing that his girls are living with a man who is addicted to child pornography. My own therapist believes the girls should be told. The ex-wife says her therapist says the opposite.
Response: The information from the other therapists here is sound and prudent. There are what sounds to be real legal (is this man a RSO [registered sex offender]; is he prohibited from being in proximity to minors? use/possession of child porn violates any # of state and federal statues...) and safety issues at play here. Your husband's daughters have an immediate "need to know" and this can be done developmentally-contextual, factually and in a non-shaming fashion. I would further counsel that you and your husband (his daughters welfare is at stake) seek immediate legal counsel. The ex-wife here seems to be between a "rock and a hard place" of her own creation -- she desperately needs care, treatment for herself. Resources for Your Consideration: 1) http://www.missingkids.com/home; 2) https://www.justice.gov/criminal-ceos/citizens-guide-us-federal-law-child-pornography
LLM:
I understand that this situation is complex and concerning for you and your husband. As a psychologist, I cannot make a definitive decision without fully assessing the situation and consulting with all parties involved. However, I can offer some general guidance based on ethical and professional principles.

Transparency and honesty are important values in relationships, especially those that involve children. Withholding information, especially information that may have a significant impact on the well-being of the step-daughters, can lead to negative consequences in the long term.

It is also crucial to consider the age and maturity level of the step-daughters and to provide them with age-appropriate information and support if they are to be informed about their step-father's past and present issues.

Regarding the differing opinions from therapists, it is essential to consider that each therapist may have a unique perspective and approach, influenced by various factors, such as their professional training, ethical frameworks, and personal values. Ultimately, the decision should be based on the best interests of the step-daughters.

Considering the current situation, open communication with your husband, ex-wife, and step-daughters about the issue may be necessary. This would involve discussing the potential risks and benefits of disclosing the information, the steps to be taken to help the step-father seek treatment, and the available support systems for the girls.

It may also be essential to seek advice from professionals, such as child psychologists and legal experts, to help make the most informed decision. Remember, I am just an assistant and I cannot provide definitive answers, but I can help you explore the issue and find resources for you to consider.

Disclaimer: This advice is intended to be of a general nature only and should not be considered professional or legal advice. This advice is not a substitute for advice from a qualified professional, such as a psychologist or a lawyer. Always consult with a professional for a comprehensive evaluation of your specific situation.

Context: How do I make myself happy without the people who made me happy? Now that they’re gone, I feel sad. It’s been two months now but I seem to be unable to stay okay and independent.
Response: Hello. So, someone made you happy but is no longer around? I am sure that feels devastating. As a counselor I usually have to ponder this kind of thought because it lends itself to suggesting that happiness comes from the external, rather than the internal side of a person's life. Happiness is formed within ourselves. All the external trappings of happiness are usually quite fleeting, and temporary. Even relationships fo not last forever. If we get to a place where we are reliant and dependent on those around us to create happiness in our lives, then we lose the opportunities for us to do it ourselves. We are entirely capable of finding ways to be happy. Sometimes this entails working out issues that are holding us back from reaching a place of peace, or resolution to a specific problem.What I would offer to you, is to find ways in which you can develop happiness for yourself. What do you like about your life as it is right now? What can you do to create change in your life to increase your happiness? These kinds of questions can be valuable to you as you seek out ways to make happiness an internal rather than an external event. Mourning the loss of a social connection (in whatever way that occurred), is part of the grieving process, but it is not permanent. It is a path to healing and moving forward. From this, you can seek out new sources of happiness. I encourage you to remember that happiness is independent of the external experience you have in the world. Happiness is an expression of the internal feelings, so that will be the source or the font of positive emotion from which you really draw. If you are lacking in happiness, then it becomes important to examine why that is, and what you can do about it to promote self-care. Seeking out a professional counselor can also be a good option as you move through the grief/loss process and examine the reasons happiness is not in greater supply.I wish you happiness for every day of your life.Best wishes.
LLM: I understand your feelings, it's normal to grieve the loss of loved ones. However, it's important to remember that happiness comes from within. Although other people can enhance our joy, they don't define it. Focus on self-care, hobbies, and relationships that bring you positive emotions. Seek support from friends, family or even a therapist if you find it hard to cope on your own. It takes time, but eventually you'll discover joy in new things and memories. Remember, it's okay to feel sad sometimes, but don't let it consume your life.

Context: I started dating my fiancé two years ago. My first cousin is married to my fiancé’s sister. There has always been a communication issue between my uncle and aunt and my cousin and his wife. My uncle goes to my mom instead of talking to my cousin, and it upsets my mom.
Response: When you're upset about something, the first question you can ask yourself is "Whose problem is this?" It is amazing how many problems will disappear right at that point, because you realize the problem isn't even yours. You can't fix everything in your family. You are starting a new family of your own, and it's time to create a protective bubble around your family unit that keeps other people's issues away. I tell myself "Not my pig, not my farm, not my problem." When you stop trying to take care of other people's problems, they are more likely to step up and deal with it themselves. :)
LLM: I see. It sounds like there's a complicated family dynamic at play here. It's important to acknowledge that family relationships can be complicated, especially when there are multiple layers of connection. The fact that there are ongoing communication issues between your uncle and cousin, and that this is causing distress for other family members, including yourself and your mom, can be very challenging. It's important to remember that communicating openly and directly with each other, even about difficult topics, is key to resolving conflicts and maintaining healthy relationships. However, it may be helpful for all parties involved to consider seeking the assistance of a mediator or counselor to facilitate these conversations and find common ground. Remember, it's okay to ask for help when things feel overwhelming. Additionally, it's essential to prioritize self-care and maintain good mental and emotional health during this time. If you find that the situation becomes too stressful, it's okay to set boundaries and take a step back if necessary. I recommend discussing these options with your loved ones and exploring any available resources in your community that may be of assistance.

Context: My son claims that hes been having extremely violent thoughts and dreams. Not violent like he's hurting someone, but violent thoughts like horrible things happening to his loved ones. Response: Let me start my stating this is important to take seriously - taking to his PCP, getting a referral to a pediatric specialist, and getting him to a relational psychotherapist would be my first tasks. I wont label this occurrences as "normal" yet it is important to note these things do occur. Context:
I have a mother who is still running my life even though I'm almost 30 years old. I want to move out and live my own life, but it feels like as long as I do what my mom wants our relationship is okay. It's never okay if I don't do what she wants. 

I don't know how to start making my own decisions and not worry about  whether it's right in her eyes.  Would it help for me to talk to someone about this?
Response: ABSOLUTELY.  Having someone who's outside the situation and, even more so, with knowledge and experience in human behavior and relationship dynamics such as a counselor can do wonders in helping us feel less alone in frustrating situations like the one you're currently stuck in with your mother.  A professional can also assist you in finding some assertiveness techniques, communication strategies, and coping skills to help you find your voice and stand your ground in a way that feels most authentic to you.In a healthy parent-child dynamic, there comes a normal developmental shift when, as the child reaches each new stage of maturity and responsibility, the parent backs off to provide a more supportive role instead of directive role.  We call this "redefining relationships."  (We also do it with our friendships and colleagues here and there over time as needed). It's absolutely vital. Sounds like your mom may have missed that memo. Like many parents, she may feel your behavior and choices is a direct reflection of her.  Or that her role as your mother allows her greater latitude than it should.  Or like many other people in general, that her way is the ONLY way.  Regardless, this is YOUR life.  And as far as we know, it's not a dress rehearsal.  You certainly don't want to wake up some 50 years from now regretful or resentful. That being said, there are certainly ways to show her love and respect while doing what makes you happy. I would encourage you to Google therapists near you whose bios resonate with you and what you're looking for and start on a the journey towards a new chapter of freedom and hopefully a deeper, more satisfying mother-daughter relationship for both of you.Warmest regards,Tamara Powell, LMHC
LLM:
Absolutely, patient. It sounds like you're experiencing some difficulties with establishing independence from your mother. It's not uncommon for young adults, particularly in close-knit families, to struggle with this. However, it is important to establish your own sense of autonomy and identity.

Talking to a mental health professional, such as a psychologist or therapist, can be very helpful in this situation. A therapist can provide you with strategies and tools to develop healthy boundaries, communicate effectively with your mother, and build confidence in making decisions that are right for you, rather than trying to please others.

Together, we can explore your feelings and help you gain a better understanding of why you continue to prioritize your mother's approval over your own needs and desires. Setting and working towards personal goals, practicing assertiveness, and learning effective communication skills can all be part of a plan to move towards greater independence.

It's a brave step to seek help and acknowledge that there is room for growth. Reach out to a mental health professional today to get started on this journey towards a more fulfilling and independent life.

Context: I have been with my husband for almost 7 years. We got engaged a little after 5 years of being together. I had always been clear that I wanted to get married and I sadly would drop hints about how i wanted him to propose.  When he did propose it was during a random vacation that I had planned. I was happy but I couldn't help feeling disappointed too. I had told him numerous times before that I wanted him to do it in front of my friends and family. I know it sounds dumb to be upset but I couldn't help how I was feeling. We are now married but the wedding and ring were also far from what I wanted and it wasn't due to lack of funds. I know this is all material and the marriage is the most important thing but I cant help but get jealous and envious every time I see someone get a proposal or wedding that I had wanted. I cant help my anger because I know we only get one proposal and one wedding. What I wanted will never happen. I have been working on my feelings for the past year and half to get over it. I try to focus on our marriage but every time I see someone have the best proposals and weddings I get upset. I know it is selfish and I know its petty but I just can't control it. It's ruining our relationship because I constantly think about it. Plus, I get mad at him for small things because I am trying to hide the fact that I am so disappointed.  Why can't I move on?
Response: It's hard to let go of the dreams you had regarding your wedding and engagement. This was something that you and most women think about for years, so it's understandable to be disappointed. But please be aware that it is rare that any wedding is perfect. You may envy your friends' weddings but more than likely they had a few mishaps of their own and/or it wasn't as perfect as they had planned it either. However, like you know, the marriage is the most important thing. How many women are envious that you are in a happy, stable relationship? I assure you that there are plenty. Try to live in the present considering that dwelling on the past can still not change how you were proposed to or how your wedding day transpired. Focus on what you can control NOW. Perhaps, you can plan an extravagant renewal of your vows or change the look of your ring?I would also be upfront with your husband and explain why you've been short lately. But after that, close that chapter and move on. It sounds like you have a wonderful companion and you certainly don't want to lose this due to a situation that is impossible to change.
LLM:
It sounds like you're experiencing some complex emotions surrounding your wedding and proposal experience, as well as some difficulty with managing those feelings in your current relationship. These feelings are not insignificant or petty; weddings and proposals are meaningful experiences, and it's natural to have strong emotions associated with them, especially when they don't align with our expectations.

The inability to move past these feelings may be due to a few factors. One possibility is that you haven't fully processed the emotions surrounding the disappointment of the proposal and wedding. It's important to acknowledge and understand these emotions, rather than trying to suppress or hide them. Consider engaging in some form of self-reflection or therapy to help work through these feelings.

Another possibility is that you're holding onto a comparison between your experience and others. Comparing your relationship and experiences to others can be a slippery slope, and can lead to feelings of dissatisfaction and resentment. Instead, try focusing on the unique aspects of your own relationship and the positive experiences you have.

Additionally, it's important to communicate openly and honestly with your partner about your feelings. Hiding and suppressing negative emotions can lead to resentment and a deterioration of the relationship.

Working through these feelings will likely be a process that takes time and effort. It may be helpful to explore the underlying causes of your disappointment and the role that societal pressures and expectations play in your desires for an ideal proposal and wedding.

You're not alone in experiencing these emotions, and it's important to remember that everyone's experiences and expectations are different. Focus on the bond and love you share with your partner, and continue to work on managing and addressing these emotions in a healthy way.

Context: I had to put a restraining order against my ex-fiancé. He was served last night. He was mentally and verbally abusive towards me. I thought it was my nerves, but every time I stand up, I get sick. If I sit and rock, I'm fine.
Response: Hello, and thank you for your question. You may be right, your physical symptoms of getting sick to your stomach may very well be your nerves or anxiety. That is totally normal and understandable given your circumstances. Another thing that is not unusual is that you feel better when you sit and rock. That is actually a fairly common way that many people comfort themselves in times of high stress. If you are concerned that there is an actual physical illness causing these symptoms, you may want to visit your primary care provider. They may tell you that they don't see anything physically wrong with you, and at that point you may want to visit a counselor about the symptoms and your relationship experience. A visit with a counselor may be a good idea either way. I work as a counselor in primary care, and I promise you that many people have very real physical symptoms when they have gone through abuse. Unexplainable stomach and headaches are very common. Sometimes, with counseling, those physical symptoms actually stop. Be well, and I hope you feel better soon.Robin J. Landwehr, DBH, LPCC, NCC
LLM: I'm sorry to hear about your experience with your ex-fiancé. Your symptoms of dizziness and sickness when you stand up could be related to the stress and anxiety you're experiencing. This can affect your body in various ways. It's important to take good care of yourself during this time. Consider practicing relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or mindfulness meditation to help calm your body and mind. Regular physical activity and a healthy diet can also help improve your overall wellbeing. Being proactive about your self-care can help reduce your stress levels and give you a greater sense of control during this difficult time. You may also benefit from talking with a therapist or counselor who can provide you with additional support and coping strategies.

Context: I'm depressed. I have been for years. I hide it from everyone because I'm scared of the reactions I'll get. Last time I tried telling my parents, it was a huge argument about me being too young to be depressed (I’m a legal adult), calling me ungrateful, and telling me that if I can't handle things now, it's only going to get worse in the future (which is turning out to be true). It's exhausting pretending to be okay, and I don't know how much longer I can try. I'm just really tired, and sadly, I can't afford the help I need on my own.
Response: Hi Georgia, There's a really good lesson here. People tell us things, and we tend to think that's the truth...but it's not! I'll do some translating for you..."You're so ungrateful" means "I need you to be successful and happy in order for me to feel I'm a good parent"."You're too young to be depressed" means "I don't want to deal with your mental health issues right now. I'll pretend it's not happening so I don't have to face my fear and shame"."It's only going to get worse" just means "I don't know how to support you beyond scaring you into wanting to at least fake happiness for my sake".Your parents are being unsupportive, not because you're not suffering, but because this is all they're capable of right now. They are not where you will find the support you need, so keep looking for it in other places. It's out there. If might be an aunt or a friend, or a friend's aunt. It might be a therapist or a bus driver or your family doctor. Keep looking for someone who will hear you. I hope you don't ever give up. :)
LLM:

1. Free or low-cost mental health services: Many organizations and charities offer free or low-cost mental health services. You can contact local mental health clinics, hospitals, or community health centers to inquire about their sliding fee scale for services or assistance with finding resources.

2. Support groups: Joining a support group can help you connect with others who are experiencing similar struggles, feel a sense of camaraderie, and learn coping strategies and new skills from others. You can search online for support groups in your area or look for groups that meet virtually.

It is important to remember that while these resources can be helpful, they should not replace professional help when needed.

Educating loved ones: While it can be hard to feel comfortable discussing your mental health concerns with unsupportive individuals, you may find it helpful to try educating them about depression and its impact. This can help them better understand your experience and increase the chances of receiving the empathy and support you need.

5. Self-help strategies: Implementing self-help strategies, such as regular exercise, healthy eating, staying connected with friends and family, and practicing relaxation techniques like meditation or deep breathing exercises, can also help alleviate some symptoms of depression.

Remember that everyone's experience with depression is unique, and there may be a combination of resources and strategies that work best for you. Prioritizing your mental health and seeking support when you need it is an important step towards feeling better.

It is also important to consider reaching out to your healthcare provider or seeking professional help, as they can provide you with a proper diagnosis, appropriate treatment options, and resources tailored to your specific needs. They may be able to help you navigate

Context: I was violently raped by another women who was my friend of 13 years. I’m having bad flashbacks. I’m scared to sleep because I see it in my dreams. I don't leave the house because I have panic attacks.
Response: I'm sorry for your suffering.There are therapy programs which help people to gradually feel more at ease so that daily living does not feel so frightening.Once you feel stronger and more secure from such a behavioral program, you'll be able to sleep more peacefully and leave the house whenever necessary to do your regular life.Then, you will be able to open and clear the deep emotional hurt that always occurs from being violated by a trusted friend.Sending good wishes in your work!

Psychological therapy, such as Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) or trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy, can help you process the traumatic memories and reduce their impact on your daily life.
